subsections.title: 1.1 Regulatory Framework
paragraphs: The securities industry operates within a comprehensive regulatory framework designed to protect investors and maintain fair, orderly, and efficient markets. The Securities Act of 1933 (the 'Paper Act') regulates the primary market — the initial issuance of securities. It requires that all new securities offered to the public be registered with the SEC through a registration statement that includes a prospectus containing material information about the issuer and the offering. The fundamental principle is full disclosure: the SEC does not approve or disapprove securities, nor does it guarantee the accuracy of the registration statement — it only ensures that required disclosures are made.

The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (the 'People Act') regulates the secondary market — the trading of previously issued securities. It created the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), established requirements for broker-dealer registration, prohibited manipulative and deceptive practices, and authorized the SEC to oversee self-regulatory organizations (SROs). The 1934 Act also governs insider trading, proxy solicitations, tender offers, and periodic reporting requirements for public companies (10-K annual reports, 10-Q quarterly reports, 8-K current reports).

The Investment Company Act of 1940 regulates investment companies (mutual funds, closed-end funds, unit investment trusts). It establishes requirements for registration, disclosure, governance, and operations. The Investment Advisers Act of 1940 regulates investment advisers, requiring registration with the SEC (for advisers with $100M+ AUM) or the state (below $100M). The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform Act of 2010 created the Financial Stability Oversight Council, established the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, and enhanced regulation of derivatives and systemically important financial institutions.

subsections.title: 1.2 Self-Regulatory Organizations
paragraphs: FINRA (Financial Industry Regulatory Authority) is the primary self-regulatory organization for broker-dealers and their registered representatives. FINRA's responsibilities include writing and enforcing rules governing BD activities, examining BDs for compliance, licensing industry professionals through qualification exams (SIE, Series 7, Series 66, etc.), operating the Central Registration Depository (CRD) for registration records, and disciplining firms and individuals who violate rules. FINRA can impose fines, suspensions, bars, and expulsions.

The MSRB (Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board) writes rules for municipal securities dealers and municipal advisors but does not enforce its own rules — enforcement is handled by FINRA (for BD municipal dealers) and the SEC (for municipal advisors). The MSRB operates the EMMA system providing free public access to municipal bond data. The CBOE (Chicago Board Options Exchange) is the SRO for options markets. The Federal Reserve Board sets margin requirements (Regulation T). The OCC (Options Clearing Corporation) guarantees options contracts.

subsections.title: 1.3 Primary Market: Underwriting
paragraphs: The primary market is where new securities are first sold to investors through the underwriting process. The issuer selects a managing underwriter who forms a syndicate to share risk and distribution. A firm commitment underwriting means the syndicate purchases the entire offering from the issuer at a fixed price and assumes the risk of selling to investors. A best efforts underwriting means the underwriter acts as agent, using best efforts to sell but not guaranteeing any specific amount. An all-or-none offering requires the entire issue to be sold or the offering is cancelled.

The underwriting spread is the difference between the public offering price (POP) and the proceeds to the issuer, consisting of the manager's fee, the underwriting fee, and the selling concession (the largest component, typically 50-60% of the spread). During the 20-day cooling-off period after filing the registration statement, the underwriter can distribute preliminary prospectuses (red herrings) and take indications of interest but cannot accept orders or payments. The final prospectus must be delivered to all purchasers.

subsections.title: 1.4 Securities Registration and Exemptions
paragraphs: Exempt securities do not need registration under the 1933 Act: U.S. government securities (Treasuries), municipal securities, bank securities, commercial paper (maturity 270 days or less), and insurance company securities. Exempt transactions include: Regulation D private placements (Rule 506(b): unlimited accredited investors plus up to 35 non-accredited, no general solicitation; Rule 506(c): accredited only, general solicitation allowed with verification), Rule 147 intrastate offerings, Regulation A+ offerings (Tier 1 up to $20M, Tier 2 up to $75M), and Rule 144 resales of restricted and control securities.

EXAM TIP   Exempt Securities vs. Exempt Transactions Exempt securities are always exempt regardless of how they are sold. Exempt transactions are specific types of sales that are exempt — the security itself may not be exempt in other transactions. This distinction is frequently tested.

subsections.title: 1.5 Secondary Market Structure
paragraphs: The secondary market is where previously issued securities trade between investors. The NYSE is an auction market with designated market makers (DMMs) maintaining orderly markets. NASDAQ is a dealer market where multiple market makers compete on bid and ask prices. ECNs allow direct trading between investors. Market orders execute immediately at the best available price. Limit orders specify a maximum purchase price or minimum selling price. Stop orders become market orders when the stop price is reached. The National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) under Regulation NMS requires BDs to honor the best available prices across all markets.

The bid price is the highest price a buyer will pay; the ask price is the lowest price a seller will accept. The spread is the difference between bid and ask — narrow spreads indicate high liquidity, wide spreads indicate low liquidity. Market makers profit from the spread. Settlement for most securities is T+1 (trade date plus one business day). Regular way settlement for U.S. government securities is T+1. Options settle T+1.
Unit 2: Understanding Products and Their Risks

subsections.title: 2.1 Equity Securities
paragraphs: Common stock represents ownership in a corporation with voting rights (typically one vote per share), the right to receive dividends (if declared by the board), preemptive rights (the right to maintain proportional ownership when new shares are issued), and residual claim on assets in liquidation (after all creditors and preferred stockholders are paid). Common stockholders bear the most risk but have unlimited upside potential. Stock splits increase the number of shares while proportionally reducing the price per share; reverse splits decrease shares and increase price.

Preferred stock is a hybrid security with characteristics of both equity and debt. It pays a fixed dividend (stated as a percentage of par value or a dollar amount), has priority over common stock for dividends and liquidation, but typically has no voting rights. Cumulative preferred stock accumulates unpaid dividends (dividends in arrears) that must be paid before any common dividends. Convertible preferred stock can be converted into a specified number of common shares. Callable preferred stock can be redeemed by the issuer at a specified price.

subsections.title: 2.2 Debt Securities
paragraphs: Bonds are debt instruments representing a loan from the investor to the issuer. The issuer promises to pay periodic interest (coupon) and return the principal (par value, typically $1,000) at maturity. Bond prices and interest rates have an inverse relationship: when interest rates rise, bond prices fall, and vice versa. This is because existing bonds with lower coupons become less attractive compared to new bonds issued at higher rates. Duration measures a bond's price sensitivity to interest rate changes — longer duration means greater price sensitivity.

Treasury securities are backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government and are considered the safest fixed-income investments. T-bills (4-52 weeks) are issued at a discount. T-notes (2-10 years) and T-bonds (20-30 years) pay semi-annual coupons. TIPS adjust principal for inflation. Corporate bonds carry credit risk (the risk of default) in addition to interest rate risk. Investment-grade bonds are rated BBB/Baa or higher; high-yield (junk) bonds are rated below BBB/Baa. Municipal bonds provide tax-exempt interest income at the federal level.

Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) are pools of residential mortgages packaged into securities. Pass-through certificates pass principal and interest payments directly to investors. CMOs divide the pool into tranches with different maturities and risk profiles. Prepayment risk is the primary risk: when rates fall, homeowners refinance, returning principal early when reinvestment rates are lower. Extension risk is the opposite: when rates rise, prepayments slow, extending effective maturity.

FORMULA   Bond Price and Interest Rate Relationship A bond with a 5% coupon trading at par ($1,000) when market rates are 5%. If market rates rise to 6%, the bond's price falls below par (discount) because investors can buy new bonds paying 6%. If rates fall to 4%, the bond's price rises above par (premium) because the 5% coupon is more attractive than new 4% bonds. The longer the maturity, the greater the price change for a given rate change.

subsections.title: 2.3 Investment Companies
paragraphs: Open-end funds (mutual funds) continuously issue and redeem shares at NAV. NAV = (Total Assets - Total Liabilities) / Shares Outstanding, calculated daily. Class A shares have front-end loads (up to 5.75%) with lower ongoing expenses. Class B shares have no front-end load but charge a CDSC if redeemed early plus higher 12b-1 fees. Class C shares have level loads with small CDSC for the first year. Breakpoints provide volume discounts on Class A loads — breakpoint selling (selling just below a breakpoint to earn a higher commission) is a violation.

Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) hold baskets of securities but trade on exchanges throughout the day at market prices. ETFs are generally more tax-efficient than mutual funds due to the in-kind creation/redemption process. Closed-end funds issue a fixed number of shares through an IPO and trade at market prices that may be above (premium) or below (discount) NAV. Unit Investment Trusts (UITs) hold a fixed portfolio for a specified period — they are not actively managed.

subsections.title: 2.4 Options Fundamentals
paragraphs: Options give the holder the right, but not the obligation, to buy (call) or sell (put) an underlying security at a specified strike price on or before the expiration date. The buyer pays a premium; the seller receives the premium and assumes the obligation. A call is in-the-money when the stock price exceeds the strike price. A put is in-the-money when the stock price is below the strike price. Intrinsic value is the amount an option is in-the-money (minimum zero). Time value is premium minus intrinsic value.

Long call: bullish, max gain unlimited, max loss is premium paid. Short call (naked): bearish/neutral, max gain is premium received, max loss is unlimited. Long put: bearish, max gain is strike minus premium, max loss is premium paid. Short put (naked): bullish/neutral, max gain is premium received, max loss is strike minus premium. A covered call (own stock + sell call) generates income but caps upside. A protective put (own stock + buy put) provides downside insurance.
Unit 3: Trading, Customer Accounts, and Prohibited Activities

subsections.title: 3.1 Customer Accounts
paragraphs: Cash accounts require full payment by settlement date (T+1). Margin accounts allow borrowing from the BD to purchase securities. Regulation T sets initial margin at 50% for equities. FINRA maintenance margin requires at least 25% equity (most firms require 30-35%). A margin call is issued when equity falls below maintenance. If the customer fails to meet the margin call, the BD can liquidate securities without notice. Pattern day traders (4+ day trades in 5 business days) must maintain $25,000 minimum equity.

Options accounts require a signed Options Account Agreement and approval by a Registered Options Principal. The ODD must be delivered at or before account approval. Discretionary accounts require written power of attorney from the customer. Each discretionary order must be approved by a principal promptly. For non-discretionary accounts, choosing only time and price of execution does not require written authorization. Fiduciary accounts (trust, estate, custodial) require documentation of the fiduciary's authority.

UGMA/UTMA custodial accounts are established for minors with an adult custodian managing the assets until the minor reaches the age of majority (18 for UGMA, 18-25 for UTMA depending on state). Only one custodian and one minor per account. Gifts to the account are irrevocable. The custodian has a fiduciary duty to manage the assets in the minor's best interest. Margin trading and options are generally prohibited in custodial accounts.

subsections.title: 3.2 Customer Protection
paragraphs: SIPC protects customers of failed BDs by returning securities and cash held in customer accounts. Coverage: $500,000 per customer (including up to $250,000 in cash). SIPC does NOT protect against market losses, commodities, futures, or fixed annuities. Separate accounts (individual, joint, IRA) each receive separate coverage. SIPC is NOT a government agency and is NOT insurance — it is funded by BD assessments.

The Customer Protection Rule (SEC Rule 15c3-3) requires BDs to maintain a reserve of cash or qualified securities for the exclusive benefit of customers. The Net Capital Rule (SEC Rule 15c3-1) requires minimum net capital to ensure BDs can meet obligations. Regulation Best Interest (Reg BI) requires BDs to act in the retail customer's best interest at the time of recommendation, with four obligations: Disclosure (Form CRS), Care, Conflict of Interest, and Compliance.

subsections.title: 3.3 Anti-Money Laundering
paragraphs: The Bank Secrecy Act and USA PATRIOT Act require AML programs. Customer Identification Program (CIP): verify identity of all new customers using government ID, SSN/TIN, date of birth, and address. Currency Transaction Reports (CTRs): file with FinCEN for cash transactions exceeding $10,000 in a single business day. Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs): file for suspicious transactions of $5,000+ (structuring, unusual patterns, no business purpose). SARs are confidential — never inform the customer. All BD employees must receive AML training.

EXAM TIP   AML Red Flags Structuring (breaking transactions below $10,000), wire transfers to/from high-risk countries, reluctance to provide ID, accounts with no apparent business purpose, transactions inconsistent with known business. Report to the firm's AML compliance officer immediately.

subsections.title: 3.4 Prohibited Activities
paragraphs: Market manipulation includes: wash trading (buying and selling the same security to create artificial volume), matched orders (coordinated buy and sell orders at predetermined prices), painting the tape (series of transactions to create the appearance of activity), front-running (trading ahead of a large customer order), and spoofing (placing orders with intent to cancel before execution). Churning is excessive trading to generate commissions without regard to the customer's interests.

Insider trading is buying or selling securities based on material, non-public information (MNPI). Material information is any information that a reasonable investor would consider important in making an investment decision. Non-public information has not been disseminated to the general public. Tippers (who share MNPI) and tippees (who trade on it) are both liable. The SEC can impose civil penalties of up to 3x the profit gained or loss avoided. Criminal penalties include up to 20 years imprisonment and $5 million in fines for individuals.
Unit 4: Overview of the Regulatory Framework

subsections.title: 4.1 Registration and Licensing
paragraphs: Broker-dealers must register with the SEC (Form BD) and become members of FINRA. Associated persons (agents/registered representatives) must register with FINRA and pass appropriate qualification exams. The SIE is a co-requisite for all representative-level registrations. The Series 7 (General Securities Representative) allows the sale of all securities products. The Series 6 (Investment Company/Variable Contracts) is limited to mutual funds, variable annuities, and variable life insurance. The Series 63/65/66 address state registration requirements.

Form U4 is the registration form for associated persons, disclosing employment history, disciplinary history, criminal history, financial disclosures (bankruptcies, liens, judgments), and customer complaints. Form U5 is the termination form filed when an associated person leaves a BD. The U5 must be filed within 30 days of termination and must disclose the reason for termination. Both forms are maintained in the CRD system and are publicly available through BrokerCheck.

subsections.title: 4.2 Continuing Education and Conduct Rules
paragraphs: FINRA's continuing education program has two elements: the Regulatory Element (computer-based training within 120 days of the second registration anniversary and every 3 years thereafter — failure results in automatic suspension until completed) and the Firm Element (annual training developed by the firm based on its business and regulatory developments). FINRA conduct rules require fair dealing with customers, suitability of recommendations, disclosure of material information, and supervision of associated persons.

Communications with the public are classified as: correspondence (written to 25 or fewer retail investors within 30 days), retail communication (written to more than 25 retail investors within 30 days), and institutional communication (written exclusively to institutional investors). Retail communications must be approved by a principal before first use. All communications must be fair, balanced, and not misleading. Performance claims must include appropriate disclosures and disclaimers.

subsections.title: 4.3 Customer Complaints and Arbitration
paragraphs: FINRA requires BDs to report customer complaints to FINRA within 30 days. Written customer complaints must be maintained for at least 4 years. FINRA arbitration is the primary dispute resolution mechanism for securities industry disputes. Arbitration is mandatory for disputes between BDs and their associated persons (employment disputes) and between BDs. For customer disputes, arbitration is mandatory if the customer signed a pre-dispute arbitration agreement (which is standard in most account agreements). Arbitration decisions are final and binding with very limited grounds for appeal.

The arbitration process: the claimant files a Statement of Claim with FINRA, the respondent files an Answer, arbitrators are selected (typically 3 for claims over $100,000, 1 for smaller claims), a hearing is conducted, and the arbitrators issue an award. The award must be paid within 30 days. If a BD or associated person fails to pay an arbitration award, FINRA can suspend or cancel their registration. Mediation is a voluntary, non-binding alternative where a neutral mediator helps the parties reach a settlement.

chapters.title: Appendix: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle
chapters.intro:
Economic indicators are statistics that provide insight into the health and direction of the economy. Leading indicators predict future economic activity: building permits, initial jobless claims, stock market performance, consumer confidence, manufacturer new orders, and the yield curve spread. Coincident indicators move with the economy: GDP, industrial production, personal income, and employment levels. Lagging indicators confirm trends after they occur: unemployment rate, CPI, prime rate, and average duration of unemployment.
The business cycle consists of four phases: expansion (rising GDP, employment, and consumer spending), peak (maximum economic output before a downturn), contraction/recession (declining GDP for two consecutive quarters, rising unemployment, falling consumer confidence), and trough (the lowest point before recovery begins). The Federal Reserve uses monetary policy to influence the business cycle: lowering the federal funds rate and buying securities (quantitative easing) during contractions to stimulate growth, and raising rates and selling securities during expansions to prevent overheating and inflation.
The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities. A normal (upward-sloping) yield curve indicates economic expansion expectations — longer maturities yield more to compensate for time risk. A flat yield curve suggests economic uncertainty or transition. An inverted yield curve (short-term rates exceed long-term rates) has historically been a reliable predictor of recession, as it indicates the market expects the Fed to cut rates in the future due to economic weakness.
11. Which of the following is a leading economic indicator? A) Unemployment rate B) Consumer Price Index C) Building permits D) GDP. Answer: C. Building permits are a leading indicator. Unemployment rate and CPI are lagging indicators. GDP is a coincident indicator. Leading indicators predict future economic activity.
12. A customer purchases 100 shares of XYZ at $50 in a margin account. Under Regulation T, the minimum initial deposit is: A) $1,250 B) $2,500 C) $5,000 D) $10,000. Answer: B. Reg T requires 50% initial margin. 50% of $5,000 (100 shares x $50) = $2,500. The broker-dealer loans the remaining $2,500.
13. Which of the following is NOT an exempt security under the Securities Act of 1933? A) U.S. Treasury bonds B) Municipal bonds C) Corporate bonds D) Commercial paper with maturity under 270 days. Answer: C. Corporate bonds must be registered. Treasuries, municipals, and commercial paper (under 270 days) are exempt.
14. An investor holds a long call option with a strike price of $60 and paid a $3 premium. The stock is trading at $65. The option's intrinsic value is: A) $2 B) $3 C) $5 D) $8. Answer: C. Intrinsic value = Stock Price - Strike Price = $65 - $60 = $5. The premium paid ($3) is the total cost but does not affect intrinsic value calculation.
15. The primary purpose of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 is to regulate: A) New securities offerings B) The secondary market C) Investment companies D) Investment advisers. Answer: B. The 1934 Act regulates secondary market trading. The 1933 Act covers new offerings. The 1940 Acts cover investment companies and advisers.
MEMORY AID   SIE Number Mnemonics 33 = Primary (new issues, 1933). 34 = Secondary (trading, 1934). 40 = Companies and Advisers (1940). T = 50% (Reg T margin). 25% = FINRA maintenance. $500K/$250K = SIPC. $10K = CTR. $5K = SAR. 70% = SIE passing score. 85 = SIE questions.
The Securities Investor Protection Corporation (SIPC) protects customers of failed broker-dealers up to $500,000 per customer (including a $250,000 limit for cash claims). SIPC does NOT protect against market losses — it only protects against the loss of securities and cash held by a failed BD. SIPC coverage is per customer, not per account. Joint accounts are treated as separate customers from individual accounts. SIPC is NOT a government agency — it is a nonprofit membership corporation funded by member assessments.
The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) insures bank deposits up to $250,000 per depositor, per insured bank, per ownership category. FDIC covers checking accounts, savings accounts, money market deposit accounts, and CDs. FDIC does NOT cover stocks, bonds, mutual funds, annuities, or life insurance. The key distinction: SIPC covers securities at broker-dealers, FDIC covers deposits at banks. Neither covers investment losses.
Remember: the SIE is a co-requisite for all FINRA representative-level registrations. Passing the SIE demonstrates foundational knowledge of the securities industry and is valid for four years.

subsections.title: Monetary and Fiscal Policy
paragraphs: The Federal Reserve controls monetary policy through three primary tools: the federal funds rate (the rate banks charge each other for overnight loans — the Fed's primary policy tool), open market operations (buying and selling Treasury securities to increase or decrease the money supply), and the reserve requirement (the percentage of deposits banks must hold in reserve, though this was reduced to 0% in 2020). When the Fed buys securities, it injects money into the banking system, lowering interest rates and stimulating borrowing and spending. When it sells securities, it removes money, raising rates and slowing economic activity.

Fiscal policy is controlled by Congress and the President through government spending and taxation. Expansionary fiscal policy (increased spending or tax cuts) stimulates the economy during recessions but increases the budget deficit. Contractionary fiscal policy (reduced spending or tax increases) slows the economy to combat inflation. The relationship between monetary and fiscal policy is critical: if the Fed tightens monetary policy while fiscal policy is expansionary, the effects may partially offset each other.

KEY INSIGHT   Interest Rate Impact on Securities Rising rates: bond prices fall, stock prices may fall (higher borrowing costs reduce corporate earnings), dollar strengthens (attracting foreign investment). Falling rates: bond prices rise, stock prices may rise (lower costs stimulate growth), dollar weakens. Long-duration bonds are most sensitive to rate changes.

subsections.title: Investment Risks
paragraphs: Systematic risk (market risk) affects all securities and cannot be eliminated through diversification: interest rate risk (bond prices fall when rates rise), inflation risk (purchasing power erosion), currency risk (foreign investment value changes), political risk (government policy changes), and market risk (overall market declines). Unsystematic risk (company-specific risk) can be reduced through diversification: business risk (company operational failures), financial risk (excessive leverage), credit/default risk (issuer cannot pay), and liquidity risk (cannot sell quickly at fair price).

Beta measures a security's sensitivity to market movements. A beta of 1.0 means the security moves with the market. Beta > 1.0 indicates higher volatility than the market (aggressive). Beta < 1.0 indicates lower volatility (defensive). Beta of 0 indicates no correlation with the market. Negative beta indicates inverse correlation. Alpha measures the excess return of a security relative to its expected return based on beta — positive alpha indicates outperformance, negative alpha indicates underperformance.
Deep Dive: Retirement Accounts and Tax Treatment

rows: Bond prices fall when rates rise

subsections.title: Retirement Account Types
paragraphs: Traditional IRA contributions may be tax-deductible (depending on income and employer plan participation). Earnings grow tax-deferred. Withdrawals are taxed as ordinary income. RMDs begin at age 73 (75 starting in 2033 under SECURE 2.0). The 2026 contribution limit is $7,500 ($8,600 with catch-up for age 50+). Early withdrawals before age 59 1/2 incur a 10% penalty plus income tax, with exceptions for first-time home purchase ($10,000), qualified education expenses, disability, death, and substantially equal periodic payments (72(t)).

Roth IRA contributions are made with after-tax dollars (not deductible). Earnings grow tax-free. Qualified distributions (after age 59 1/2 and 5-year holding period) are completely tax-free. No RMDs during the owner's lifetime (SECURE 2.0 extended this to Roth 401(k) as well). Income limits apply for contributions: 2026 phase-out begins at $150,000 (single) / $236,000 (MFJ). The backdoor Roth strategy involves contributing to a non-deductible traditional IRA and converting to Roth, effectively bypassing income limits.

401(k) plans are employer-sponsored defined contribution plans. Employee contributions are pre-tax (traditional) or after-tax (Roth). The 2026 employee contribution limit is $24,500 ($32,500 with catch-up for age 50+, $34,750 for ages 60-63 under SECURE 2.0 'super catch-up'). Employer matching contributions are always pre-tax. The total annual limit (employee + employer) is $72,000. Loans are permitted up to the lesser of $50,000 or 50% of the vested balance. Hardship withdrawals are available for immediate and heavy financial need.

subsections.title: Tax Treatment of Investment Income
paragraphs: Ordinary income includes wages, salaries, interest income, short-term capital gains (assets held 1 year or less), and non-qualified dividends. Taxed at the individual's marginal tax rate (10%-37% in 2026). Qualified dividends (from domestic corporations and qualified foreign corporations, held for 60+ days) and long-term capital gains (assets held more than 1 year) are taxed at preferential rates: 0% (income up to $47,025 single), 15% (up to $518,900), or 20% (above $518,900). The 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) applies to investment income when MAGI exceeds $200,000 (single) / $250,000 (MFJ).

Municipal bond interest is exempt from federal income tax and may be exempt from state tax if the bond is issued in the investor's home state. However, capital gains on municipal bonds are taxable. Private activity bond interest may be subject to AMT. Tax-loss harvesting involves selling securities at a loss to offset capital gains, reducing the current year's tax liability. The wash sale rule prohibits claiming a loss if a substantially identical security is purchased within 30 days before or after the sale.

EXAM TIP   Capital Gains Netting Process Step 1: Net short-term gains against short-term losses. Step 2: Net long-term gains against long-term losses. Step 3: If one category has a net gain and the other a net loss, net them together. The character of the final net gain determines the tax rate. Up to $3,000 of net capital losses can be deducted against ordinary income annually; excess carries forward indefinitely.
Deep Dive: Alternative Investments and Variable Products

subsections.title: Variable Annuities and Variable Life Insurance
paragraphs: Variable annuities are insurance products with investment features. During the accumulation phase, the contract owner invests in subaccounts (similar to mutual funds) that may include equity, bond, money market, and balanced options. Returns are not guaranteed and depend on subaccount performance. Variable annuities grow tax-deferred — no taxes are owed until withdrawals begin. Withdrawals are taxed as ordinary income on the earnings portion (LIFO for non-qualified annuities). A 10% penalty applies to withdrawals before age 59 1/2.

Variable annuity fees include: mortality and expense (M&E) risk charges (typically 1.0-1.5% annually), administrative fees, subaccount management fees (similar to mutual fund expense ratios), and optional rider fees (guaranteed minimum income benefit, guaranteed minimum withdrawal benefit, death benefit riders). Surrender charges apply during the surrender period (typically 5-8 years), declining annually. Because variable annuities are both securities and insurance products, they require both a securities license (Series 6 or 7) and an insurance license to sell.

Variable life insurance combines permanent life insurance with investment subaccounts. The cash value fluctuates based on subaccount performance — there is no guaranteed minimum cash value (unlike whole life). The death benefit has a guaranteed minimum but can increase if investments perform well. Variable universal life (VUL) adds premium flexibility to variable life. Both variable life and VUL require a prospectus to be delivered to the purchaser before or at the time of sale, as they are considered securities under the 1933 Act.

subsections.title: Direct Participation Programs (DPPs)
paragraphs: Direct Participation Programs are business ventures structured to pass income, gains, losses, deductions, and credits directly to investors (limited partners) without entity-level taxation. Common DPP types include: oil and gas programs (exploratory/wildcat programs are highest risk, development programs are moderate risk, income programs are lowest risk), real estate programs (raw land, new construction, existing properties, government-assisted housing), and equipment leasing programs. DPPs are illiquid investments with no secondary market.

The general partner manages the partnership and has unlimited liability. Limited partners are passive investors with liability limited to their investment. Limited partners cannot participate in management without risking their limited liability status. Tax benefits include: depletion allowances (oil and gas), depreciation (real estate, equipment), and intangible drilling costs (oil and gas). The passive activity loss rules (IRC Section 469) limit the deductibility of passive losses to passive income — excess passive losses carry forward. The at-risk rules limit deductions to the amount the investor has at risk.

EXAM TIP   DPP Suitability DPPs are suitable for high-income investors seeking tax benefits, portfolio diversification, and income. They are NOT suitable for investors needing liquidity, conservative investors, or those in low tax brackets (tax benefits have less value). Suitability analysis must consider: income level, tax bracket, investment objectives, time horizon, liquidity needs, and overall portfolio composition.

subsections.title: Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs)
paragraphs: REITs allow investors to invest in large-scale real estate without directly owning property. Equity REITs own and operate income-producing properties (apartments, offices, retail, industrial, healthcare). Mortgage REITs invest in mortgages and mortgage-backed securities. Hybrid REITs combine both strategies. Publicly traded REITs trade on exchanges and provide liquidity. Non-traded REITs are illiquid with limited redemption options and higher fees — suitability concerns are significant.

REIT qualification requirements: at least 75% of total assets must be real estate assets, cash, or government securities; at least 75% of gross income must come from real estate sources (rents, mortgage interest, real estate sales); at least 90% of taxable income must be distributed to shareholders as dividends; must have at least 100 shareholders; no more than 50% of shares can be held by 5 or fewer individuals (the 5/50 test). REIT dividends are generally taxed as ordinary income, but the 20% Section 199A QBI deduction effectively reduces the maximum tax rate on REIT dividends to 29.6%.
Deep Dive: Municipal Securities and Packaged Products

subsections.title: Municipal Securities
paragraphs: Municipal securities are issued by state and local governments and their agencies. General obligation (GO) bonds are backed by the full faith, credit, and taxing power of the issuer — they are considered safer because the issuer can raise taxes to pay debt service. Revenue bonds are backed only by the revenue from a specific project or source (toll roads, airports, water systems, hospitals). Revenue bonds carry more risk than GO bonds because the revenue source may be insufficient. Industrial development bonds (IDBs) are issued by municipalities on behalf of private companies — the company is responsible for debt service.

Municipal bond interest is exempt from federal income tax and may be exempt from state and local tax if the investor resides in the issuing state (triple tax-exempt). This tax advantage makes municipal bonds particularly attractive to investors in high tax brackets. The tax-equivalent yield formula converts a municipal yield to its equivalent taxable yield: Tax-Equivalent Yield = Municipal Yield / (1 - Marginal Tax Rate). For example, a 4% municipal bond for an investor in the 37% bracket: 4% / (1 - 0.37) = 6.35% tax-equivalent yield. This means the investor would need a 6.35% taxable bond to match the after-tax return of the 4% municipal bond.

Municipal securities are regulated by the MSRB, which writes rules for municipal dealers and municipal advisors. The MSRB operates EMMA (Electronic Municipal Market Access), providing free public access to municipal bond official statements, trade data, and continuing disclosures. Key MSRB rules: Rule G-17 (fair dealing), Rule G-19 (suitability), Rule G-30 (fair pricing), Rule G-37 (political contributions — pay-to-play restrictions), and Rule G-47 (disclosure of material information). Municipal securities are exempt from SEC registration under the Securities Act of 1933.

EXAM TIP   GO vs. Revenue Bonds GO bonds: backed by taxing power, voter approval usually required, safer. Revenue bonds: backed by project revenue, no voter approval needed, higher risk but potentially higher yield. The feasibility study is critical for revenue bonds — it projects whether the project will generate sufficient revenue to cover debt service. A debt service coverage ratio of 1.25x or higher is generally considered adequate.

subsections.title: Structured Products and Derivatives Basics
paragraphs: Structured products are pre-packaged investment strategies that combine derivatives with traditional investments. Market-linked CDs offer principal protection with returns linked to a market index — the investor cannot lose principal but may earn zero return if the index declines. Principal-protected notes guarantee return of principal at maturity but link the upside to an index or basket of securities. Equity-linked notes (ELNs) provide exposure to equity returns through a debt instrument. These products are complex, often illiquid, and may have high embedded fees that are not transparent.

Exchange-traded notes (ETNs) are unsecured debt obligations of the issuing bank that promise to pay a return linked to a specific index. Unlike ETFs, ETNs carry credit risk of the issuer (as demonstrated by the Lehman Brothers ETN losses in 2008). Leveraged and inverse ETFs use derivatives to amplify returns (2x, 3x) or provide inverse exposure (-1x, -2x, -3x). These products reset daily and are designed for short-term trading — holding them for extended periods can produce results that differ significantly from the expected multiple of the index return due to the compounding effect of daily resets.
Comprehensive Review: Key Formulas and Exam Strategies

subsections.title: Essential SIE Formulas
paragraphs: Current Yield = Annual Coupon Payment / Current Market Price. A bond with a $50 annual coupon trading at $950 has a current yield of 5.26% ($50/$950). Yield to Maturity (YTM) accounts for the total return including coupon payments and the gain or loss at maturity. For a discount bond, YTM > current yield > coupon rate. For a premium bond, YTM < current yield < coupon rate. For a par bond, YTM = current yield = coupon rate. This relationship is critical for the SIE exam.

Dividend Yield = Annual Dividend / Current Stock Price. A stock paying $2.00 annually and trading at $50 has a 4% dividend yield. Price-to-Earnings Ratio (P/E) = Market Price / Earnings Per Share. A stock trading at $60 with EPS of $4 has a P/E of 15. Higher P/E suggests investors expect higher future growth. Earnings Per Share (EPS) = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Common Shares Outstanding. Book Value Per Share = (Total Assets - Total Liabilities - Preferred Stock) / Common Shares Outstanding.

subsections.title: SIE Exam Strategy
paragraphs: The SIE exam consists of 85 questions (75 scored, 10 unscored pilot questions) with a 1 hour 45 minute time limit and a 70% passing score. Content breakdown: Knowledge of Capital Markets (16%), Understanding Products and Their Risks (44%), Understanding Trading, Customer Accounts, and Prohibited Activities (31%), and Overview of the Regulatory Framework (9%). Focus your study time on Products (44%) and Trading/Accounts (31%) — together they comprise 75% of the exam.

Key exam strategies: read each question carefully and identify what is being asked before reviewing the answer choices. Eliminate obviously wrong answers first. Watch for absolute words like 'always,' 'never,' and 'all' — these are often incorrect. Pay attention to qualifiers like 'except,' 'not,' and 'least likely.' For calculation questions, write out the formula before plugging in numbers. For regulatory questions, remember the key numbers: $10,000 CTR threshold, $5,000 SAR threshold, $500,000 SIPC coverage, 50% Reg T margin, 25% FINRA maintenance margin, 2-year contestability period, 31-day grace period.

EXAM TIP   Final Review Checklist Before the exam, review: (1) Securities Act of 1933 vs. 1934 Act, (2) Bond price/yield relationships, (3) Options positions and max gain/loss, (4) Mutual fund share classes, (5) Retirement account types and limits, (6) SIPC vs. FDIC coverage, (7) AML requirements (CTR/SAR), (8) Prohibited activities, (9) Registration requirements, (10) Economic indicators.

rows: Building permits, stock prices, yield curve, consumer confidence
GDP, industrial production, personal income, employment
Unemployment rate, CPI, prime rate, avg unemployment duration

chapters.title: 2026 OBBBA Legislative Update
chapters.intro: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), signed July 4, 2025, made permanent many provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and introduced new provisions effective 2026. All figures below are verified for 2026.

chapters.title: Supplementary Scenarios and Exam Preparation
chapters.intro:
SCENARIO Account Type Selection Client: Recently widowed, 67, inherited $500K IRA from spouse. Options: (1) Spousal rollover to own IRA — delay RMDs until 73, grow tax-deferred. (2) Inherited IRA — must take RMDs based on own life expectancy. (3) Lump sum — entire amount taxable as ordinary income. Best choice: Spousal rollover. Delays RMDs, maintains tax deferral, treats as own IRA with all normal rules.
SCENARIO Margin Call in Action Client buys $20,000 stock on margin. Deposits $10,000 (Reg T 50%). Broker lends $10,000. Stock falls to $12,000: Equity = $12,000 - $10,000 = $2,000 Equity% = $2,000/$12,000 = 16.7% — BELOW 25% maintenance Margin call = (0.25 × $12,000) - $2,000 = $1,000 Client must deposit $1,000 cash or $1,333 in marginable securities. Leverage amplifies BOTH gains AND losses.
COMMON TRAP SIE Common Traps "The SEC approves securities." → WRONG. SEC ensures disclosure but does NOT approve or guarantee. "SIPC protects against market losses." → WRONG. SIPC only covers broker-dealer failure ($500K/$250K cash). "Municipal bond capital gains are tax-exempt." → WRONG. Only the INTEREST is exempt; gains are taxable. "Preferred stockholders have voting rights." → WRONG. Typically no voting rights. "T-Bill interest is state taxable." → WRONG. Treasury interest is state/local EXEMPT.
EXAM TIP Must-Know SIE Formulas Current Yield = Annual Coupon / Market Price Tax-Equivalent Yield = Muni Yield / (1 - Tax Rate) NAV = (Assets - Liabilities) / Shares Outstanding POP = NAV / (1 - Sales Charge %) Discount bond yield order: Coupon < Current Yield < YTM < YTC Premium bond: reverse the order.

practice_questions.prompt: The Securities Act of 1933 primarily regulates:
practice_questions.explanation: The 1933 Act (Paper Act) regulates the primary market — new issues. The 1934 Act regulates the secondary market.

practice_questions.prompt: FINRA can impose all of the following EXCEPT:
practice_questions.explanation: FINRA can impose fines, suspensions, bars, and expulsions. Criminal penalties can only be imposed by courts through criminal prosecution.

practice_questions.prompt: In a firm commitment underwriting, the risk of unsold securities is borne by:
practice_questions.explanation: In firm commitment, the syndicate purchases the entire offering and bears the risk.

practice_questions.prompt: Which security has the highest priority in liquidation?
practice_questions.explanation: Liquidation priority: secured debt > unsecured debt > subordinated debt > preferred stock > common stock.

practice_questions.prompt: A bond trading at a premium has:
options: A coupon rate below market rates
A coupon rate above market rates
A coupon rate equal to market rates
practice_questions.explanation: Premium bonds have coupon rates above current market rates, making them more valuable.

practice_questions.prompt: SIPC coverage per customer is:
practice_questions.explanation: SIPC: $500,000 per customer including up to $250,000 in cash.

practice_questions.prompt: A CTR must be filed for cash transactions exceeding:
practice_questions.explanation: CTRs: cash transactions >$10,000. SARs: suspicious transactions >$5,000.

practice_questions.prompt: The Regulatory Element of continuing education must be completed:
options: Within 120 days of the 2nd anniversary, then every 3 years
practice_questions.explanation: Regulatory Element: 120 days after 2nd registration anniversary, then every 3 years.

practice_questions.prompt: Which mutual fund share class has the highest front-end sales charge?
practice_questions.explanation: Class A: front-end load (up to 5.75%), lower ongoing expenses. Class B: CDSC. Class C: level load. Class I: institutional.

practice_questions.prompt: An investor who buys a call option has:
options: The obligation to buy the stock
The right to buy the stock
The obligation to sell the stock
The right to sell the stock
practice_questions.explanation: Call buyer has the RIGHT to buy. Call seller has the OBLIGATION to sell. Put buyer has the right to sell.

flashcards.definition: The prohibited practice of buying and selling a security without having the funds to cover the purchase. The account will be frozen for 90 days.
A long-term municipal bond with a floating interest rate and a put option that allows the investor to sell the note back to the issuer at par.
A voting system that allows shareholders to pool their votes and allocate them as they see fit, strengthening the ability of minority shareholders to elect a director.
The process by which issuers raise money from investors by selling securities.
The market where investors trade previously issued securities with each other. The issuer does not receive proceeds from these trades.
A self-regulatory organization that writes rules for the municipal securities market but does not have enforcement authority.
The highest tier of the OTC market, requiring companies to meet stringent financial and disclosure standards.
$7,500 per year. Catch-up for age 50+ is $1,100 (2026).
An alternative investment that uses pooled funds and various strategies, including leverage and derivatives, to generate high returns.
Allows firms to place a temporary hold on disbursements from the account of a specified adult if financial exploitation is suspected.
A complex security, typically consisting of a note and a derivative, whose value is based on an underlying asset.
A financial security collateralized by a pool of assets such as loans, leases, or credit card debt.
A non-traded pooled investment, often in real estate or energy, where tax benefits and cash flow pass through to investors.
A tax deduction, similar to depreciation, that applies to natural resources like oil, gas, and timber.
The process of directing money to its most productive use. Markets reward strong issuers with lower borrowing costs and punish weak issuers with higher rates.
The market where securities are sold for the first time. The issuer receives the proceeds. Examples include IPOs and new bond offerings.
A firm or individual that executes trades on behalf of customers, does not own the securities, and earns commissions.
A firm that trades securities for its own account, owns inventory, and earns profits through markups or markdowns.
A dealer that continuously quotes bid and ask prices for a security, providing liquidity to the market. Market makers always act as principals.
A person or firm that provides investment advice for compensation. Held to a fiduciary standard. Focuses on advice, not trade execution.
The total market value of all goods and services produced in a country during a specific period. The broadest measure of economic activity.
A measure of inflation that tracks the price changes of a basket of consumer goods and services over time.
The interest rate at which banks lend reserves to each other overnight. The Federal Reserve's primary tool for monetary policy.
Economic policy controlled by Congress and the President through government spending and taxation. Not controlled by the Federal Reserve.
A graph plotting bond yields against maturities. A normal curve slopes upward; an inverted curve (short rates higher than long rates) historically signals a potential recession.
The underwriter purchases the entire issue from the issuer and assumes the risk of reselling to investors. The underwriter bears the risk of unsold shares.
The underwriter acts as agent, selling as much of the issue as possible but returning unsold shares to the issuer. The issuer bears the risk.
A preliminary prospectus distributed during the cooling-off period. It contains most final prospectus information but not the final offering price.
The SEC ensures adequate disclosure through registration but does NOT approve or endorse any security. Saying a security is "SEC approved" is a prohibited practice.
Represents ownership in a corporation. Holders have voting rights and may receive dividends, but dividends are not guaranteed. Last in line in liquidation.
A class of stock with priority over common stock for dividends and liquidation. Usually pays a fixed dividend. Junior to all bonds and debt in liquidation.

Debt security issued by a state or local government. Interest is generally exempt from federal income tax. General obligation bonds are backed by taxing power; revenue bonds are backed by project revenue.

Debt securities issued by the U.S. government. Considered to have minimal credit risk. Include T-bills (up to 1 year), T-notes (2-10 years), and T-bonds (20-30 years). Subject to federal tax, exempt from state and local tax.
Short-term unsecured corporate debt with maturities up to 270 days. Exempt from SEC registration due to short maturity. Used by corporations for working capital.
A short-term collateralized borrowing arrangement where one party sells securities and agrees to repurchase them at a higher price. Used by dealers and institutions for short-term financing.
A pooled investment that issues redeemable shares. Priced once daily at NAV. Forward-priced (orders filled at next calculated NAV). Must deliver a prospectus.
A pooled investment that trades on an exchange throughout the day at market prices. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs can be bought and sold intraday and typically have lower expense ratios.

A call option gives the holder the RIGHT to BUY at the strike price. A put option gives the holder the RIGHT to SELL at the strike price. Buyers pay premiums; sellers collect premiums and have obligations.

Suitability (FINRA Rule 2111) requires recommendations be suitable for the customer. Reg BI (best interest) requires broker-dealers to act in the customer's best interest, mitigate conflicts, and provide disclosure via Form CRS.
Trading on material, non-public information. Prohibited under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Both the tipper and tippee can be held liable.
Financial institutions must have AML programs including a compliance officer, training, independent testing, and customer identification (CIP). Suspicious activity reported via SARs to FinCEN.
Protects customers up to $500,000 per account ($250,000 cash limit) if a broker-dealer fails. Does NOT protect against market losses, fraud, or bad investment decisions.
$24,500 employee deferral. Catch-up (age 50-59, 64+): $8,000 additional. Super catch-up (age 60-63): $11,250 additional.
$15 million per person ($30 million married couple with portability). Made permanent by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, 2025.

tracks.name: Series 7 — General Securities Representative
tracks.title: Series 7 Examination
tracks.subtitle: General Securities Representative Study Manual Comprehensive Study Guide Updated March 2026  |  OBBBA Integrated

chapters.title: Table of Contents
chapters.intro: Right-click → 'Update Field' in Word for clickable page numbers.

chapters.title: How to Use This Manual
chapters.intro:
The Series 7 (General Securities Representative Qualification Examination) is the premier securities license in the United States, authorizing the holder to sell virtually all types of securities products including stocks, bonds, options, mutual funds, variable annuities, direct participation programs, and government securities. The Series 7 is significantly more demanding than the SIE: while the SIE tests foundational knowledge (what products are and how the industry works), the Series 7 tests deep application (how to analyze securities, calculate returns, construct suitable portfolios, and apply complex options strategies). The Series 7 requires FINRA member firm sponsorship and the SIE as a co-requisite.
This manual covers all major content areas tested on the Series 7 in twelve comprehensive chapters. The exam places heaviest emphasis on options (approximately 25% of questions), making Chapter 3 the single most important chapter for exam preparation. Customer accounts, suitability, and regulatory requirements collectively account for another 25-30%. The remaining questions cover equity securities, debt securities, packaged products, margin, retirement plans, taxation, municipal securities, and economic factors. Success on the Series 7 requires both conceptual understanding and calculation proficiency — you must be able to compute option breakevens, bond yields, margin calls, and tax-equivalent yields under time pressure.
Study strategy recommendation: Begin with options (Chapter 3) since it is the highest-weighted topic and requires the most practice. Then move to debt securities (Chapter 2) and margin (Chapter 6), which are calculation-intensive. Cover customer accounts and suitability (Chapters 5 and 12) thoroughly, as these are tested both directly and through scenario-based questions throughout the exam. Use the practice exam at the end to identify weak areas, then return to those chapters for targeted review.

chapters.title: Exam Overview
chapters.intro:
KEY INSIGHT   Options Dominate the Exam Options questions make up approximately 25% of the Series 7 exam (about 31-32 questions). You must be able to calculate maximum gain, maximum loss, and breakeven for all basic strategies: long/short calls and puts, covered calls, protective puts, spreads, straddles, and combinations. This is the single highest-yield study area — mastering options can be the difference between passing and failing.
Chapter 1: Equity Securities

subsections.title: 1.1 Common Stock
paragraphs: Common stock represents ownership (equity) in a corporation. Each share of common stock typically carries one vote per share, exercised at the annual shareholders' meeting to elect the board of directors and vote on major corporate actions such as mergers, stock splits, and changes to the corporate charter. Shareholders have the right to receive dividends if declared by the board of directors (dividends are not guaranteed and are paid at the board's discretion), the right to inspect corporate books and records, the right to transfer shares, and a residual claim on assets in liquidation (after all creditors, bondholders, and preferred stockholders are paid).

Preemptive rights give existing shareholders the right to maintain their proportional ownership when the company issues new shares. If a company with 1 million shares outstanding issues 100,000 new shares, a shareholder owning 10% (100,000 shares) has the right to purchase 10,000 of the new shares before they are offered to the public. Preemptive rights may be waived in the corporate charter. The liquidation priority (from first paid to last paid) is: secured creditors, unsecured creditors, subordinated debtholders, preferred stockholders, and finally common stockholders. This priority is frequently tested on the Series 7.

Stock splits increase the number of shares outstanding while proportionally reducing the price per share, leaving the total market capitalization unchanged. A 2-for-1 split doubles shares and halves the price: an investor holding 100 shares at $80 would hold 200 shares at $40 after the split. A reverse split reduces shares and increases price (e.g., a 1-for-10 reverse split converts 1,000 shares at $1 into 100 shares at $10). Reverse splits are often used by companies whose stock price has fallen below exchange listing requirements. Stock dividends are similar to splits but expressed as a percentage: a 10% stock dividend gives 1 additional share for every 10 held. Neither splits nor stock dividends change the total value of the investor's position or create a taxable event.

subsections.title: 1.2 Preferred Stock
paragraphs: Preferred stock is a hybrid security with characteristics of both equity and debt. It pays a fixed dividend (stated as a percentage of par value or a dollar amount) with priority over common stock dividends, but typically carries no voting rights. Preferred stock is senior to common stock in liquidation but junior to all debt obligations. Because of its fixed dividend, preferred stock price is sensitive to interest rate changes — when rates rise, preferred stock prices fall, similar to bonds.

Cumulative preferred stock requires that all missed (accumulated) dividends must be paid before any common stock dividends can be declared. This feature protects preferred shareholders from the company skipping dividends. Participating preferred stock may receive additional dividends beyond the stated rate if the company performs exceptionally well. Convertible preferred stock can be converted into a specified number of common shares at the holder's option, providing upside potential if the common stock price rises significantly. Callable preferred stock can be redeemed by the issuer at a specified call price after a specified date, which limits the investor's upside potential.

FORMULA   Preferred Stock Current Yield Current Yield = Annual Dividend / Market Price. A $100 par preferred stock with a 6% dividend rate ($6 annual dividend) trading at $90 has a current yield of $6 / $90 = 6.67%. When preferred stock trades below par, the current yield exceeds the stated dividend rate. When it trades above par, the current yield is below the stated rate.

subsections.title: 1.3 ADRs, Rights, and Warrants
paragraphs: American Depositary Receipts (ADRs) allow U.S. investors to own shares of foreign companies, traded in U.S. dollars on U.S. exchanges. Each ADR represents a specified number of foreign shares held by a U.S. depositary bank. ADRs carry all the risks of the underlying foreign stock (business risk, market risk, political risk) plus currency risk — the risk that the foreign currency will depreciate relative to the U.S. dollar, reducing the dollar value of dividends and the ADR price. Sponsored ADRs are created with the cooperation of the foreign company and are listed on major exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ). Unsponsored ADRs are created by depositories without the foreign company's involvement and trade over-the-counter.

Rights are short-term instruments (typically 30-60 days) issued to existing shareholders, allowing them to buy new shares below the current market price. Rights have intrinsic value equal to the difference between the market price and the subscription price, divided by the number of rights needed to buy one share plus one (cum-rights) or without the plus one (ex-rights). Warrants are long-term instruments (often 2-5 years or perpetual) that give the holder the right to purchase shares at a specified exercise price. Warrants are often attached to bond issues as sweeteners to lower the required coupon rate. Warrants are more speculative than rights because the exercise price is typically above the current market price at issuance, and the longer time frame introduces more uncertainty.
Chapter 2: Debt Securities

subsections.title: 2.1 Bond Fundamentals and Pricing
paragraphs: Bonds represent a loan from the investor (bondholder) to the issuer. The issuer pays periodic interest (the coupon, typically semi-annually for corporate and government bonds) and returns the principal (par value, typically $1,000) at maturity. Bond prices and interest rates have an inverse relationship: when market interest rates rise, existing bond prices fall because their fixed coupon payments become less attractive compared to newly issued bonds offering higher coupons. Conversely, when rates fall, existing bond prices rise. This inverse relationship is the single most important concept in fixed-income investing.

Bond prices are quoted as a percentage of par value. A bond quoted at 98 trades at $980 (98% of $1,000 par). A bond trading below par is trading at a discount; a bond trading above par is trading at a premium. A bond trading at exactly par is trading at par. The relationship between price and yield creates three important yield measures. Current yield equals the annual coupon divided by the market price. Yield to maturity (YTM) accounts for both coupon payments and the gain or loss from the difference between purchase price and par at maturity. Yield to call (YTC) calculates the return assuming the bond is called at the earliest call date.

For a discount bond, the yield hierarchy from lowest to highest is: coupon rate, current yield, YTM, YTC (if callable). For a premium bond, the hierarchy reverses: YTC (lowest), YTM, current yield, coupon rate (highest). This hierarchy is one of the most frequently tested concepts on the Series 7. Understanding it requires recognizing that a discount bond provides a capital gain at maturity (boosting YTM above current yield), while a premium bond suffers a capital loss at maturity (reducing YTM below current yield).

subsections.title: 2.2 Bond Risks
paragraphs: Interest rate risk is the primary risk for bondholders. Longer-maturity bonds and lower-coupon bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes because a larger proportion of their total return comes from distant cash flows, which are more heavily discounted when rates rise. Duration quantifies this sensitivity: a bond with a duration of 8 will experience approximately an 8% price decline for each 1% increase in interest rates. Zero-coupon bonds have the highest duration (equal to their maturity) because all cash flow occurs at maturity. Conversely, high-coupon, short-maturity bonds have the lowest duration and least interest rate risk.

Credit risk (default risk) is the risk that the issuer will fail to make scheduled interest or principal payments. Credit ratings from Moody's (Aaa to C), Standard and Poor's (AAA to D), and Fitch assess the probability of default. Investment grade is BBB/Baa or higher. Below investment grade (BB/Ba or lower) is classified as high-yield or speculative grade (commonly called junk bonds). These bonds offer higher yields to compensate for higher default risk. U.S. Treasury securities are considered free of credit risk because they are backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government.

Call risk exists for callable bonds: when interest rates fall significantly, the issuer may exercise its right to redeem the bonds early (typically at a slight premium to par), forcing investors to reinvest the returned principal at lower prevailing rates. This is why callable bonds trade at lower prices (higher yields) than comparable non-callable bonds — the call feature benefits the issuer at the expense of the bondholder. Reinvestment risk is the risk that coupon payments and returned principal will be reinvested at rates lower than the original yield. Zero-coupon bonds eliminate reinvestment risk entirely because there are no periodic cash flows to reinvest. Inflation risk (purchasing power risk) erodes the real value of fixed coupon payments over time. TIPS protect against inflation by adjusting the principal value based on changes in the Consumer Price Index.

FORMULA   Duration Price Change Approximate Price Change = -Duration x Change in Yield. Example: A bond with duration 7.5 experiences a 0.75% rate increase. Price change = -7.5 x 0.75% = -5.625%. For a $1,000 bond, the price drops approximately $56.25 to $943.75. This is a linear approximation; convexity provides a correction for large rate changes.
Chapter 3: Options

subsections.title: 3.1 Options Fundamentals
paragraphs: A call option gives the buyer (holder) the right, but not the obligation, to buy 100 shares of the underlying stock at the strike (exercise) price before the expiration date. A put option gives the buyer the right to sell 100 shares at the strike price. Option buyers pay a premium to acquire these rights and have limited risk (the maximum they can lose is the premium paid). Option sellers (writers) receive the premium and assume obligations: a call writer must sell shares at the strike price if assigned, and a put writer must buy shares at the strike price if assigned. Option sellers have potentially unlimited risk (uncovered call writers) or substantial risk (uncovered put writers, limited to the strike price minus the premium received).

Options are derivative securities because their value derives from the underlying asset. An option is in the money (ITM) when exercising it would be profitable: a call is ITM when the market price exceeds the strike price; a put is ITM when the market price is below the strike price. An option is out of the money (OTM) when exercising would result in a loss: a call is OTM when the market price is below the strike; a put is OTM when the market price is above the strike. An option is at the money (ATM) when the market price equals the strike price. The premium consists of intrinsic value (the ITM amount, if any) plus time value (the portion of the premium above intrinsic value, reflecting the probability of favorable price movement before expiration).

Options settle on the next business day (T+1). American-style options can be exercised at any time before expiration. European-style options can only be exercised at expiration. Most equity options are American-style. Index options are typically European-style and settle in cash rather than shares. The Options Clearing Corporation (OCC) guarantees all listed options contracts, eliminating counterparty risk for exchange-traded options.

subsections.title: 3.2 Options Strategies
paragraphs: A covered call involves owning the underlying stock and selling (writing) a call option against it. This is the most conservative options strategy and is used to generate income from a stock position. The premium received provides a limited cushion against price declines. However, the upside is capped: if the stock rises above the strike price, the stock will be called away. Maximum gain = (Strike Price - Stock Purchase Price) + Premium Received. Maximum loss = Stock Purchase Price - Premium Received (if the stock goes to zero). Breakeven = Stock Purchase Price - Premium Received. Covered calls are suitable for investors who are neutral to moderately bullish on the stock and want to generate income.

A protective put involves owning the stock and buying a put option, creating a floor (minimum selling price) for the stock position. This is essentially portfolio insurance. Maximum loss is limited to (Stock Purchase Price - Put Strike Price) + Premium Paid. Maximum gain is unlimited (the stock can rise without limit, and the put simply expires worthless). Breakeven = Stock Purchase Price + Premium Paid. Protective puts are suitable for investors who are bullish long-term but want to protect against short-term downside risk.

A straddle involves buying (or selling) both a call and a put with the same strike price and expiration date. A long straddle profits from large price movements in either direction — the buyer expects high volatility but is uncertain about the direction. Maximum loss = total premiums paid (occurs if the stock is exactly at the strike price at expiration). Maximum gain is unlimited on the upside and substantial on the downside (strike price minus total premiums). A short straddle profits from price stability — the seller expects the stock to remain near the strike price. Maximum gain = total premiums received. Maximum loss is unlimited.

A spread involves buying and selling options of the same type (both calls or both puts) with different strike prices (vertical spread) or different expiration dates (horizontal/calendar spread). A bull call spread (buy a lower-strike call, sell a higher-strike call) profits from a moderate price increase. Maximum gain = difference in strikes - net premium paid. Maximum loss = net premium paid. A bear put spread (buy a higher-strike put, sell a lower-strike put) profits from a moderate price decline. Maximum gain = difference in strikes - net premium paid. Maximum loss = net premium paid.

FORMULA   Covered Call Calculation An investor buys 100 shares of XYZ at $50 and sells 1 XYZ 55 call for $3. Maximum gain: ($55 - $50) + $3 = $8/share = $800. Breakeven: $50 - $3 = $47. Maximum loss: $47/share = $4,700 (if stock goes to zero). If the stock is at $60 at expiration, the stock is called away at $55, and the investor's total profit is $800 (not $1,000, because the call caps the upside at $55).

EXAM TIP   Options Calculation Shortcut For any options position, remember: Buyers pay premiums (debit), sellers receive premiums (credit). To find breakeven: for calls, add the net premium to the lower strike; for puts, subtract the net premium from the higher strike. For maximum gain/loss: draw a T-chart with money in on one side and money out on the other. The difference is your max gain or max loss depending on the best/worst case scenario.

subsections.title: 3.3 Options Taxation
paragraphs: When an option expires unexercised, the buyer has a capital loss equal to the premium paid, and the seller has a short-term capital gain equal to the premium received (regardless of how long the option was held). When a call option is exercised, the buyer's cost basis in the acquired stock is the strike price plus the premium paid. The seller's sale proceeds are the strike price plus the premium received. When a put option is exercised, the buyer's sale proceeds are the strike price minus the premium paid. The seller's cost basis in the acquired stock is the strike price minus the premium received. The holding period for the stock begins on the day after exercise, not the day the option was purchased.

Closing transactions (selling an option you bought, or buying back an option you sold) result in a capital gain or loss equal to the difference between the opening and closing premiums. The holding period of the option itself determines whether the gain or loss is short-term or long-term. Listed equity options have a maximum term of 9 months, so gains and losses from closing transactions on standard options are always short-term. LEAPS (Long-Term Equity Anticipation Securities) have terms up to 3 years and can produce long-term capital gains if held more than 12 months.
Chapter 4: Packaged Products and Alternatives

subsections.title: 4.1 Mutual Funds
paragraphs: Open-end mutual funds continuously issue and redeem shares at net asset value (NAV), calculated once daily after market close (4:00 PM ET). NAV = (Total Fund Assets - Total Fund Liabilities) / Total Shares Outstanding. Investors purchase shares at the public offering price (POP), which equals NAV plus any applicable sales charge: POP = NAV / (1 - Sales Charge %). For example, a fund with NAV of $20 and a 5% front-end load has a POP of $20 / (1 - 0.05) = $21.05. Investors redeem shares at NAV (minus any applicable back-end load).

Share classes determine how the investor pays for the fund. Class A shares charge a front-end sales load (typically 3-5.75%) with lower ongoing expenses. Breakpoints reduce the sales charge for larger investments: a fund might charge 5.75% for investments under $25,000, 5.00% for $25,000-$49,999, 4.50% for $50,000-$99,999, and so on. A letter of intent (LOI) allows the investor to receive the breakpoint discount by committing to invest a specified amount over 13 months. Rights of accumulation (ROA) allow the investor to count existing holdings toward the next breakpoint. Class B shares charge a back-end load (contingent deferred sales charge, or CDSC) that declines over time (typically 5% in year 1, declining to 0% after 6-8 years) with higher ongoing 12b-1 fees. Class C shares charge a level load (typically 1% CDSC for the first year only) with the highest ongoing 12b-1 fees.

The expense ratio is the total annual cost of owning the fund, expressed as a percentage of assets. It includes the management fee, 12b-1 fee, and other operating expenses. The 12b-1 fee covers marketing and distribution costs and is capped at 1% annually (0.75% for distribution, 0.25% for service). A fund charging more than 0.25% in 12b-1 fees cannot call itself a no-load fund. Mutual fund distributions include ordinary dividends (from interest and short-term gains, taxed as ordinary income), qualified dividends (from qualifying stock dividends, taxed at capital gains rates), and capital gains distributions (from the fund's sale of securities, taxed as long-term or short-term depending on the fund's holding period).

EXAM TRAP   Breakpoint Sales Selling shares just below a breakpoint to earn a higher commission is a violation called a breakpoint sale. For example, if a client wants to invest $24,500 and the breakpoint is $25,000, the representative must inform the client that investing an additional $500 would significantly reduce the sales charge. Failing to disclose breakpoints is a violation of FINRA rules.

subsections.title: 4.2 ETFs, Closed-End Funds, and UITs
paragraphs: Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) trade intraday on exchanges like stocks, with prices determined by supply and demand. Most ETFs passively track an index and have lower expense ratios than actively managed mutual funds. ETFs are more tax-efficient than mutual funds because of the in-kind creation/redemption mechanism, which minimizes taxable capital gains distributions. Investors can buy ETFs on margin, sell them short, and place limit orders — none of which are possible with open-end mutual funds. However, investors pay brokerage commissions on ETF trades and may face bid-ask spreads.

Closed-end funds issue a fixed number of shares through an IPO and then trade on exchanges at market prices that may differ from NAV. A closed-end fund trading above NAV is at a premium; below NAV is at a discount. Unlike open-end funds, closed-end funds do not redeem shares — investors must sell on the secondary market. Closed-end funds can use leverage (borrowing to invest), which amplifies both gains and losses. Unit Investment Trusts (UITs) hold a fixed, unmanaged portfolio of securities for a specified period (typically 15-30 years for bond UITs, 1-5 years for equity UITs). UITs do not have portfolio managers and do not trade securities within the trust.

subsections.title: 4.3 Variable Annuities and DPPs
paragraphs: Variable annuities are insurance products with investment subaccounts that grow tax-deferred. During the accumulation phase, the contract owner selects subaccounts (similar to mutual funds) and the value fluctuates based on investment performance. Withdrawals during accumulation are taxed LIFO (last-in, first-out): earnings come out first and are taxed as ordinary income, with a 10% penalty before age 59 1/2. During the annuitization phase, each payment is split between taxable earnings and non-taxable return of basis using the exclusion ratio. Variable annuities are securities and require both a securities license and an insurance license to sell. Surrender charges typically apply for 6-8 years.

Direct Participation Programs (DPPs) are limited partnerships that pass through income, losses, deductions, and credits directly to investors without corporate-level taxation. Common DPPs include oil and gas programs (exploratory programs carry the highest risk and largest deductions; income programs carry the lowest risk), real estate programs (raw land is most speculative; existing properties generate immediate income), and equipment leasing programs. DPPs are highly illiquid (no secondary market), carry significant risk, and are suitable only for high-income investors who can benefit from the tax deductions and can afford to lose their entire investment. The general partner manages the partnership and has unlimited liability; limited partners have liability limited to their investment.
Chapter 5: Customer Accounts

subsections.title: 5.1 Account Types and Documentation
paragraphs: Opening a customer account requires collecting comprehensive information through the new account form: legal name, address, date of birth, Social Security number (or Tax Identification Number), citizenship status, employment status and employer name, annual income, liquid net worth, total net worth, investment objectives (capital preservation, income, growth, speculation), risk tolerance, time horizon, tax status (tax bracket, tax-advantaged needs), and prior investment experience. The customer must sign the new account form. The registered representative signs the form, and a principal (supervisor) must approve the account before any transactions occur.

Joint accounts can be structured as Joint Tenants with Right of Survivorship (JTWROS), where the entire account passes to the surviving owner(s) at death bypassing probate, or as Tenants in Common (TIC), where each owner's share passes to their estate at death. In community property states, assets acquired during marriage are owned equally by both spouses. Custodial accounts under UGMA (Uniform Gifts to Minors Act) or UTMA (Uniform Transfers to Minors Act) are owned by the minor with an adult custodian managing the account in a fiduciary capacity. Gifts to custodial accounts are irrevocable. At the age of majority (18 or 21 depending on the state and whether UGMA or UTMA), the account transfers to the former minor with no restrictions on use.

Trust accounts are managed by a trustee according to the terms of the trust document. The trustee has a fiduciary duty to manage the account in accordance with the trust's objectives and the beneficiaries' interests. Corporate and partnership accounts require documentation including the corporate resolution (authorizing specific individuals to trade), articles of incorporation or partnership agreement, and tax identification number. Fiduciary accounts (estates, guardianships, conservatorships) are managed by a court-appointed fiduciary who must act in the best interest of the beneficiary and comply with the Prudent Investor Rule.
Chapter 6: Margin Accounts

subsections.title: 6.1 Margin Fundamentals and Regulation T
paragraphs: A margin account allows the customer to borrow money from the broker-dealer to purchase securities, using the securities as collateral.
