chapters.title: How to Use This Manual
chapters.intro:
The Life and Health Insurance examination qualifies individuals to sell life insurance, health insurance, annuities, and related products. This is a state-administered exam with content varying by state, though core concepts are consistent nationwide. The exam tests knowledge of insurance principles, product features, policy provisions, taxation, and state regulatory requirements. Unlike securities exams (SIE, Series 7), the Life and Health exam focuses exclusively on insurance products and the insurance regulatory framework.
This manual covers seven chapters spanning the complete Life and Health curriculum. Chapters 1-4 cover life insurance and annuities. Chapters 5-6 cover health insurance. Chapter 7 covers regulation and ethics. The exam typically includes approximately 150 questions with a time limit of 2-3 hours (varies by state). Focus your study on understanding the differences between product types, the tax treatment of premiums and benefits, and the key policy provisions that protect policyholders.

chapters.title: Exam Overview
chapters.intro: Chapter 1: Life Insurance Fundamentals

subsections.title: 1.1 Principles of Insurance
paragraphs: Insurance is a risk transfer mechanism where the insured pays a premium to the insurer in exchange for a promise to pay benefits upon the occurrence of a covered loss. The law of large numbers allows insurers to predict losses with reasonable accuracy by pooling a large number of similar risks. Adverse selection is the tendency of higher-risk individuals to seek insurance more actively than lower-risk individuals. Insurers combat adverse selection through underwriting — the process of evaluating and classifying risk to determine whether to issue a policy and at what premium.

Insurable interest requires that the policyholder would suffer a financial loss from the insured event. For life insurance, insurable interest must exist at the time of application (not at the time of claim). A person always has insurable interest in their own life. Insurable interest also exists between spouses, parents and children, business partners, and employers and key employees. The principle of indemnity (applicable to property and health insurance, not life insurance) limits recovery to the actual financial loss suffered, preventing the insured from profiting from a loss.

subsections.title: 1.2 Needs Analysis and Underwriting
paragraphs: The human life value approach estimates the present value of the insured's future earnings, adjusted for taxes, personal consumption, and inflation. The needs approach (more commonly used) calculates specific financial obligations: final expenses ($10,000-$25,000), outstanding debts (mortgage, loans), income replacement (present value of needed income for dependents), education funding, emergency fund, and special needs. Existing resources (savings, investments, Social Security survivor benefits, existing insurance) are subtracted to determine the insurance gap.

Underwriting classifies applicants into risk categories: preferred (lowest risk, lowest premiums), standard (average risk, standard premiums), substandard/rated (higher risk, higher premiums), and declined (uninsurable). Factors considered include age, gender, health history, family medical history, occupation, hobbies, tobacco use, driving record, and financial situation. The Medical Information Bureau (MIB) maintains a database of medical conditions reported by member insurance companies to detect fraud and adverse selection.
Chapter 2: Life Insurance Products

subsections.title: 2.1 Term Life Insurance
paragraphs: Term life insurance provides pure death protection for a specified period (10, 15, 20, or 30 years) with no cash value accumulation. It is the most affordable type of life insurance and is appropriate for temporary needs such as income replacement during working years, mortgage protection, and coverage during child-rearing years. Level term maintains a constant death benefit and premium throughout the term. Decreasing term maintains a constant premium but the death benefit decreases over time (used for mortgage protection). Annual renewable term (ART) renews annually with increasing premiums based on the insured's attained age.

Convertible term policies allow the policyholder to convert to a permanent policy without evidence of insurability, typically within a specified conversion period. This is valuable because the insured's health may deteriorate during the term, making it impossible to qualify for a new policy. The conversion is based on the insured's attained age (current age) at the time of conversion, resulting in higher premiums than if the permanent policy had been purchased originally.

subsections.title: 2.2 Whole Life Insurance
paragraphs: Whole life (ordinary life) insurance provides permanent coverage for the insured's entire lifetime with guaranteed level premiums, a guaranteed death benefit, and guaranteed cash value growth. The cash value grows at a guaranteed minimum rate and can be accessed through policy loans or partial surrenders. Policy loans accrue interest but do not need to be repaid (outstanding loans reduce the death benefit). Whole life premiums are significantly higher than term premiums because a portion of each premium funds the cash value. Whole life is appropriate for permanent needs: estate liquidity, final expenses, wealth transfer, and supplemental retirement income.

Limited-pay whole life (such as 20-pay life or paid-up at 65) compresses the premium payments into a shorter period while providing lifetime coverage. Single-premium whole life is paid with one lump sum and is always classified as a Modified Endowment Contract (MEC). Participating whole life policies pay dividends (which are considered a return of premium and are not taxable until they exceed the total premiums paid). Dividends can be taken in cash, used to reduce premiums, left to accumulate at interest, or used to purchase paid-up additions (additional insurance).

subsections.title: 2.3 Universal and Variable Life
paragraphs: Universal life (UL) offers flexible premiums and an adjustable death benefit. The policyholder can increase or decrease premiums (within limits) and adjust the death benefit. Cash value earns interest based on current rates (with a guaranteed minimum). UL has two death benefit options: Option A (level death benefit, where the cash value is included in the death benefit) and Option B (increasing death benefit, where the death benefit equals the face amount plus the cash value). Option B is more expensive but provides increasing coverage.

Variable life (VL) allows the policyholder to invest the cash value in subaccounts (similar to mutual funds), offering higher growth potential but with investment risk. The death benefit has a guaranteed minimum but can increase if investments perform well. Variable universal life (VUL) combines the flexibility of UL with the investment options of VL. Both VL and VUL are securities and require a securities license (Series 6 or Series 7) in addition to a life insurance license. Indexed universal life (IUL) credits interest based on a market index (such as the S&P 500) with a floor (minimum rate, often 0-2%) and a cap (maximum rate, often 8-12%).
Chapter 3: Life Insurance Policy Provisions

Standard policy provisions protect both the insurer and the policyholder. The free-look period (typically 10-30 days) allows the policyholder to return the policy for a full refund after delivery. The grace period (typically 31 days) allows premium payment after the due date without lapse. The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from contesting the policy after it has been in force for 2 years (except for non-payment of premiums). The suicide clause excludes death by suicide within the first 2 years of the policy (after 2 years, suicide is covered).

The misstatement of age provision allows the insurer to adjust the death benefit (rather than void the policy) if the insured's age was misstated on the application. The entire contract provision states that the policy and the application (attached to the policy) constitute the entire contract. The reinstatement provision allows a lapsed policy to be reinstated within a specified period (typically 3-5 years) with evidence of insurability and payment of back premiums with interest. Non-forfeiture options (for permanent policies) protect the policyholder's cash value if the policy lapses: cash surrender value, reduced paid-up insurance, or extended term insurance.

Settlement options determine how the death benefit is paid to the beneficiary. Lump sum provides the entire benefit at once. Fixed period pays equal installments over a specified number of years. Fixed amount pays a specified dollar amount until the proceeds are exhausted. Life income (annuitization) pays for the beneficiary's lifetime. Interest only leaves the proceeds with the insurer, paying only the interest to the beneficiary. The beneficiary designation can be revocable (the policyholder can change it) or irrevocable (requires the beneficiary's consent to change).
Chapter 4: Annuities

An annuity is a contract between an individual and an insurance company designed to provide income, typically during retirement. During the accumulation phase, the contract owner makes premium payments and the value grows tax-deferred. During the annuitization (payout) phase, the insurance company makes periodic payments to the annuitant. Fixed annuities guarantee a minimum interest rate and provide predictable payments. Variable annuities invest in subaccounts with returns dependent on market performance — they are securities requiring a securities license.

Annuity payout options include: life only (payments for the annuitant's lifetime, highest payment but nothing to beneficiaries at death), life with period certain (payments for life with a guaranteed minimum period, such as 10 or 20 years), joint and survivor (payments for two lives, continuing to the survivor at 50-100% of the original amount), and fixed period or fixed amount. The exclusion ratio determines the tax treatment of each payment: Exclusion Ratio = Investment in Contract / Expected Return. The excluded portion is a tax-free return of basis; the remainder is taxable as ordinary income.

Taxation of annuities: contributions to non-qualified annuities are made with after-tax dollars (no deduction). Growth is tax-deferred. Withdrawals during accumulation are taxed LIFO (earnings first, as ordinary income) with a 10% penalty before age 59 1/2. Qualified annuities (held in IRAs or employer plans) follow the same distribution rules as the underlying retirement account. The 1035 exchange allows tax-free exchange of one annuity for another, or a life insurance policy for an annuity (but NOT an annuity for a life insurance policy).
Chapter 5: Health Insurance Fundamentals

Health insurance protects against the financial consequences of illness and injury. Major medical insurance covers a broad range of medical expenses with a deductible (the amount the insured pays before coverage begins), coinsurance (the percentage split between insurer and insured, typically 80/20), and an out-of-pocket maximum (the most the insured pays in a year, after which the insurer pays 100%). The Affordable Care Act (ACA) established essential health benefits that all individual and small group plans must cover, prohibited denial of coverage for pre-existing conditions, and eliminated lifetime benefit limits.

Disability income insurance replaces a portion of income (typically 60-70%) when the insured cannot work due to illness or injury. Own-occupation coverage pays if the insured cannot perform their specific occupation. Any-occupation coverage pays only if the insured cannot perform any occupation for which they are reasonably qualified. The elimination period (waiting period) is the time between disability onset and benefit commencement — longer elimination periods reduce premiums. Tax treatment: employer-paid premiums result in taxable benefits; employee-paid (after-tax) premiums result in tax-free benefits.
Chapter 6: Health Insurance Products

Long-term care (LTC) insurance covers custodial care, assisted living, home health care, and skilled nursing facility care. Benefits are triggered when the insured cannot perform 2 or more activities of daily living (ADLs: bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, continence) or has a cognitive impairment. Tax-qualified LTC policies allow premium deductions as medical expenses (subject to age-based limits) and provide tax-free benefits up to a per-diem limit ($420/day in 2026). Partnership policies protect assets equal to benefits received from Medicaid spend-down requirements.

Medicare is the federal health insurance program for individuals age 65+ and certain disabled individuals. Part A covers hospital care (funded by payroll taxes, no premium for most). Part B covers physician services and outpatient care (monthly premium, approximately $185/month in 2026). Part C (Medicare Advantage) is a private alternative to Parts A and B. Part D covers prescription drugs. Medigap (Medicare Supplement) policies fill the gaps in Original Medicare coverage. Medicare does NOT cover long-term custodial care, dental, vision, or hearing (with limited exceptions under Medicare Advantage).
Chapter 7: Insurance Regulation and Ethics

Insurance is regulated primarily at the state level. Each state has an insurance department headed by a commissioner (or superintendent) who oversees licensing, market conduct, rate approval, and consumer protection. The McCarran-Ferguson Act (1945) affirmed state regulation of insurance and exempted insurance from most federal antitrust laws. Producers (agents and brokers) must be licensed in each state where they sell insurance. Continuing education is required for license renewal (typically 24 hours per renewal period, including ethics).

Unfair trade practices include misrepresentation (making false statements about a policy), twisting (using misrepresentation to induce a policyholder to replace an existing policy), churning (replacing policies to generate commissions without benefit to the client), rebating (giving part of the commission to the client as an inducement to purchase), and defamation (making false statements about a competitor). Replacement regulations require the agent to provide a comparison of the existing and proposed policies and to notify the existing insurer of the replacement. The agent has a duty to ensure that the replacement is in the client's best interest.

rows: Pre-licensing education (varies by state, typically 20-40 hours)

chapters.title: Appendix: A.1 Life Insurance Policy Provisions
chapters.intro:
The free-look provision gives the policyowner a specified period (typically 10-30 days, depending on state law) after policy delivery to examine the policy and return it for a full refund of premiums paid. The grace period (typically 31 days for annual premium policies, 10 days for monthly) allows the policyowner to make a late premium payment without the policy lapsing. If the insured dies during the grace period, the death benefit is paid minus the overdue premium.
The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from contesting the validity of the policy after it has been in force for a specified period (typically 2 years from the date of issue). After the contestability period, the insurer cannot void the policy even if the application contained material misrepresentations — with the exception of fraud (in most states). The misstatement of age or sex provision allows the insurer to adjust the death benefit (not void the policy) if the insured's age or sex was misstated on the application.
The entire contract clause states that the policy and the attached application constitute the entire contract between the parties. No statement made by the applicant can be used to void the policy unless it is contained in the written application attached to the policy. The reinstatement provision allows a lapsed policy to be reinstated within a specified period (typically 3-5 years) if the policyowner pays all back premiums with interest, provides evidence of insurability, and pays any outstanding policy loans. A reinstated policy has a new contestability period and a new suicide exclusion period.

subsections.title: A.2 Life Insurance Riders
paragraphs: The waiver of premium rider waives premium payments if the insured becomes totally disabled (as defined in the rider) for a specified waiting period (typically 6 months). The policy remains in force as if premiums were being paid. The guaranteed insurability rider (guaranteed purchase option) allows the insured to purchase additional coverage at specified future dates (typically every 3 years or at life events such as marriage or birth of a child) without evidence of insurability. The accelerated death benefit rider (living benefit) allows the insured to receive a portion of the death benefit (typically 25-75%) while still living if diagnosed with a terminal illness (life expectancy of 12-24 months).

The accidental death benefit rider (double indemnity) pays an additional death benefit (typically equal to the face amount) if the insured dies as a result of an accident. The death must occur within a specified period after the accident (typically 90 days). Exclusions typically include death from war, aviation (as a pilot), and self-inflicted injuries. The term rider adds term insurance coverage to a permanent policy, providing additional temporary coverage at a lower cost than purchasing a separate term policy. The children's term rider provides term coverage for all children of the insured, typically converting to permanent coverage at age 25.

subsections.title: A.3 Health Insurance Fundamentals
paragraphs: Health insurance provides coverage for medical expenses, including hospitalization, surgery, physician visits, prescription drugs, and preventive care. The Affordable Care Act (ACA) established key requirements: guaranteed issue (insurers cannot deny coverage based on pre-existing conditions), community rating (premiums can vary only by age, tobacco use, family size, and geographic area), essential health benefits (10 categories of coverage that all ACA-compliant plans must include), and the metal tier system (Bronze: 60% actuarial value, Silver: 70%, Gold: 80%, Platinum: 90%).

Health Savings Accounts (HSAs) are available to individuals enrolled in a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP). For 2026, the minimum deductible is $1,700 (individual) / $3,400 (family), and the maximum out-of-pocket is $8,500 (individual) / $17,000 (family). HSA contributions are tax-deductible, growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free (triple tax benefit). After age 65, HSA withdrawals for non-medical expenses are taxed as ordinary income (no penalty). Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs) allow employees to set aside pre-tax dollars for medical expenses, but FSAs have a use-it-or-lose-it provision (with a $640 carryover or 2.5-month grace period).

Medicare is the federal health insurance program for individuals age 65+ and certain disabled individuals. Part A (Hospital Insurance) covers inpatient hospital care, skilled nursing facility care, home health care, and hospice care. Part A is premium-free for individuals with 40+ quarters of Social Security coverage. Part B (Medical Insurance) covers physician services, outpatient care, preventive services, and durable medical equipment. Part B has a monthly premium ($185/month standard in 2026, higher for high-income individuals under IRMAA). Part D covers prescription drugs through private plans. Medicare Advantage (Part C) is an alternative to Original Medicare, offered by private insurers, combining Parts A, B, and usually D with additional benefits.

chapters.title: Appendix: Disability Insurance
chapters.intro:
Disability insurance replaces a portion of income (typically 60-70%) when the insured is unable to work due to illness or injury. Own-occupation coverage pays benefits if the insured cannot perform the duties of their specific occupation. Any-occupation coverage pays only if the insured cannot perform any occupation for which they are reasonably suited by education, training, or experience. Own-occupation is more expensive but provides broader protection, especially for professionals (physicians, attorneys) whose specialized skills command higher income.
Key disability policy features: the elimination period (waiting period before benefits begin, typically 30-180 days — longer elimination periods reduce premiums), the benefit period (how long benefits are paid, ranging from 2 years to age 65 or lifetime), residual/partial disability benefits (pays a proportional benefit when the insured can work part-time but earns less than before disability), cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) rider (increases benefits annually to keep pace with inflation), and the non-cancelable/guaranteed renewable distinction.
Non-cancelable policies guarantee that the insurer cannot cancel the policy, increase premiums, or change benefits as long as premiums are paid — this is the strongest form of disability coverage. Guaranteed renewable policies guarantee the insured can renew the policy, but the insurer can increase premiums for the entire class of policyholders (not individually). Conditionally renewable policies can be cancelled by the insurer under specified conditions. The tax treatment of disability benefits depends on who paid the premiums: employer-paid premiums result in taxable benefits; individually-paid premiums result in tax-free benefits.
Group life insurance is employer-sponsored coverage. Key features: no individual underwriting (guaranteed issue up to a specified amount), the employer is the policyholder and employees receive certificates of insurance, coverage is typically term insurance equal to 1-2x annual salary, and employees can convert to individual policies upon termination (conversion privilege). Under IRC Section 79, employer-paid group term life insurance premiums are tax-free to the employee for the first $50,000 of coverage. Premiums for coverage exceeding $50,000 are taxable income to the employee based on IRS Table I rates (imputed income).
Long-term care (LTC) insurance covers the cost of care for individuals who cannot perform activities of daily living (ADLs): bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and continence. Benefits are triggered when the insured cannot perform 2 of 6 ADLs or has a severe cognitive impairment. The elimination period (typically 30-90 days) is the waiting period before benefits begin — longer elimination periods reduce premiums. LTC premiums may be tax-deductible as medical expenses (subject to age-based limits). Partnership policies allow the insured to protect assets equal to the benefits received from Medicaid spend-down requirements.
Continuing education is required to maintain a life and health insurance license. Most states require 24 hours of CE every 2 years, including ethics credits. Agents must also complete any state-specific requirements such as flood insurance CE or long-term care partnership program training. Failure to complete CE by the deadline results in license lapse, and the agent must cease all insurance activities until the license is reinstated.
Success on the life and health exam requires mastering both the technical insurance concepts and the state-specific regulatory requirements that govern agent conduct and consumer protection in your jurisdiction.
Figure 15: Life Insurance Product Spectrum
Figure 16: Insurance Needs Analysis Waterfall

subsections.title: Long-Term Care Insurance
paragraphs: Long-term care (LTC) insurance covers the cost of care when the insured needs assistance with activities of daily living (ADLs): bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring (moving in/out of bed/chair), and continence. Benefits are triggered when the insured cannot perform 2 of 6 ADLs or has a severe cognitive impairment (such as Alzheimer's disease). LTC policies typically have a daily or monthly benefit amount ($150-$400/day), a benefit period (2-5 years or lifetime), and an elimination period (30-180 days).

LTC coverage options include: nursing home care (the most expensive, averaging $8,000-$12,000/month), assisted living facility care ($4,000-$7,000/month), home health care (professional care provided in the insured's home), adult day care, and hospice care. Inflation protection riders are critical for LTC policies because the cost of care increases significantly over time — a 3-5% compound inflation rider ensures benefits keep pace with rising care costs. Partnership programs in many states allow policyholders to protect assets equal to the benefits received from a qualified LTC policy, providing Medicaid asset protection.

Tax-qualified LTC policies (meeting IRC Section 7702B requirements) provide tax benefits: premiums are deductible as medical expenses (subject to age-based limits and the 7.5% AGI threshold), and benefits received are tax-free up to the per diem limit ($420/day in 2026) or actual costs incurred. Non-tax-qualified policies may provide broader benefit triggers but do not receive favorable tax treatment. Hybrid LTC policies (combining life insurance or annuities with LTC benefits) have become increasingly popular as they guarantee a benefit even if LTC is never needed.

subsections.title: Group Insurance and Employee Benefits
paragraphs: Group life insurance is typically provided by employers as a benefit. Group term life is the most common form, providing coverage for the duration of employment. The employer can deduct premiums as a business expense. The first $50,000 of employer-provided group term life is tax-free to the employee; coverage above $50,000 results in imputed income based on IRS Table I rates. Group life is guaranteed issue (no individual underwriting) during the initial enrollment period, making it valuable for employees with health conditions who might not qualify for individual coverage.

COBRA (Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act) requires employers with 20+ employees to offer continuation of group health coverage to employees and dependents who lose coverage due to a qualifying event (termination, reduction in hours, divorce, death of the covered employee). COBRA coverage lasts 18 months for termination/reduction in hours, 36 months for divorce/death/Medicare eligibility. The former employee pays the full premium plus a 2% administrative fee. COBRA is a bridge to other coverage, not a long-term solution.
Deep Dive: Annuities and Health Insurance

subsections.title: Annuity Types and Taxation
paragraphs: Fixed annuities guarantee a minimum interest rate during the accumulation phase and a fixed payment during the annuitization phase. The insurer bears the investment risk. Immediate annuities begin payments within one year of purchase (single premium immediate annuity — SPIA). Deferred annuities accumulate value over time before payments begin. Annuity payout options include: life only (highest payment, no beneficiary), life with period certain (payments for life or a guaranteed period, whichever is longer), joint and survivor (payments continue for the surviving annuitant), and fixed period/fixed amount.

The exclusion ratio determines the tax-free portion of each annuity payment: Exclusion Ratio = Investment in Contract / Expected Return. For example, if an annuitant invested $100,000 and the expected return is $200,000, the exclusion ratio is 50% — half of each payment is tax-free (return of principal) and half is taxable (earnings). After the investment is fully recovered, all subsequent payments are fully taxable. For qualified annuities (funded with pre-tax dollars in retirement plans), the entire payment is taxable because no after-tax contributions were made.

The 1035 exchange allows tax-free exchanges between certain insurance products: life insurance to life insurance, life insurance to annuity, annuity to annuity, and life insurance to long-term care. An annuity CANNOT be exchanged tax-free for life insurance (this would convert tax-deferred gains into a tax-free death benefit). The exchange must be between the same owner and insured/annuitant. Partial 1035 exchanges are also permitted. This is a critical planning tool for clients who want to change products without triggering a taxable event.

subsections.title: Health Insurance Fundamentals
paragraphs: Health insurance plans are categorized by metal tier under the Affordable Care Act: Bronze (60% actuarial value — lowest premiums, highest out-of-pocket costs), Silver (70% AV), Gold (80% AV), and Platinum (90% AV — highest premiums, lowest out-of-pocket costs). High-Deductible Health Plans (HDHPs) qualify for Health Savings Account (HSA) contributions. 2026 HDHP minimums: $1,700 individual / $3,400 family deductible. 2026 HSA contribution limits: $4,400 individual / $8,750 family ($1,000 catch-up for age 55+).

HSAs offer triple tax advantages: contributions are tax-deductible (or pre-tax if through payroll), growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free. Unlike FSAs, HSAs have no use-it-or-lose-it provision — funds roll over indefinitely. After age 65, HSA funds can be withdrawn for any purpose (taxed as ordinary income, like a traditional IRA) or tax-free for medical expenses. HSAs are individually owned and portable — they are not tied to an employer. This makes them a powerful retirement planning tool in addition to their healthcare function.
Deep Dive: Life Insurance Policy Provisions and Riders

subsections.title: Standard Policy Provisions
paragraphs: Life insurance policies contain standard provisions required by state law. The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from contesting the policy after it has been in force for 2 years (except for non-payment of premiums). Even material misrepresentations on the application cannot void the policy after the contestability period. The suicide clause excludes death benefits if the insured commits suicide within the first 2 years of the policy — after 2 years, suicide is covered. The misstatement of age provision allows the insurer to adjust the death benefit to what the premium would have purchased at the correct age, rather than voiding the policy.

The grace period (typically 31 days for annual premium policies) allows the policyholder to pay a late premium without losing coverage. If the insured dies during the grace period, the death benefit is paid minus the overdue premium. The reinstatement provision allows a lapsed policy to be reinstated within a specified period (typically 3-5 years) if the policyholder provides evidence of insurability and pays all back premiums with interest. A new contestability period begins upon reinstatement. The entire contract provision states that the policy and attached application constitute the entire contract — no external documents can modify the agreement.

Common life insurance riders include: waiver of premium (waives premiums if the insured becomes totally disabled — typically requires disability for 6 months with a retroactive waiver to the date of disability), accelerated death benefit (allows access to a portion of the death benefit if the insured is diagnosed with a terminal illness with 12-24 months life expectancy), guaranteed insurability rider (allows the purchase of additional coverage at specified future dates without evidence of insurability), accidental death benefit (pays an additional death benefit — typically double — if death results from an accident), and children's term rider (provides term coverage on the insured's children).

subsections.title: Life Insurance Needs Analysis Methods
paragraphs: The human life value approach calculates the present value of the insured's future earnings, adjusted for taxes, personal consumption, and inflation. For example, a 35-year-old earning $100,000 with 30 years to retirement, assuming 3% annual raises and a 5% discount rate, might have a human life value of $2.5 million. The needs approach is more practical: it identifies specific financial needs that would arise at death (final expenses, debt payoff, income replacement, education funding, emergency fund) and subtracts existing resources (savings, group life, Social Security survivor benefits, spouse's income) to determine the insurance gap.

The capital retention approach assumes the death benefit is invested and only the investment income is used, preserving the principal for the next generation. This requires a larger death benefit but provides permanent income replacement. The capital liquidation approach assumes both principal and interest are consumed over a specified period (e.g., until the youngest child reaches age 22 or the surviving spouse reaches retirement age). This requires a smaller death benefit but provides no legacy. Most financial planners use a combination of approaches, with the needs analysis as the primary method supplemented by human life value as a reasonableness check.

subsections.title: Life & Health Key Concepts Review
paragraphs: Replacement regulations protect consumers when replacing existing life insurance or annuity policies. The replacing agent must: provide the applicant with a Notice Regarding Replacement, obtain a list of all existing policies to be replaced, and submit copies to both the existing and replacing insurers. The existing insurer has 20 days to contact the policyholder to provide information about the existing policy's benefits. Twisting (inducing replacement through misrepresentation) and churning (replacing for the purpose of generating commissions) are prohibited and can result in license revocation.

Key life insurance tax rules: death benefits are generally income tax-free to the beneficiary (IRC Section 101(a)). Cash value growth is tax-deferred. Policy loans are not taxable events (as long as the policy remains in force). Surrendering a policy triggers income tax on the gain (surrender value minus cost basis). Modified Endowment Contracts (MECs) — policies that fail the 7-pay test — lose favorable tax treatment for lifetime distributions: withdrawals and loans are taxed LIFO (earnings first) and subject to a 10% penalty if taken before age 59 1/2.

The 7-pay test determines whether a life insurance policy is a MEC: if cumulative premiums paid in the first 7 years exceed the net level premium that would pay up the policy in 7 years, the policy becomes a MEC. Once a policy is classified as a MEC, it cannot be reclassified. This rule prevents people from overfunding life insurance policies to use them primarily as tax-sheltered investment vehicles. MECs still provide income tax-free death benefits, but lifetime access to cash value is penalized.

chapters.title: 2026 OBBBA Legislative Update
chapters.intro: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), signed July 4, 2025, made permanent many provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and introduced new provisions effective 2026. All figures below are verified for 2026.

chapters.title: Supplementary Scenarios and Exam Preparation
chapters.intro:
SCENARIO Life Insurance Needs Analysis James, 35, married, 2 kids (3, 5). Income $120K. Spouse $40K. NEEDS: Income replacement 20yr × $80K = $1.6M + Mortgage $350K + Education $240K + Final $25K + Emergency $30K = $2,245,000 RESOURCES: Group life $240K + Savings $85K + SS survivors $450K = $775,000 GAP: $1,470,000 Recommendation: $1.5M 20-year level term (~$50/month vs $1,200+ for whole life). Why term? Temporary need — children independent in 20 years.
SCENARIO Annuity Suitability Analysis Mrs. Chen, 72, retired teacher, $300K savings, risk-averse, needs predictable income. Rep recommends variable annuity with 7-year surrender and 2.5% annual fees. PROBLEMS: 7-year lock-up at age 72 (may need funds for medical). Variable returns contradict need for predictable income. 2.5% fees vs 0.5% for bond ladder. BETTER FIT: Fixed annuity with 3-year surrender, or diversified bond portfolio. RULE: Match product risk, liquidity, and time horizon to CLIENT'S needs.
COMMON TRAP Life & Health Traps "Whole life is always better because it builds cash value." → WRONG for temporary needs. "Universal life has guaranteed returns." → WRONG — guaranteed MINIMUM, not guaranteed actual. "VUL is suitable for conservative clients." → WRONG — policyholder bears investment risk. "Annuity→Life 1035 exchange is tax-free." → WRONG — the one prohibited direction. "Non-cumulative preferred must eventually pay missed dividends." → WRONG — once missed, gone forever.
EXAM TIP 1035 Exchange Rules Tax-free exchanges under Section 1035: Life → Life ✓ Life → Annuity ✓ Annuity → Annuity ✓ Annuity → Life ✗ (NEVER — this is ALWAYS the exam trap) Must be same insured. New policy inherits old cost basis.
WORKED EXAMPLE Annuity Tax Calculation Non-qualified annuity: $100,000 invested (cost basis). Current value: $150,000. Withdraw $30,000: LIFO rule — earnings come out first. Entire $30,000 = ordinary income (still $20K earnings remaining). Withdraw $60,000: First $50,000 = ordinary income (all earnings). Next $10,000 = tax-free return of basis. 10% penalty applies on earnings withdrawn before age 59½.

practice_questions.prompt: Insurable interest for life insurance must exist at:
practice_questions.explanation: For life insurance, insurable interest must exist at the time of application. For property insurance, it must exist at the time of loss.

practice_questions.prompt: Which life insurance product has NO cash value?
practice_questions.explanation: Term life provides pure death protection with no cash value accumulation. All permanent products (whole, universal, variable) build cash value.

practice_questions.prompt: The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from contesting the policy after:
practice_questions.explanation: The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from voiding the policy after it has been in force for 2 years, even if the application contained misrepresentations (except for non-payment of premiums).

practice_questions.prompt: A Modified Endowment Contract (MEC) results from:
practice_questions.explanation: A policy becomes a MEC if cumulative premiums exceed the 7-pay test. MECs lose favorable FIFO taxation — withdrawals and loans are taxed LIFO with a 10% penalty before 59 1/2.

practice_questions.prompt: Medicare Part B covers:
practice_questions.explanation: Part A = hospital. Part B = physician/outpatient. Part C = Medicare Advantage (private). Part D = prescription drugs. Long-term custodial care is NOT covered by Medicare.

practice_questions.prompt: The elimination period in disability insurance is:
options: The waiting period before benefits begin
The time to process the application
The grace period for premium payment
practice_questions.explanation: The elimination period is the waiting period between disability onset and benefit commencement. Longer elimination periods reduce premiums.

practice_questions.prompt: The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from voiding a policy after:
practice_questions.explanation: The incontestability period is typically 2 years from the date of issue. After this period, the insurer cannot contest the policy even for material misrepresentation (except fraud in most states).

practice_questions.prompt: An HSA contribution for a family in 2026 is limited to:
practice_questions.explanation: The 2026 family HSA contribution limit is $8,750 ($9,550 with the $1,000 catch-up for age 55+). The $17,000 figure is the maximum out-of-pocket for family HDHP coverage.

practice_questions.prompt: The waiver of premium rider waives premiums when the insured:
options: Is diagnosed with a terminal illness
practice_questions.explanation: The waiver of premium rider waives premiums during total disability. The accelerated death benefit rider applies to terminal illness. Neither is triggered by age or retirement.

practice_questions.prompt: Medicare Part B covers:
practice_questions.explanation: Part B covers physician services, outpatient care, preventive services, and DME. Part A covers hospital/SNF. Part D covers prescriptions.

exam_overview: Pre-licensing education (varies by state, typically 20-40 hours)

flashcards.definition: Pure death benefit protection for a specified period. No cash value. Lowest cost per dollar of coverage. Convertible term allows conversion to permanent without medical underwriting.
Permanent insurance with guaranteed death benefit, level premiums, and guaranteed cash value growth. Dividends (participating policies) are not guaranteed.
Flexible premium permanent insurance with adjustable death benefit. Cash value earns a declared interest rate. Risk of lapse if funding is insufficient.
UL with cash value growth tied to a market index (e.g., S&P 500) subject to a cap and floor. Offers upside potential with downside protection.
Permanent insurance with cash value invested in separate accounts (subaccounts). Investment risk borne by the policyholder. Requires securities license.
Combines flexible premiums of UL with investment options of variable life. Cash value in subaccounts; investment risk on policyholder.
Must exist at the time of application for life insurance. The applicant must face financial loss from the insured's death.
Insurance contract where the exchange of values is unequal. The insurer may pay far more (death benefit) than premiums received, or nothing at all.
Insurance contract drafted entirely by the insurer. Ambiguities are interpreted in favor of the policyholder (insured).
Only the insurer makes a legally enforceable promise (to pay claims). The policyholder can stop paying premiums at any time.
The insurer's obligation to pay is conditional upon certain events occurring (e.g., death, disability) and conditions being met (e.g., premiums paid).
Both parties to an insurance contract must act honestly. Material misrepresentation can void the contract within the contestability period.
Typically 2 years from policy issuance. During this period, the insurer can void the policy for material misrepresentation.
After the contestability period, the insurer cannot deny a claim based on misstatements in the application, except for fraud in some states.
Typically 30-31 days after premium due date during which the policy remains in force. If insured dies during grace period, death benefit is paid minus overdue premium.
10-30 days (varies by state) after policy delivery during which the policyholder can return the policy for a full refund.
Loan from the insurance company using the policy's cash value as collateral. Outstanding loans reduce the death benefit and cash value.
Methods of paying death benefits: lump sum, fixed period, fixed amount, life income, interest only. Beneficiary typically chooses.
Tax-free exchange of one life insurance policy for another, or life to annuity. Cannot exchange annuity to life insurance.
A life insurance policy that fails the 7-pay test. Distributions are taxed LIFO (gains first) and subject to 10% penalty before age 59½.
Allows early access to a portion of the death benefit if the insured is diagnosed with a terminal illness, typically defined as life expectancy under 12-24 months.
Rider that waives premium payments if the insured becomes totally disabled. Policy remains in force during disability.
Allows the policyholder to purchase additional coverage at specified dates without evidence of insurability.
Health Maintenance Organization. Requires primary care physician referral for specialists. Generally lowest premiums, most restrictive network.
Preferred Provider Organization. No referral needed for specialists. Higher premiums but more flexibility. In-network and out-of-network coverage.

Health Savings Account. Triple tax advantage: tax-deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for qualified medical expenses. Requires HDHP. 2026 limits: $4,400 self/$8,750 family.
Continues group health coverage for 18-36 months after qualifying events (termination, reduction in hours). Employee pays full premium plus 2% admin fee.
Waiting period before disability benefits begin. Longer elimination periods reduce premiums. Common periods: 30, 60, 90, 180 days.
Own-occ: disabled if unable to perform duties of your own occupation. Any-occ: disabled only if unable to perform any occupation. Own-occ is more favorable and more expensive.
Benefits triggered when insured cannot perform 2 of 6 Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, continence) or has cognitive impairment.
Accumulation phase: premiums build cash value tax-deferred. Distribution (annuitization) phase: payments made to annuitant.
Fixed: guaranteed minimum interest rate, insurer bears investment risk. Variable: invested in subaccounts, annuitant bears investment risk. Variable requires securities license.
Earnings taxed as ordinary income when withdrawn. LIFO: gains come out first. 10% penalty on earnings withdrawn before age 59½. Exclusion ratio applies during annuitization.
Replacing an existing life insurance policy with a new one. Requires comparison disclosure and signed replacement forms. Agent must determine if replacement is in client's best interest.
Misrepresenting or making incomplete comparisons to induce a policyholder to lapse, surrender, or replace an existing policy. Prohibited unfair trade practice.
Excessive replacement of policies primarily to generate commissions rather than to benefit the policyholder. Prohibited practice.
Giving part of the agent's commission back to the policyholder as an inducement to purchase. Prohibited in most states.
Process of determining the appropriate amount and type of insurance coverage based on the client's financial situation, goals, and risk exposures.
Employer-sponsored coverage, typically term insurance. Master policy issued to the employer. Employees receive certificates. Conversion right upon termination.
Options when a permanent life policy lapses: cash surrender value, reduced paid-up insurance, or extended term insurance. Required by law.

tracks.name: General Insurance
tracks.title: General Insurance
tracks.subtitle: General Insurance Concepts Study Manual Comprehensive Study Guide Updated March 2026  |  OBBBA Integrated

chapters.title: Table of Contents
chapters.intro: Right-click → 'Update Field' in Word for clickable page numbers.

chapters.title: How to Use This Manual
chapters.intro:
This manual covers the foundational insurance concepts that apply across all lines of insurance — life, health, property, and casualty. It is designed for individuals preparing for general insurance licensing examinations or seeking a comprehensive understanding of insurance principles. The concepts covered here form the foundation for the more specialized Life & Health and Property & Casualty manuals in this library.
Chapter 1: Insurance Principles and Concepts
Insurance is a mechanism for transferring risk from an individual or entity (the insured) to an insurance company (the insurer) in exchange for a premium. The law of large numbers allows insurers to predict aggregate losses with reasonable accuracy by pooling many similar, independent risks. The larger the pool, the more predictable the losses become. This predictability allows insurers to set premiums that are sufficient to pay claims, cover operating expenses, and generate a reasonable profit.
Six principles govern insurance: (1) Insurable interest — the insured must have a financial stake in the subject of insurance. (2) Indemnity — the insured should be restored to their pre-loss financial position, not profit from a loss (applies to property and health, not life insurance). (3) Utmost good faith — both parties must deal honestly; the applicant must disclose all material facts. (4) Subrogation — after paying a claim, the insurer can pursue the responsible third party. (5) Contribution — when multiple policies cover the same loss, each insurer pays its proportional share. (6) Proximate cause — the insurer is liable only for losses proximately caused by a covered peril.
Risks are classified as pure risk (possibility of loss only, insurable) vs. speculative risk (possibility of gain or loss, generally not insurable). Insurable risks must be: (1) due to chance, (2) definite and measurable, (3) statistically predictable, (4) not catastrophic to the insurer, (5) economically feasible to insure, and (6) the loss must represent a hardship to the insured. Hazards increase the likelihood or severity of loss: physical hazards (icy roads), moral hazards (dishonesty, intent to cause loss), and morale hazards (carelessness due to having insurance).
Chapter 2: The Insurance Contract
An insurance policy is a legal contract requiring four elements: offer and acceptance (the application is the offer; the insurer's issuance of the policy is the acceptance), consideration (the premium paid by the insured and the promise to pay claims by the insurer), legal purpose (the contract must not violate public policy), and competent parties (both parties must have legal capacity). Insurance contracts are contracts of adhesion (written by the insurer, accepted or rejected by the insured — ambiguities are interpreted in favor of the insured), aleatory contracts (the values exchanged are unequal and depend on an uncertain event), unilateral contracts (only the insurer makes a legally enforceable promise), and conditional contracts (the insurer's obligation to pay is conditioned on the insured meeting policy conditions).
Policy components include: declarations (who, what, when, where, how much), insuring agreement (the insurer's promise to pay), conditions (obligations of both parties), exclusions (what is not covered), and endorsements/riders (modifications to the standard policy). Common policy conditions include: duty to report losses promptly, duty to cooperate with the insurer's investigation, duty to protect property from further damage, and duty to provide proof of loss. Failure to comply with conditions can result in denial of the claim.
Chapter 3: Types of Insurers and Distribution
Stock insurance companies are owned by shareholders and operated for profit. Mutual insurance companies are owned by policyholders and may pay dividends (return of premium) to policyholders. Reciprocal exchanges are unincorporated groups of individuals who insure each other. Lloyd's of London is a marketplace where syndicates of underwriters accept risk. Fraternal benefit societies are nonprofit organizations that provide insurance to their members.
Insurance distribution channels include: independent agents (represent multiple insurers, own their book of business), captive/exclusive agents (represent one insurer, the insurer owns the book of business), direct writers (sell directly to consumers without agents), and brokers (represent the insured, not the insurer, and shop for the best coverage and price). Managing general agents (MGAs) have underwriting authority from insurers and can bind coverage, issue policies, and settle claims on behalf of the insurer.
Chapter 4: Underwriting and Claims
Underwriting is the process of evaluating and selecting risks to determine whether to issue a policy and at what premium. The underwriter assesses the applicant's risk characteristics, classifies the risk (preferred, standard, substandard, or declined), and determines the appropriate premium. Underwriting tools include applications, medical exams (for life and health), inspection reports, credit reports, motor vehicle records, and loss history (CLUE reports for property insurance). The goal is to achieve a balanced book of business with an acceptable loss ratio.
Claims handling involves: receiving notice of loss, investigating the claim (determining coverage, cause, and extent of loss), evaluating the claim (applying policy provisions, deductibles, and limits), and settling the claim (paying the appropriate amount). The claims adjuster may be a company adjuster (employee of the insurer), independent adjuster (contracted by the insurer), or public adjuster (hired by the insured to negotiate with the insurer). Unfair claims practices include unreasonable delays, lowball offers, failure to investigate, and misrepresenting policy provisions.
Chapter 5: Insurance Regulation
Insurance is regulated primarily at the state level under the authority affirmed by the McCarran-Ferguson Act (1945). Each state has an insurance department responsible for licensing insurers and producers, approving policy forms and rates, conducting market conduct examinations, handling consumer complaints, and overseeing insurer solvency. The NAIC develops model laws and regulations that states may adopt to promote uniformity. State guaranty associations protect policyholders if an insurer becomes insolvent, paying claims up to specified limits (typically $300,000-$500,000).
Producer licensing requires completing pre-licensing education, passing the state licensing exam, submitting a license application, and undergoing a background check. Licenses must be renewed periodically (typically every 2 years) with continuing education requirements (typically 24 hours per renewal period, including ethics). Producers have a duty to act in the best interest of their clients, disclose material information, avoid misrepresentation, and comply with all applicable laws and regulations. Violations can result in fines, license suspension or revocation, and criminal prosecution.

chapters.title: Appendix: A.1 Principles of Insurance
chapters.intro:
Insurance is a risk transfer mechanism that pools losses of many to compensate the few who suffer losses. For a risk to be insurable: the loss must be definite and measurable, fortuitous (accidental), significant (causing financial hardship), predictable (law of large numbers), and not catastrophic to the insurer. The principle of indemnity restores the insured to their pre-loss financial position, no better and no worse, preventing moral hazard.
Subrogation supports indemnity: after paying a claim, the insurer can pursue recovery from the responsible third party. Insurable interest requires a financial stake in the subject of insurance. For property insurance, insurable interest must exist at the time of loss. For life insurance, insurable interest must exist at the time of application. Utmost good faith requires both parties to deal honestly and disclose all material information.

subsections.title: A.2 Insurance Contract Structure
paragraphs: An insurance policy consists of: declarations page (identifies insured, coverage, period, premium, limits), insuring agreement (what the insurer promises), conditions (duties of both parties), exclusions (what is NOT covered), and endorsements/riders (modifications). Insurance contracts are contracts of adhesion (ambiguities favor the insured), aleatory (unequal exchange depending on chance), unilateral (only insurer makes enforceable promise), and conditional (insurer's obligation depends on insured meeting requirements).

rows: Small premium may yield large claim

subsections.title: 11. The principle of indemnity means:
paragraphs: A) The insured should profit from a lossB) The insured is restored to pre-loss positionC) Maximum policy limit for every claimD) Deductible required for every claim
MASTERY CHECK   Answer: B Indemnity = restored to pre-loss position, no better, no worse.

subsections.title: 12. Insurable interest for life insurance must exist at:
paragraphs: A) Time of lossB) Time of applicationC) Both application and lossD) Time of premium payment
MASTERY CHECK   Answer: B Life insurance: insurable interest at application. Property insurance: at time of loss.
Deep Dive: Insurance Operations and Regulation

chapters.title: Appendix: Insurance Company Operations
chapters.intro:
Insurance companies operate through several key functions: underwriting (evaluating and selecting risks to insure, setting premiums, and determining policy terms), claims management (investigating, evaluating, and settling claims), actuarial science (using statistical methods to calculate premiums, reserves, and financial projections), marketing and distribution (selling policies through agents, brokers, and direct channels), and investment management (investing premium income to generate returns that support claims payments and profitability).
The underwriting process evaluates risk factors to determine insurability and appropriate premium. For life insurance: age, health history, family medical history, occupation, hobbies, tobacco use, and driving record. For property insurance: construction type, location, fire protection class, occupancy, and loss history. For auto insurance: driver age, driving record, vehicle type, annual mileage, and geographic location. Underwriters use classification systems to group similar risks and apply appropriate rates.
Insurance company financial strength is evaluated by rating agencies: A.M. Best (the primary insurance rating agency), Standard & Poor's, Moody's, and Fitch. These ratings assess the insurer's ability to meet its ongoing obligations to policyholders. A.M. Best ratings range from A++ (Superior) to F (In Liquidation). Agents and brokers have a responsibility to place coverage with financially sound insurers. State guaranty funds provide limited protection if an insurer becomes insolvent, but coverage limits vary by state and are typically much lower than policy limits.
13. The McCarran-Ferguson Act established that insurance is primarily regulated by: A) The federal government B) The state governments C) The NAIC D) The SEC. Answer: B. McCarran-Ferguson (1945) affirmed state primacy in insurance regulation. Federal law applies only where state law is inadequate.
14. A contract of adhesion means: A) Both parties negotiate terms equally B) The contract is written by one party on a take-it-or-leave-it basis C) The contract requires mutual agreement D) The contract can be modified by either party. Answer: B. Insurance contracts are contracts of adhesion — written by the insurer, accepted or rejected by the insured. Ambiguities are interpreted in favor of the insured.
15. Twisting is best described as: A) Selling insurance without a license B) Inducing replacement through misrepresentation C) Sharing commissions with unlicensed persons D) Charging excessive premiums. Answer: B. Twisting is inducing a policyholder to replace existing coverage through misrepresentation of the existing or replacement policy. It is a serious violation that can result in license revocation.
The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) is a voluntary organization of state insurance regulators that develops model laws, regulations, and guidelines. While NAIC model laws are not binding, most states adopt them in whole or in part, creating substantial uniformity in insurance regulation across states. Key NAIC initiatives include: the Insurance Regulatory Information System (IRIS) for monitoring insurer financial health, the System for Electronic Rate and Form Filing (SERFF) for streamlining rate and form approvals, and the Uniform Certificate of Authority Application (UCAA) for standardizing insurer licensing across states.
Insurance professionals must maintain the highest ethical standards in all client interactions, ensuring transparency, honesty, and the client's best interests guide every recommendation.
Figure 19: Combined Ratio Components
Figure 20: Risk Management Decision Matrix

subsections.title: Insurance Regulation
paragraphs: Insurance is primarily regulated at the state level. The McCarran-Ferguson Act of 1945 affirmed that states have the primary authority to regulate the insurance industry, with federal law applying only to the extent that state law does not adequately regulate the business of insurance. Each state has a Department of Insurance (or equivalent) headed by an Insurance Commissioner (elected or appointed) who oversees insurer licensing, rate approval, market conduct examinations, consumer complaints, and insurer solvency.

Rate regulation varies by state: prior approval (rates must be approved before use), file-and-use (rates are filed and can be used immediately, subject to later disapproval), use-and-file (rates can be used immediately and filed within a specified period), and open competition (no filing required, market forces determine rates). The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) is a voluntary organization of state insurance commissioners that develops model laws and regulations, promotes uniformity across states, and provides regulatory support services.

Producer (agent/broker) licensing requires passing a state examination, completing pre-licensing education, submitting a license application, and passing a background check. Continuing education is required for license renewal (typically 24 hours every 2 years, including ethics). Producers must maintain separate trust accounts for premium funds (fiduciary responsibility). Producers can be disciplined for: misrepresentation, twisting (inducing a policyholder to replace existing coverage through misrepresentation), rebating (returning part of the premium as an inducement to purchase), and unfair claims practices.
Deep Dive: Claims Process and Ethics

subsections.title: The Claims Process
paragraphs: The claims process begins when the insured reports a loss to the insurer or agent. The insured's duties after a loss include: providing prompt notice (as soon as practicable), protecting the property from further damage, cooperating with the insurer's investigation, providing a sworn proof of loss (within 60 days for most policies), and submitting to examination under oath if requested. The insurer assigns a claims adjuster who investigates the claim, determines coverage, evaluates the loss, and negotiates a settlement.

Types of claims adjusters: company/staff adjusters (employees of the insurance company), independent adjusters (contracted by insurers to handle claims, especially in areas where the insurer has no local staff or during catastrophes), public adjusters (hired by the insured to represent their interests in the claims process — paid a percentage of the settlement, typically 10-15%), and adjusters from TPA (third-party administrators who handle claims for self-insured entities). The adjuster must act in good faith and deal fairly with the insured. Bad faith claims handling can result in extra-contractual damages (damages beyond the policy limits).

Unfair claims settlement practices (prohibited by state law) include: misrepresenting policy provisions, failing to acknowledge claims promptly, failing to adopt reasonable standards for investigation, refusing to pay claims without conducting a reasonable investigation, not attempting in good faith to effectuate prompt and fair settlements when liability is clear, compelling insureds to institute litigation by offering substantially less than amounts ultimately recovered, and making claim payments without indicating the coverage under which payment is made.
Deep Dive: Insurance Marketing and Distribution

subsections.title: Distribution Channels
paragraphs: Insurance is distributed through multiple channels. Independent agents (brokers) represent multiple insurance companies and can shop the market for the best coverage and price for their clients. They own their book of business (expirations) and can move clients between carriers. Captive (exclusive) agents represent a single insurance company and sell only that company's products. The company typically owns the book of business. Direct writers sell directly to consumers without agents (online, phone, mail). Managing General Agents (MGAs) are specialized intermediaries with underwriting authority from insurers — they can bind coverage, issue policies, and sometimes handle claims on behalf of the insurer.

The choice of distribution channel affects the consumer: independent agents provide choice and advocacy, captive agents provide deep knowledge of one company's products, and direct writers often provide lower premiums (no agent commission). Surplus lines brokers are a specialized distribution channel for non-admitted insurance — they must hold a specific surplus lines license and have expertise in placing hard-to-insure risks. Wholesale brokers act as intermediaries between retail agents/brokers and insurers, providing access to markets that the retail agent cannot access directly.

subsections.title: Insurance Terminology Quick Reference
paragraphs: Deductible: the amount the insured must pay before the insurer pays. Types include: flat dollar (fixed amount per claim), percentage (percentage of the insured value, common in hurricane and earthquake coverage), aggregate (total deductible for all claims in a policy period), and elimination period (waiting period for disability and LTC insurance). Higher deductibles reduce premiums because the insured retains more risk. The optimal deductible balances premium savings against the insured's ability to absorb losses.

Coinsurance: in property insurance, a provision requiring the insured to maintain coverage equal to a specified percentage of value (typically 80%). In health insurance, the percentage of covered expenses shared between the insurer and the insured after the deductible is met (e.g., 80/20 coinsurance means the insurer pays 80% and the insured pays 20% until the out-of-pocket maximum is reached). Policy limits: per-occurrence limit (maximum for any single claim), aggregate limit (maximum for all claims in a policy period), and split limits (separate limits for different types of coverage, common in auto liability).

chapters.title: 2026 OBBBA Legislative Update
chapters.intro: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), signed July 4, 2025, made permanent many provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and introduced new provisions effective 2026. All figures below are verified for 2026.

chapters.title: Supplementary Scenarios and Exam Preparation
chapters.intro:
SCENARIO Risk Management Decision Matrix Restaurant owner assesses risks: 1. Kitchen fire → HIGH severity × LOW frequency → TRANSFER (property insurance) + REDUCE (fire suppression) 2. Customer slip/fall → MEDIUM × MEDIUM → TRANSFER (GL insurance) + REDUCE (non-slip mats) 3. Minor food spoilage → LOW × HIGH → RETAIN (self-insure; cost of doing business) 4. Employee theft → MEDIUM × LOW → TRANSFER (fidelity bond) + REDUCE (dual controls, audits) 5. Earthquake → HIGH × VERY LOW → EVALUATE cost/benefit; may retain or transfer
SCENARIO Claims Process Walkthrough Homeowner reports kitchen fire. Process: 1. NOTICE: Insured calls agent/company within 24-72 hours 2. INVESTIGATION: Adjuster inspects damage, photographs, interviews 3. COVERAGE DETERMINATION: Does policy cover fire? Yes (named peril on HO-2, open peril on HO-3) 4. LOSS ADJUSTMENT: Adjuster estimates repair cost at $45,000 5. CHECK COINSURANCE: Home $500K, 80% required, carrying $420K — above $400K minimum, no penalty 6. PAYMENT: $45,000 minus $1,000 deductible = $44,000 to insured 7. SUBROGATION: If fire caused by neighbor's negligence, insurer may pursue recovery
COMMON TRAP Insurance Traps "Insurance is a form of investment." → WRONG. Insurance is risk TRANSFER, not investment. "ACV and replacement cost are the same." → WRONG. ACV deducts depreciation. "Subrogation lets the insured collect twice." → WRONG. Insurer recovers FROM the at-fault party. "Combined ratio >100% means the insurer is failing." → NOT NECESSARILY. Investment income may still produce overall profit.
EXAM TIP Combined Ratio Interpretation Combined Ratio = Loss Ratio + Expense Ratio Below 90%: Excellent underwriting 90-95%: Good underwriting 95-100%: Marginal underwriting profit 100%: Breakeven on underwriting Above 100%: Underwriting LOSS (but may still profit from investment income) Above 110%: Concerning — company may be mispricing risk
WORKED EXAMPLE Combined Ratio Calculation Insurer data: Earned premiums $10M. Incurred losses $6.5M. Expenses $3.2M. Loss Ratio = $6.5M / $10M = 65% Expense Ratio = $3.2M / $10M = 32% Combined Ratio = 65% + 32% = 97% Result: 3% underwriting profit per premium dollar. This is a healthy insurer.

practice_questions.prompt: The law of large numbers allows insurers to:
options: Predict aggregate losses with reasonable accuracy
practice_questions.explanation: The law of large numbers states that as the number of similar, independent exposures increases, actual losses approach expected losses, allowing accurate prediction.

practice_questions.prompt: An insurance contract is a contract of adhesion, meaning:
options: The insurer writes the contract and the insured accepts or rejects it
The contract can be modified at any time
Both parties can cancel at will
practice_questions.explanation: Adhesion means the insurer drafts the contract and the insured has no ability to negotiate terms. Ambiguities are interpreted in favor of the insured.

practice_questions.prompt: Which type of hazard involves intentional dishonesty?
practice_questions.explanation: Moral hazard involves dishonesty or intent to cause a loss (e.g., arson for insurance proceeds). Morale hazard involves carelessness. Physical hazard involves physical conditions.

practice_questions.prompt: The principle of indemnity means:
options: The insured should profit from a loss
The insured is restored to pre-loss position
Maximum policy limit for every claim
practice_questions.explanation: Indemnity = restored to pre-loss position, no better, no worse.

practice_questions.prompt: Insurable interest for life insurance must exist at:
practice_questions.explanation: Life insurance: insurable interest at application. Property insurance: at time of loss.

flashcards.definition: A mechanism for transferring risk of financial loss from an individual or entity to an insurer in exchange for a premium.
Uncertainty about outcomes, particularly the possibility of financial loss. Pure risk (loss or no loss) is insurable; speculative risk is not.
The cause of a loss (fire, theft, windstorm). Distinguished from hazard, which is a condition that increases the likelihood of loss.
A tangible condition that increases the probability of loss. Examples: icy sidewalks, faulty wiring, poor construction.
Dishonesty or character defects that increase the chance of loss. Example: arson for profit, staged accidents.
Carelessness or indifference because insurance exists. Example: leaving doors unlocked because theft is covered.
As the number of similar exposure units increases, the actual results will approach the expected probability. Foundation of insurance pricing.
Tendency of higher-risk individuals to seek insurance more than lower-risk individuals. Insurers use underwriting to manage this.
The principle that insurance should restore the insured to their pre-loss financial position, neither more nor less.
After paying a claim, the insurer steps into the insured's legal rights to recover from the responsible third party.
When multiple policies cover the same loss, each insurer pays its proportionate share. Prevents profit from insurance.
Both insurer and insured must act honestly and disclose all material information. Foundation of the insurance contract.
Exchange of unequal values. Insurer may pay far more than premiums received, or nothing at all.
Only the insurer is legally bound to perform. Policyholder can stop paying premiums without legal penalty.
Written by one party (insurer). Ambiguities are construed against the drafter and in favor of the insured.
Insurer's obligation depends on conditions being met: premium paid, loss occurred, timely notice, proof of loss submitted.
Property insurance is personal to the insured and generally cannot be transferred without insurer consent.
Must have a financial stake in the subject of insurance. Required at inception for property, at inception for life.
Representations: statements believed to be true. Warranties: statements guaranteed to be true. Breach of warranty can void the policy.
Intentional failure to disclose material information. Can void the policy if the concealed information was material to the risk.
Waiver: voluntary giving up of a known right. Estoppel: prevention from asserting a right after taking an inconsistent position.
First page of the policy identifying the named insured, policy period, coverage limits, deductibles, and premium.
Section of the policy describing what the insurer promises to do (pay covered losses) and the scope of coverage.
Policy provisions that outline duties of both insured and insurer: premium payment, notice of loss, cooperation, proof of loss.
Specific causes of loss, types of property, or circumstances not covered by the policy. Manage moral hazard and uninsurable risks.
Modifications to the standard policy that add, delete, or change coverage. Override conflicting policy provisions.
Amount the insured pays before the insurer pays. Higher deductibles reduce premiums. Types: flat, percentage, aggregate, franchise.
Requires insuring to a stated percentage (usually 80%) of value. Penalty applies at claim time if underinsured.
Replacement cost minus depreciation. Standard valuation method unless replacement cost coverage is purchased.
Cost to replace damaged property with like kind and quality without deduction for depreciation.
When multiple policies cover the same loss, each pays in proportion to its coverage limit relative to the total.
1) Notice of loss, 2) Investigation, 3) Proof of loss, 4) Adjustment, 5) Payment or denial. Time limits apply at each step.
Individual who investigates claims and determines the amount of loss. Company adjuster, independent adjuster, or public adjuster.
Works for the insured (not the insurer) to negotiate claims. Licensed separately from insurance agents.
Insurer's right to recover and sell damaged property after paying a total loss claim. Reduces the net cost of the claim.
Insurance purchased by insurers to manage risk. Ceding company transfers risk to reinsurer. Reduces catastrophic exposure.
Automatic agreement covering all risks in a defined category. Reinsurer must accept all risks ceded under the treaty.
Individual risk placement. Each risk is separately submitted and either accepted or declined by the reinsurer.
Proportional reinsurance where the reinsurer shares a fixed percentage of all premiums and losses. Simplest form.
Non-proportional reinsurance where the reinsurer pays losses exceeding a specified retention. Most common for catastrophe protection.
Process of identifying, analyzing, and responding to risk. Techniques: avoidance, reduction, retention, transfer.
Eliminating exposure entirely. Most effective but often impractical (e.g., not driving to avoid auto risk).
Lowering the frequency or severity of losses. Examples: sprinkler systems, safety training, security systems.
Assuming the financial consequences of risk. Appropriate for high-frequency, low-severity losses. Self-insurance is an example.
Shifting financial consequences to another party. Insurance is the primary method. Contracts and hold-harmless agreements also transfer risk.
The process of evaluating, selecting, and pricing risks. Determines whether to accept, modify, or reject an application.
The process of determining insurance premiums. Based on loss history, expenses, and desired profit margin. Must be adequate, not excessive, and not unfairly discriminatory.
Incurred losses divided by earned premiums. Measures the percentage of premium used to pay claims.
Underwriting expenses divided by written premiums. Measures operational efficiency.
Loss ratio plus expense ratio. Under 100% indicates underwriting profit. Over 100% indicates underwriting loss.
Insurer organized under the laws of the state where it is writing business. Subject to full state regulation.
Insurer organized under the laws of a different US state. Must obtain a Certificate of Authority to write business.
Protects policyholders when an admitted insurer becomes insolvent. Does NOT cover surplus lines or self-insured entities.
Prohibited activities: misrepresentation, twisting, churning, rebating, unfair discrimination, unfair claims settlement.
Expressed: explicitly granted in the contract. Implied: reasonably necessary to perform duties. Apparent: what a reasonable person would believe the agent can do.

tracks.title: Property & Casualty Insurance
tracks.subtitle: Property and Casualty Insurance Study Manual Comprehensive Study Guide Updated March 2026  |  OBBBA Integrated

chapters.title: Table of Contents
