chapters.title: How to Use This Manual
chapters.intro:
The Property and Casualty (P&C) insurance examination qualifies individuals to sell property insurance (protecting physical assets against loss or damage) and casualty insurance (protecting against liability for injuries or damage caused to others). P&C insurance is fundamentally different from life and health insurance: P&C policies are contracts of indemnity (the insured is compensated for actual losses, not a predetermined benefit amount), they are typically short-term (annual policies), and they cover both first-party losses (damage to the insured's own property) and third-party losses (liability claims from others).
This manual covers six chapters: property insurance principles, homeowners insurance, auto insurance, commercial insurance, casualty and liability, and regulation. The exam typically includes approximately 150 questions. Focus on understanding the differences between policy types, coverage limits, exclusions, and the conditions under which claims are paid or denied.
Chapter 1: Property Insurance Principles
The principle of indemnity limits insurance recovery to the actual financial loss suffered, preventing the insured from profiting from a loss. Actual cash value (ACV) equals replacement cost minus depreciation. Replacement cost value (RCV) covers the cost to replace damaged property with new property of like kind and quality, without deduction for depreciation. Most homeowners prefer replacement cost coverage because ACV can result in significantly lower claim payments for older items.
Coinsurance in property insurance requires the insured to maintain coverage equal to a specified percentage (typically 80%) of the property's replacement cost. If the insured fails to meet the coinsurance requirement, the insurer will only pay a proportional share of the loss. The coinsurance formula: Payment = (Amount of Insurance / Required Amount) x Loss - Deductible. For example, if a building worth $500,000 is insured for $300,000 with an 80% coinsurance clause, the required amount is $400,000. For a $100,000 loss: Payment = ($300,000 / $400,000) x $100,000 = $75,000 minus the deductible.
Subrogation allows the insurer, after paying a claim, to pursue recovery from the third party responsible for the loss. For example, if another driver causes an accident and the insured's insurer pays for the damage, the insurer can seek reimbursement from the at-fault driver's insurer. The insured cannot waive the insurer's right of subrogation after a loss. Other insurance clauses determine how multiple policies covering the same loss share the payment: pro rata (each insurer pays proportionally based on policy limits), excess (one policy is primary, the other pays only amounts exceeding the primary's limits), or escape (one policy pays nothing if other insurance exists).
Chapter 2: Homeowners Insurance
Homeowners insurance policies are standardized forms (HO-1 through HO-8) providing coverage for the dwelling, other structures, personal property, loss of use, and personal liability. HO-3 (Special Form) is the most common policy: it covers the dwelling on an open-perils basis (all risks except those specifically excluded) and personal property on a named-perils basis (only the 16 named perils). HO-5 (Comprehensive Form) covers both the dwelling and personal property on an open-perils basis, providing the broadest coverage. HO-4 (Contents Broad Form) is for renters, covering personal property only. HO-6 (Unit-Owners Form) is for condominium owners.
Standard homeowners exclusions include flood, earthquake, war, nuclear hazard, ordinance or law, neglect, intentional loss, and earth movement. Flood insurance is available through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP). Earthquake coverage is available as an endorsement or separate policy. Personal liability coverage (Coverage E, typically $100,000-$300,000) protects against claims for bodily injury or property damage caused by the insured. Medical payments to others (Coverage F, typically $1,000-$5,000) pays medical expenses for guests injured on the insured's property regardless of fault.
Chapter 3: Auto Insurance
The Personal Auto Policy (PAP) provides four coverages: Part A (Liability) covers bodily injury and property damage the insured causes to others; Part B (Medical Payments) covers medical expenses for the insured and passengers regardless of fault; Part C (Uninsured/Underinsured Motorists) covers injuries caused by drivers with no or insufficient insurance; and Part D (Physical Damage) covers damage to the insured's own vehicle through collision (any impact) and other-than-collision (comprehensive: theft, fire, vandalism, weather, animal strikes).
Liability limits are expressed as split limits (e.g., 100/300/50: $100,000 per person bodily injury, $300,000 per accident bodily injury, $50,000 per accident property damage) or combined single limit (one amount covering all liability per accident). Most states require minimum liability coverage. No-fault auto insurance (Personal Injury Protection, PIP) requires each driver's own insurer to pay their medical expenses regardless of fault, up to specified limits. In no-fault states, the right to sue for non-economic damages (pain and suffering) is restricted unless injuries meet a specified threshold (verbal or monetary).
Chapter 4: Commercial Insurance
Commercial property insurance covers business-owned buildings, equipment, inventory, and other business personal property. The Business Owners Policy (BOP) packages property and liability coverage for small to medium businesses at a lower cost than purchasing separate policies. Commercial General Liability (CGL) insurance covers third-party claims for bodily injury, property damage, personal injury (libel, slander), and advertising injury arising from business operations. CGL policies are typically written on an occurrence basis (covering claims arising from incidents during the policy period, regardless of when the claim is filed) or claims-made basis (covering claims filed during the policy period, regardless of when the incident occurred).
Workers' compensation insurance covers employees for work-related injuries and illnesses, providing medical benefits, disability income, rehabilitation, and death benefits. Workers' comp is a no-fault system — benefits are paid regardless of who caused the injury. In exchange, employees generally cannot sue their employer for work-related injuries (the exclusive remedy doctrine). Employers' liability insurance (Part B of the workers' comp policy) covers the employer against lawsuits that fall outside the workers' comp system. Professional liability (errors and omissions, or E&O) insurance covers professionals against claims of negligence, errors, or omissions in their professional services.
Chapter 5: Casualty and Liability Insurance
Umbrella liability insurance provides excess liability coverage above the limits of underlying policies (homeowners, auto, commercial). Umbrella policies typically provide $1-$10 million in additional coverage and may also cover claims excluded by underlying policies (drop-down coverage). The insured must maintain specified minimum limits on underlying policies to qualify for umbrella coverage. Umbrella policies are relatively inexpensive because they only pay after underlying limits are exhausted.
Inland marine insurance covers property in transit and specialized equipment (contractors' equipment, fine arts, jewelry, musical instruments). Ocean marine insurance covers ships, cargo, and related liabilities. Surety bonds guarantee performance of a contractual obligation (unlike insurance, which transfers risk, a surety bond involves three parties: the principal who must perform, the obligee who is protected, and the surety who guarantees performance). Fidelity bonds protect employers against employee dishonesty (theft, embezzlement, fraud).
Chapter 6: Insurance Regulation
Property and casualty insurance is regulated at the state level. State insurance departments oversee licensing, rate approval, market conduct, and consumer protection. Rate regulation approaches include prior approval (rates must be approved before use), file-and-use (rates are filed and can be used immediately), use-and-file (rates can be used and must be filed within a specified period), and open competition (no filing required). The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) develops model laws and regulations that states may adopt, promoting uniformity across state lines.
Insurance producers must be licensed in each state where they sell insurance. Continuing education is required for license renewal. Unfair claims settlement practices include failing to acknowledge claims promptly, failing to investigate claims thoroughly, making lowball settlement offers, and failing to provide a reasonable explanation for claim denials. The insured has a duty to cooperate with the insurer's investigation, provide proof of loss, and mitigate further damage. Failure to comply with policy conditions can result in denial of the claim.

chapters.title: Appendix: A.1 Homeowners Insurance Forms
chapters.intro:
Homeowners insurance policies come in several standardized forms. HO-1 (Basic Form) covers 10 named perils only and is rarely used. HO-2 (Broad Form) covers 16 named perils, adding perils such as falling objects, weight of ice/snow, and accidental discharge of water. HO-3 (Special Form) is the most common homeowners policy: it provides open-peril (all-risk) coverage on the dwelling and other structures, and named-peril coverage on personal property. Open-peril means everything is covered unless specifically excluded.
HO-4 (Contents Broad Form) is for renters, covering personal property on a named-peril basis with liability coverage but no dwelling coverage. HO-5 (Comprehensive Form) provides open-peril coverage on both the dwelling AND personal property, the broadest coverage available. HO-6 (Unit-Owners Form) is for condominium owners. HO-8 (Modified Coverage Form) is for older homes where replacement cost exceeds market value, covering the dwelling on a functional replacement cost basis.

subsections.title: A.2 Valuation Methods and Coinsurance
paragraphs: Replacement cost coverage pays the cost to repair or replace damaged property with materials of like kind and quality, without deduction for depreciation. Actual cash value (ACV) coverage pays the replacement cost minus depreciation. The coinsurance clause requires the insured to maintain coverage equal to a specified percentage (typically 80%) of the property's replacement cost. If underinsured, the insurer pays only a proportional share: Payment = (Amount Carried / Amount Required) x Loss - Deductible.

FORMULA   Coinsurance Calculation Home replacement cost: $500,000. 80% coinsurance: $400,000 required. Insured carries $300,000. Loss: $100,000. Deductible: $1,000. Payment = ($300K/$400K) x ($100K-$1K) = 75% x $99K = $74,250. The insured bears a $25,750 penalty for underinsurance.

subsections.title: A.3 Commercial Liability Insurance
paragraphs: Commercial General Liability (CGL) insurance covers businesses against third-party claims for bodily injury, property damage, personal injury, and advertising injury. Coverage A covers bodily injury and property damage from operations and products. Coverage B covers personal and advertising injury (libel, slander, false arrest). Coverage C covers medical payments regardless of fault. CGL policies can be occurrence-based (covers incidents during the policy period regardless of when claimed) or claims-made (covers claims both made and reported during the policy period).

Umbrella liability insurance provides excess coverage above underlying policy limits and may provide broader coverage for claims not covered by underlying policies (subject to a self-insured retention). Professional liability (E&O) insurance covers claims arising from professional services, typically on a claims-made basis. Directors and Officers (D&O) liability insurance protects corporate directors and officers against claims alleging wrongful acts in their management capacity.

subsections.title: 11. The most common homeowners policy form is:
paragraphs: A) HO-1B) HO-2C) HO-3D) HO-5
MASTERY CHECK   Answer: C HO-3 (Special Form) is the most common. It provides open-peril coverage on the dwelling and named-peril coverage on personal property.

subsections.title: 12. Under 80% coinsurance, a homeowner with $300K coverage on a $500K home suffers a $100K loss. Payment (before deductible) is:
paragraphs: MASTERY CHECK   Answer: A Required: $400K (80% of $500K). Carried: $300K. Payment = ($300K/$400K) x $100K = 75% x $100K = $75,000.
Deep Dive: Auto Insurance and Risk Management

chapters.title: Appendix: Auto Insurance Coverage
chapters.intro:
Personal auto insurance consists of several coverage parts. Part A (Liability Coverage) pays for bodily injury and property damage the insured causes to others. Split limits are expressed as three numbers (e.g., 100/300/100): $100,000 per person bodily injury, $300,000 per accident bodily injury, $100,000 per accident property damage. Combined single limit (CSL) provides one limit for all liability claims from an accident. Most states require minimum liability coverage.
Part B (Medical Payments/PIP) pays medical expenses for the insured and passengers regardless of fault. Personal Injury Protection (PIP) in no-fault states provides broader coverage including lost wages and essential services. Part C (Uninsured/Underinsured Motorist) pays when the at-fault driver has no insurance or insufficient coverage. Part D (Physical Damage) includes collision coverage (damage from collision with another object, regardless of fault, minus deductible) and comprehensive coverage (other-than-collision: theft, vandalism, weather, animal strikes, glass breakage).
Auto insurance rating factors include: driver age and experience (younger drivers pay more), driving record (accidents and violations increase premiums), vehicle type (sports cars cost more than sedans), annual mileage, geographic location (urban areas have higher rates), credit score (in most states), and coverage selections. Discounts are available for: multi-policy bundling, good student, defensive driving course, anti-theft devices, low mileage, and multi-car policies.
13. Which homeowners form provides open-peril coverage on BOTH the dwelling and personal property? A) HO-2 B) HO-3 C) HO-5 D) HO-8. Answer: C. HO-5 (Comprehensive Form) provides open-peril coverage on both dwelling and personal property. HO-3 provides open-peril on the dwelling but named-peril on personal property.
14. Workers' compensation operates on what legal principle? A) Negligence B) Strict liability C) Exclusive remedy D) Comparative fault. Answer: C. Workers' comp is an exclusive remedy system: the employee receives guaranteed benefits regardless of fault but gives up the right to sue the employer for negligence.
15. The National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) maximum coverage for a residential building is: A) $100,000 B) $250,000 C) $500,000 D) $1,000,000. Answer: B. NFIP limits: $250,000 residential building, $100,000 residential contents, $500,000 commercial building, $500,000 commercial contents.
16. An experience modification factor of 0.85 means: A) The employer's losses are 85% worse than average B) The employer receives a 15% premium credit C) The employer pays 85% more than manual rates D) The employer's losses are 15% worse than average. Answer: B. A mod factor below 1.0 indicates better-than-average loss experience. 0.85 means the employer pays 85% of the manual rate — a 15% credit.
The coinsurance penalty formula in property insurance: if the insured carries less than the required percentage of coverage, the insurer pays: (Amount Carried / Amount Required) x Loss - Deductible. Example: building worth $500,000 with 80% coinsurance requirement. Required coverage: $400,000. Insured carries only $300,000. Loss: $200,000. Payment: ($300,000 / $400,000) x $200,000 = $150,000. The insured bears the $50,000 shortfall as a penalty for underinsuring. The coinsurance clause incentivizes adequate coverage and prevents insureds from purchasing minimal coverage on high-value properties.
Umbrella liability insurance provides excess coverage above underlying policies (auto, homeowners, CGL) and may also provide broader coverage for claims not covered by underlying policies (drop-down coverage). Personal umbrella policies typically require minimum underlying limits ($250,000/$500,000 auto liability, $300,000 homeowners liability) and provide $1-10 million in additional coverage at relatively low premiums. Commercial umbrella policies work similarly for businesses, sitting above the CGL, auto liability, and employers liability policies.
The property and casualty insurance industry is cyclical, alternating between hard markets (rising premiums, stricter underwriting, reduced capacity) and soft markets (declining premiums, relaxed underwriting, abundant capacity). Understanding the insurance cycle is important for agents advising clients on coverage timing and renewal strategies. In hard markets, agents should help clients improve their risk profiles to obtain better terms, explore alternative risk transfer mechanisms, and consider higher deductibles to manage premium increases.
Property and casualty insurance is the backbone of risk management for individuals and businesses alike. Mastering the fundamentals covered in this manual — policy structure, coverage analysis, claims handling, and regulatory compliance — provides the foundation for a successful career in this essential industry.
Property and casualty professionals serve as trusted advisors helping clients navigate the complex landscape of risk, ensuring adequate protection for their most valuable assets and liabilities.

Figure 18: Coinsurance Penalty Calculation

subsections.title: Risk Management Process
paragraphs: The risk management process consists of five steps: (1) Identify risks — determine all potential sources of loss (property, liability, personnel, net income). (2) Analyze risks — evaluate the frequency (how often losses occur) and severity (how large losses are) of each identified risk. (3) Select risk management techniques — choose the appropriate method for handling each risk. (4) Implement the chosen techniques. (5) Monitor and review — continuously evaluate the effectiveness of the risk management program and adjust as needed.

Risk management techniques include: avoidance (eliminating the risk entirely — e.g., not owning a swimming pool), loss control/reduction (reducing the frequency or severity of losses — e.g., installing sprinklers, security systems), retention (self-insuring through deductibles, self-insured retentions, or captive insurance companies), and transfer (shifting the risk to another party through insurance, hold-harmless agreements, or contractual risk transfer). The general rule: retain high-frequency/low-severity risks (deductibles) and transfer low-frequency/high-severity risks (insurance).
Deep Dive: Commercial Insurance and Specialty Lines

subsections.title: Workers' Compensation Insurance
paragraphs: Workers' compensation insurance provides benefits to employees who suffer work-related injuries or illnesses, regardless of fault. Benefits include: medical expenses (all reasonable and necessary treatment), disability income (temporary total, temporary partial, permanent total, permanent partial — typically 66 2/3% of average weekly wage), rehabilitation (vocational and physical), and death benefits (funeral expenses and income replacement for dependents). Workers' compensation is a statutory coverage — employers are required by law to provide it in all states except Texas (where it's optional).

Workers' compensation operates on an exclusive remedy basis: the employee receives guaranteed benefits regardless of fault, but in exchange gives up the right to sue the employer for negligence (with limited exceptions for intentional harm). Classification codes determine the premium rate based on the type of work performed — clerical workers have much lower rates than construction workers. Experience modification factors adjust premiums based on the employer's actual loss history compared to the industry average: a mod factor below 1.0 indicates better-than-average experience (premium credit), above 1.0 indicates worse (premium surcharge).

subsections.title: Business Owners Policy (BOP)
paragraphs: The Business Owners Policy (BOP) is a package policy designed for small to medium-sized businesses that combines property coverage, business income coverage, and liability coverage in a single policy at a lower premium than purchasing each coverage separately. BOPs are available for eligible businesses (typically retail stores, offices, apartments, and similar low-to-moderate hazard operations). BOPs are NOT available for auto dealers, bars, banks, or contractors.

BOP property coverage is written on a special form (open-peril) basis for the building and business personal property. Business income coverage (also called business interruption) pays for lost income and continuing expenses when a covered peril forces the business to suspend operations. Extra expense coverage pays for additional costs to continue operations during the restoration period (e.g., renting temporary space). BOP liability coverage is equivalent to the CGL occurrence form, covering bodily injury, property damage, personal injury, and advertising injury.
Deep Dive: Inland Marine and Specialty Coverages

subsections.title: Inland Marine Insurance
paragraphs: Inland marine insurance covers property in transit, property held by bailees, mobile equipment, and instrumentalities of transportation and communication. Unlike standard property insurance (which covers property at a fixed location), inland marine covers property that moves or is used in multiple locations. Common inland marine coverages include: builders risk (covers buildings under construction), installation floaters (covers equipment being installed), transit policies (covers goods in transit), bailee coverage (covers property of others in the insured's care, custody, or control), and equipment floaters (covers mobile equipment like contractors' tools and medical equipment).

Inland marine policies are typically written on an open-peril (all-risk) basis with few exclusions, providing broader coverage than standard property forms. Valuation is usually on a replacement cost or agreed value basis. Inland marine is one of the most profitable lines of insurance because losses are generally well-managed and the coverage is tailored to specific risks. The surplus lines market often provides inland marine coverage for unusual or high-value risks that the admitted market cannot accommodate, such as fine art, jewelry collections, and specialized industrial equipment.

subsections.title: Flood and Earthquake Insurance
paragraphs: Flood insurance is excluded from standard property policies and must be purchased separately. The National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) provides flood coverage for residential and commercial properties in participating communities. NFIP limits: $250,000 for residential buildings, $100,000 for residential contents, $500,000 for commercial buildings, $500,000 for commercial contents. Private flood insurance is available for higher limits and broader coverage. Earthquake insurance is also excluded from standard policies and must be purchased as a separate policy or endorsement. Earthquake policies typically have high percentage deductibles (10-20% of the coverage amount) rather than flat dollar deductibles.

subsections.title: Insurance Claim Scenarios and Calculations
paragraphs: Actual Cash Value (ACV) calculation: Replacement Cost - Depreciation = ACV. A 10-year-old roof with a 20-year life and $20,000 replacement cost has an ACV of $10,000 ($20,000 - 50% depreciation). Under replacement cost coverage, the insurer pays the full $20,000 to replace the roof (no depreciation deduction). Most policies pay ACV initially and the difference between ACV and replacement cost after the repairs are completed.

Other insurance (coordination of benefits) clauses determine how multiple policies share a loss. The most common method is pro rata by limits: each insurer pays its proportion of the total limits. For example, if Policy A has $100,000 limits and Policy B has $200,000 limits, and the loss is $150,000: Policy A pays 1/3 ($50,000) and Policy B pays 2/3 ($100,000). The excess/primary method designates one policy as primary (pays first up to its limits) and the other as excess (pays only after the primary is exhausted). Auto insurance typically uses the policy following the vehicle as primary.

Subrogation allows the insurer to recover claim payments from the responsible third party. Example: the insured's car is damaged by another driver. The insured's collision coverage pays for repairs (minus deductible). The insurer then subrogate against the at-fault driver's liability insurance to recover the claim payment. If successful, the insured's deductible is also recovered. The insured must cooperate with subrogation efforts and must not do anything to impair the insurer's subrogation rights (such as signing a release with the at-fault party without the insurer's consent).

chapters.title: 2026 OBBBA Legislative Update
chapters.intro: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), signed July 4, 2025, made permanent many provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and introduced new provisions effective 2026. All figures below are verified for 2026.

practice_questions.prompt: The most common homeowners policy form is:
practice_questions.explanation: HO-3 (Special Form) is the most common, covering the dwelling on open-perils basis and personal property on named-perils basis.

practice_questions.prompt: Actual cash value equals:
practice_questions.explanation: ACV = Replacement Cost - Depreciation. This is the standard for indemnifying property losses unless replacement cost coverage is purchased.

practice_questions.prompt: Part A of the Personal Auto Policy covers:
options: Liability for bodily injury and property damage
practice_questions.explanation: Part A = Liability, Part B = Medical Payments, Part C = Uninsured/Underinsured Motorists, Part D = Physical Damage.

practice_questions.prompt: Workers' compensation is a:
practice_questions.explanation: Workers' comp is no-fault — benefits are paid regardless of who caused the injury. In exchange, employees generally cannot sue their employer.

practice_questions.prompt: The most common homeowners policy form is:
practice_questions.explanation: HO-3 (Special Form) is the most common. It provides open-peril coverage on the dwelling and named-peril coverage on personal property.

practice_questions.prompt: Under 80% coinsurance, a homeowner with $300K coverage on a $500K home suffers a $100K loss. Payment (before deductible) is:

flashcards.definition: Covers dwelling and personal property against 16 named perils. More coverage than HO-1 (basic) but less than HO-3.
Most common homeowners policy. Covers dwelling on open-peril basis (all risks except exclusions). Personal property on named-peril basis.
Covers tenant's personal property on named-peril basis. Does not cover the dwelling structure.
Premium homeowners policy covering both dwelling and personal property on open-peril basis.
Covers condo unit owner's personal property and interior improvements. Association master policy covers building exterior and common areas.
Designed for older homes where replacement cost exceeds market value. Pays on actual cash value basis rather than replacement cost.
Open peril (all-risk): covers all causes of loss except those specifically excluded. Named peril: covers only the perils specifically listed in the policy.
Replacement cost: cost to replace with like kind and quality, no depreciation deduction. Actual Cash Value: replacement cost minus depreciation.
Requires insuring property to a specified percentage (usually 80%) of its value. Failing to meet the requirement results in a penalty at claim time.
(Amount carried / Amount required) x Loss = Payment. If you insure for less than 80% of value, you become a co-insurer for the difference.
The insurer's right to pursue a third party that caused the loss after paying the insured's claim. Prevents the insured from collecting twice.
Insurance should restore the insured to their pre-loss financial condition, not create a profit. Foundation of property insurance.
Basic coverage for dwellings not eligible for homeowners policies (e.g., rental properties). Named-peril coverage only.
Broadest dwelling form. Open-peril coverage on the dwelling. Named-peril on personal property.
Part A: Liability. Part B: Medical Payments. Part C: Uninsured/Underinsured Motorists. Part D: Physical Damage (comprehensive and collision).
Bodily injury and property damage liability. Split limits (e.g., 100/300/50) or combined single limit. Follows the driver, not the vehicle.
Uninsured Motorist: covers injuries caused by uninsured driver. Underinsured Motorist: covers gap when at-fault driver's limits are insufficient.
Covers damage from non-collision events: theft, vandalism, hail, flood, fire, glass breakage, animal collisions. Subject to deductible.
Covers damage from collision with another vehicle or object regardless of fault. Subject to deductible.
Covers business liability for bodily injury and property damage arising from operations, products, completed operations, and premises.
Occurrence: covers claims arising from events during the policy period, regardless of when the claim is filed. Claims-made: covers claims filed during the policy period.
Package policy for small to mid-sized businesses combining property and liability coverage. Simplified underwriting and lower cost than separate policies.
Provides medical benefits, disability income, death benefits, and rehabilitation for work-related injuries/illnesses. No-fault system. Exclusive remedy.
Part 2 of workers compensation policy. Covers employer liability for work-related injuries not covered by the no-fault workers comp system.
Covers business buildings, contents, and business income. Can be written on named-peril or open-peril basis.
Covers lost income and continuing expenses during the period of restoration after a covered property loss. Also called business interruption.
Covers property in transit or movable property (contractor's equipment, fine arts, computer equipment). Broader than standard property coverage.
Covers vehicles used for business purposes. Similar structure to personal auto but with commercial endorsements and higher limits.
Errors and Omissions coverage for professionals (doctors, lawyers, agents). Covers negligent acts, errors, or omissions in professional services.
Covers personal liability of corporate directors and officers for wrongful acts in their management capacity.
Provides excess liability coverage above underlying policies (auto, homeowners, CGL). Also provides broader coverage for some claims.
Coverage placed with non-admitted insurers when coverage is unavailable in the standard market. Requires diligent search of admitted market first.
Federal program providing flood insurance. Standard homeowners policies exclude flood. Maximum: $250,000 dwelling, $100,000 contents for residential.
Not covered by standard homeowners policies. Available as endorsement or standalone policy. Typically has high deductible (10-20% of coverage).
Three-party agreement guaranteeing performance. Principal (who is bonded), obligee (who is protected), surety (who guarantees). Not insurance.
Protects an employer against loss from employee dishonesty (theft, embezzlement, fraud).
Covers mechanical and electrical breakdown of equipment (boilers, HVAC, electrical systems). Also covers resulting business income loss.
Covers data breach expenses, network security liability, media liability, and business interruption from cyber events.
Determines how multiple policies respond to the same loss. Methods: pro rata (by coverage amount), excess (one policy pays first), primary (stated order).
Must be: large number of similar exposures, accidental and unintentional, determinable and measurable loss, not catastrophic, economically feasible premium.
The dominant cause of loss in an unbroken chain of events. Determines whether a loss is covered under the policy.
When two or more perils contribute to a loss simultaneously. Modern policies use anti-concurrent causation clauses to exclude certain perils.
Methods: replacement cost, actual cash value, agreed value, functional replacement cost. Policy specifies which method applies.
Flat dollar deductible, percentage deductible (% of coverage amount), split deductible, disappearing deductible, franchise deductible.
Scheduled endorsement covering specific high-value items (jewelry, art, collectibles) for their appraised value on an open-peril basis.
Coverage D of homeowners policy. Pays additional costs of living elsewhere if home is uninhabitable due to a covered loss.
Covers fair rental value if part of the insured premises is rented and becomes uninhabitable. Also covers ALE.
Legal doctrine requiring property owners to protect children from dangerous conditions (pools, trampolines) that may attract them.
Homeowners Coverage E. Covers bodily injury and property damage liability arising from personal activities. Typically $100,000-$300,000.
Coverage F. Pays medical expenses for persons injured on the insured's premises regardless of fault. Typically $1,000-$5,000 per person.
Combines two or more commercial coverage forms (property, liability, auto, crime) into a single policy for comprehensive business protection.
Premium modification based on the insured's own loss history compared to the expected losses for similar risks. Used in workers comp.
Insurance purchased by insurers to spread risk. Treaty reinsurance: automatic. Facultative: case-by-case. Quota share vs excess of loss.
Identify risks, analyze/evaluate risks, select risk management techniques (avoid, reduce, retain, transfer), implement, monitor.
Moral hazard: intentional dishonesty (arson for profit). Morale hazard: carelessness or indifference due to having insurance coverage.

tracks.name: Surplus Lines
tracks.title: Surplus Lines Insurance
tracks.subtitle: Surplus Lines Insurance Study Manual (Arizona) Comprehensive Study Guide Updated March 2026  |  OBBBA Integrated

chapters.title: Table of Contents
chapters.intro: Right-click → 'Update Field' in Word for clickable page numbers.

chapters.title: How to Use This Manual
chapters.intro:
Surplus lines insurance provides coverage for risks that the standard (admitted) insurance market is unable or unwilling to insure. These are typically unusual, high-risk, or hard-to-place risks such as special events, unique properties, high-hazard businesses, or emerging risks that lack sufficient actuarial data. Surplus lines insurers (also called non-admitted or excess and surplus lines insurers) are not licensed in the state where the risk is located but are permitted to write business through licensed surplus lines brokers.
This manual focuses on Arizona surplus lines requirements while covering the general principles applicable in most states. The surplus lines market plays a critical role in the insurance ecosystem by providing coverage that would otherwise be unavailable, ensuring that businesses and individuals can obtain necessary protection even for non-standard risks.
Chapter 1: Surplus Lines Fundamentals
The admitted market consists of insurance companies licensed by the state insurance department. Admitted insurers must comply with state rate and form filing requirements, participate in state guaranty funds, and meet state solvency requirements. The surplus lines (non-admitted) market consists of insurers not licensed in the state but permitted to write business through surplus lines brokers. Surplus lines insurers have greater flexibility in pricing and policy forms but do NOT participate in state guaranty funds, meaning policyholders have no guaranty fund protection if the surplus lines insurer becomes insolvent.
The diligent search requirement is the cornerstone of surplus lines regulation. Before placing business with a surplus lines insurer, the broker must demonstrate that the coverage was declined by the admitted market. This typically requires obtaining declinations from a specified number of admitted insurers (usually 3) or documenting that the type of coverage is not available in the admitted market. The purpose is to protect the admitted market and ensure that surplus lines are used only when necessary. Some states maintain export lists of risks that are automatically eligible for surplus lines placement without a diligent search.
Chapter 2: Eligible and Non-Eligible Surplus Lines
Eligible surplus lines insurers must meet minimum financial requirements established by the state. In most states, including Arizona, eligible surplus lines insurers must maintain minimum capital and surplus (typically $15 million or more), be listed on the state's approved list of eligible surplus lines insurers, and maintain a satisfactory financial rating. The NAIC International Insurers Department (IID) maintains a list of eligible alien (non-U.S.) surplus lines insurers that meet specified financial standards.
Certain types of insurance are typically NOT eligible for surplus lines placement, including workers' compensation (which must be written by admitted carriers or through the state fund), personal auto liability (which is mandatory and must be available through the admitted market or assigned risk plans), and other compulsory coverages required by state law. The Nonadmitted and Reinsurance Reform Act (NRRA), part of the Dodd-Frank Act (2010), established that surplus lines taxation is governed by the home state of the insured, simplifying multi-state surplus lines transactions.
Chapter 3: Arizona Surplus Lines Requirements
In Arizona, surplus lines brokers must hold a specific surplus lines license issued by the Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions (DIFI). The broker must conduct a diligent search of the admitted market before placing business with a surplus lines insurer. Arizona requires that the surplus lines insurer be on the state's list of eligible surplus lines insurers or meet specified financial requirements. The broker must provide the insured with a written disclosure that the surplus lines insurer is not admitted in Arizona and that the policy is not protected by the Arizona Property and Casualty Insurance Guaranty Fund.
Arizona surplus lines brokers must file an affidavit with DIFI for each surplus lines policy placed, documenting the diligent search, the declinations received, and the surplus lines insurer selected. The affidavit must be filed within a specified time period after policy placement. Brokers must maintain records of all surplus lines transactions for a minimum of 5 years. The broker is responsible for collecting and remitting the surplus lines premium tax to the state.
Chapter 4: Surplus Lines Taxation and Compliance
Arizona imposes a surplus lines premium tax of 3% on the gross premium for surplus lines policies. The broker is responsible for collecting the tax from the insured and remitting it to DIFI on a quarterly basis. The tax applies to the full premium, including any policy fees or surcharges. Failure to collect and remit the tax can result in penalties, fines, and license revocation. Under the NRRA, the home state of the insured collects 100% of the surplus lines tax, even for multi-state risks.
Compliance requirements for surplus lines brokers include: maintaining a valid surplus lines license, conducting diligent searches, filing required affidavits, collecting and remitting premium taxes, providing required disclosures to insureds, maintaining records, and complying with all applicable state laws and regulations. The Arizona Department of Insurance conducts periodic examinations of surplus lines brokers to ensure compliance. Violations can result in administrative actions including fines, license suspension, and license revocation.

chapters.title: Appendix: A.1 The Surplus Lines Market
chapters.intro:
The surplus lines (E&S) market provides insurance for risks the admitted market cannot or will not insure: unusual risks, high-hazard risks, hard-to-place risks, and emerging risks (cyber liability, cannabis, sharing economy). Surplus lines insurers are not subject to state rate and form filing requirements, allowing customized policies and individual pricing. However, surplus lines policies are NOT protected by the state guaranty fund, which must be disclosed to the insured.
In Arizona, surplus lines are regulated under ARS Title 20. The Arizona DIFI maintains a list of eligible surplus lines insurers. To be eligible, an insurer must meet minimum capital and surplus requirements, be licensed in its domiciliary state, and have a satisfactory financial rating. The surplus lines broker verifies eligibility before placing business.

subsections.title: A.2 Diligent Search and Broker Responsibilities
paragraphs: Before placing surplus lines business, the broker must conduct a diligent search of the admitted market: contact at least three admitted insurers, document declinations, and demonstrate coverage unavailability. Exempt commercial purchasers (net worth >$20M, revenue >$50M, or 500+ employees) may be exempt under the NRRA. The NRRA also established that surplus lines taxes are paid only to the insured's home state.

Broker responsibilities: verify insurer eligibility, conduct/document diligent search, collect/remit surplus lines taxes (Arizona: 3% + 0.3% fire marshal tax on property), file surplus lines affidavit, maintain records for 5+ years, and disclose non-admitted status. The broker acts as agent of the insured (not the insurer) and may be liable for placing coverage with an insolvent insurer if due diligence was lacking.

subsections.title: 11. Surplus lines policies are NOT protected by:
paragraphs: A) ReinsuranceB) The state guaranty fundC) E&O insuranceD) Federal deposit insurance
MASTERY CHECK   Answer: B Non-admitted insurers are not covered by the state guaranty fund. Must be disclosed.

subsections.title: 12. Arizona diligent search requires contacting:
paragraphs: A) 1 admitted insurerB) 2 admitted insurersC) 3 admitted insurersD) 5 admitted insurers
MASTERY CHECK   Answer: C At least 3 admitted insurers must be contacted with documented declinations.
Deep Dive: Surplus Lines Compliance and Market Dynamics

chapters.title: Appendix: Arizona Surplus Lines Regulatory Framework
chapters.intro:
Arizona's surplus lines regulatory framework is governed by ARS Title 20, Chapter 2, Article 5. The Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions (DIFI) maintains the list of eligible surplus lines insurers. To be eligible, a non-admitted insurer must: be authorized in its domiciliary state, maintain minimum capital and surplus of $15 million (or meet alternative financial requirements), have a satisfactory financial rating from a recognized rating agency, and file an annual statement with DIFI. The surplus lines broker must verify eligibility before each placement.
Arizona surplus lines brokers must hold a specific surplus lines license (in addition to any other insurance licenses). License requirements include: passing the surplus lines examination, completing pre-licensing education, maintaining errors and omissions (E&O) insurance, and filing a surety bond. The broker must maintain detailed records of all surplus lines transactions for at least 5 years, including: the insured's name and address, the coverage provided, the premium charged, the insurer's name and eligibility status, the diligent search documentation, and the surplus lines tax calculations.
The surplus lines tax in Arizona is 3% of gross premiums, plus an additional 0.3% fire marshal tax on property coverages. The broker is responsible for collecting the tax from the insured and remitting it to DIFI on a quarterly basis (due within 60 days after the end of each quarter). Failure to remit taxes timely can result in penalties, interest, and license revocation. The NRRA simplified multi-state surplus lines taxation by establishing that taxes are paid only to the insured's home state, eliminating the need to allocate premiums across multiple states.
13. Under the NRRA, surplus lines taxes are paid to: A) Each state where the risk is located B) The insurer's domiciliary state C) The insured's home state only D) The federal government. Answer: C. The NRRA established that surplus lines taxes are paid solely to the insured's home state, eliminating multi-state tax allocation.
14. An exempt commercial purchaser under the NRRA is a business with: A) Any commercial insurance need B) Net worth exceeding $20 million, revenue exceeding $50 million, or 500+ employees C) A surplus lines broker D) Coverage from an admitted insurer. Answer: B. Exempt commercial purchasers meet size thresholds ($20M net worth, $50M revenue, or 500+ employees) and may be exempt from diligent search requirements.
15. The Arizona surplus lines tax rate is: A) 2% B) 3% plus 0.3% fire marshal tax C) 5% D) 3.5%. Answer: B. Arizona charges 3% surplus lines tax plus an additional 0.3% fire marshal tax on property coverages, for a total of 3.3% on property risks.
The Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions (DIFI) maintains the list of eligible surplus lines insurers. To be eligible, a non-admitted insurer must: maintain minimum capital and surplus requirements (typically $15 million for domestic insurers, $45 million for alien insurers), be licensed in its domiciliary jurisdiction, have a satisfactory financial rating (typically A.M. Best rating of A- or better), and comply with Arizona reporting requirements. The surplus lines broker must verify insurer eligibility at the time of each placement — relying on a previous verification is insufficient and can result in regulatory action.
The surplus lines market continues to grow as emerging risks — including cyber liability, climate-related exposures, and autonomous vehicle liability — create demand for innovative coverage solutions that the admitted market cannot efficiently provide. Surplus lines brokers who develop expertise in these emerging areas will be well-positioned for career growth in this dynamic segment of the insurance industry.
Surplus lines brokers play a vital and indispensable role in the insurance marketplace.

subsections.title: Surplus Lines Market Cycles and Trends
paragraphs: The insurance market operates in cycles: hard markets (rising premiums, tighter underwriting, reduced capacity) and soft markets (falling premiums, relaxed underwriting, abundant capacity). During hard markets, the surplus lines market expands as more risks are declined by the admitted market and flow to E&S carriers. During soft markets, the surplus lines market contracts as admitted carriers compete aggressively for business. Understanding market cycles helps brokers anticipate capacity changes and advise clients on coverage availability and pricing trends.

Emerging risks driving surplus lines growth include: cyber liability (data breaches, ransomware, business interruption from cyber events), cannabis industry coverage (federally illegal but state-legal operations need property, liability, and product coverage), sharing economy risks (ride-sharing, home-sharing, peer-to-peer lending), autonomous vehicle liability, climate-related risks (wildfire, flood, hurricane in high-risk areas), and PFAS/environmental liability. These risks often lack sufficient actuarial data for admitted market pricing, making the surplus lines market's flexibility essential.
Deep Dive: Specialty Surplus Lines Coverages

subsections.title: Cyber Liability Insurance
paragraphs: Cyber liability insurance is one of the fastest-growing segments of the surplus lines market. First-party coverages include: data breach response costs (notification, credit monitoring, forensic investigation), business interruption from cyber events (lost income during system downtime), cyber extortion (ransomware payments and negotiation costs), data recovery (costs to restore damaged or destroyed data), and reputational harm (crisis management and public relations). Third-party coverages include: network security liability (claims from third parties whose data was compromised), privacy liability (regulatory fines and penalties for privacy violations), and media liability (claims arising from electronic content).

Cyber insurance underwriting evaluates: the applicant's industry and data sensitivity (healthcare and financial services face higher risk), security controls (firewalls, encryption, multi-factor authentication, employee training), incident response planning, data backup procedures, vendor management practices, and prior loss history. Premiums have increased significantly due to the surge in ransomware attacks and the increasing cost of data breaches. The average cost of a data breach in 2025 exceeded $4.5 million. Cyber insurance is predominantly written in the surplus lines market because the risk is evolving rapidly and lacks the actuarial history needed for admitted market pricing.

subsections.title: Environmental and Professional Liability
paragraphs: Environmental liability insurance covers claims arising from pollution conditions. Pollution Legal Liability (PLL) covers third-party claims for bodily injury and property damage from pollution emanating from the insured's site, as well as cleanup costs. Contractors Pollution Liability (CPL) covers pollution claims arising from the contractor's operations at job sites. These coverages are almost exclusively written in the surplus lines market because standard CGL policies contain absolute pollution exclusions. Environmental insurance is critical for real estate transactions (brownfield development), manufacturing operations, and waste management companies.

Professional liability (Errors and Omissions) insurance covers claims arising from the insured's professional services. Unlike CGL, which covers bodily injury and property damage, E&O covers financial losses caused by the professional's negligence, errors, or omissions. E&O is typically written on a claims-made basis with a retroactive date. The surplus lines market provides E&O coverage for professions that the admitted market finds difficult to underwrite: technology companies, consultants, real estate professionals, insurance agents, and emerging professions. Manuscript (custom) policies can be tailored to address the specific risks of each profession.
Deep Dive: Surplus Lines Compliance Best Practices

subsections.title: Documentation and Record-Keeping
paragraphs: Surplus lines brokers must maintain meticulous documentation to comply with state regulatory requirements. For each surplus lines transaction, the broker must maintain: the insured's name, address, and contact information; a description of the risk and coverage provided; the policy number, effective dates, and premium; the name and NAIC number of the surplus lines insurer; evidence of the insurer's eligibility (verified against the state's approved list); the diligent search documentation (names of admitted insurers contacted, dates of contact, and reasons for declination); the surplus lines tax calculation and proof of payment; and copies of all correspondence with the insured regarding the non-admitted status of the coverage.

Best practices for surplus lines compliance include: maintaining a compliance calendar with quarterly tax filing deadlines, conducting annual audits of surplus lines files for completeness, verifying insurer eligibility at the time of each placement (not relying on previous verifications), using standardized forms and checklists for diligent search documentation, training all staff on surplus lines regulatory requirements, and maintaining E&O insurance with adequate limits. The broker should also monitor the financial condition of surplus lines insurers throughout the policy period — if an insurer's financial condition deteriorates, the broker should consider moving the business to a more stable carrier at the next renewal.

Common compliance violations include: failure to conduct or document diligent search, placing business with ineligible insurers, failure to collect and remit surplus lines taxes timely, failure to disclose non-admitted status to the insured, failure to maintain required records for the statutory retention period, and unauthorized binding of coverage. Penalties for violations can include: fines, license suspension or revocation, personal liability for unpaid taxes, and E&O claims from insureds who suffer losses due to placement with insolvent or ineligible insurers.

subsections.title: NRRA and Multi-State Surplus Lines Taxation
paragraphs: The Nonadmitted and Reinsurance Reform Act (NRRA), part of the Dodd-Frank Act, simplified surplus lines regulation by establishing that: surplus lines taxes are paid solely to the insured's home state (eliminating multi-state tax allocation), the home state's regulatory requirements govern the placement (eliminating the need to comply with multiple states' diligent search and eligibility requirements), and the home state is defined as the state where the insured maintains its principal place of business (for commercial insureds) or principal residence (for individual insureds).

Before the NRRA, a surplus lines broker placing coverage for a multi-state risk had to: determine the premium allocation to each state, verify insurer eligibility in each state, comply with each state's diligent search requirements, file affidavits in each state, and remit taxes to each state. The NRRA eliminated this complexity. However, states have responded differently: some have joined the Surplus Lines Insurance Multi-State Compliance Compact (SLIMPACT) or the Non-admitted Insurance Multi-State Agreement (NIMA) to share tax revenue, while others have not joined any compact. Arizona participates in tax-sharing arrangements to ensure equitable distribution of surplus lines tax revenue among states where risks are located.

chapters.title: 2026 OBBBA Legislative Update
chapters.intro: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), signed July 4, 2025, made permanent many provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and introduced new provisions effective 2026. All figures below are verified for 2026.

chapters.title: Supplementary Scenarios and Exam Preparation
chapters.intro:
SCENARIO Cannabis Dispensary Placement Client: Cannabis dispensary in Arizona needs GL and property. 1. DILIGENT SEARCH: Contact 3 admitted insurers. All decline (federal illegality). 2. ELIGIBLE INSURER: Lloyd's syndicate on Arizona eligible list. 3. DISCLOSURE: Written notice — NOT covered by AZ guaranty fund. 4. TAX: Collect 3% Arizona surplus lines tax. 5. FILING: File with AZ stamping office. Why surplus? Cannabis is federally illegal → admitted insurers won't cover it.
COMMON TRAP Surplus Lines Traps "Surplus lines are regulated the same as admitted." → WRONG. Not rate-regulated, not form-regulated, NOT guaranty fund covered. "Any insurer can write surplus lines." → WRONG. Must appear on the state's eligible insurer list. "Diligent search is optional." → WRONG. Mandatory before placing with non-admitted carrier. "Multiple states can tax the same surplus lines policy." → WRONG. Under NRRA, only the insured's HOME STATE.
EXAM TIP NRRA Key Provisions The Nonadmitted and Reinsurance Reform Act (2010) simplified surplus lines: 1. Only the insured's HOME STATE regulates and taxes the transaction 2. No more allocating premium across multiple states 3. Home state determines insurer eligibility 4. Reinsurance credit based on collateral, not admission status

practice_questions.prompt: Surplus lines insurers are also known as:
practice_questions.explanation: Surplus lines insurers are non-admitted (not licensed in the state where the risk is located). They operate through licensed surplus lines brokers.

practice_questions.prompt: The diligent search requirement ensures:
options: Coverage was declined by the admitted market
The insured receives the best policy
The broker earns the highest commission
practice_questions.explanation: The diligent search demonstrates that admitted insurers declined the risk, justifying placement in the surplus lines market.

practice_questions.prompt: Arizona's surplus lines premium tax rate is:
practice_questions.explanation: Arizona imposes a 3% surplus lines premium tax on gross premiums, collected by the broker and remitted to DIFI quarterly.

practice_questions.prompt: Surplus lines policies are NOT protected by:
practice_questions.explanation: Non-admitted insurers are not covered by the state guaranty fund. Must be disclosed.

practice_questions.prompt: Arizona diligent search requires contacting:
practice_questions.explanation: At least 3 admitted insurers must be contacted with documented declinations.

flashcards.definition: Insurance placed with non-admitted (unlicensed) insurers when coverage is unavailable in the admitted market. Also called excess and surplus lines.
An insurer not licensed in the state where the risk is located. Not subject to state rate and form regulations. Not covered by state guaranty funds.
Insurers licensed by the state, subject to rate and form regulation, and backed by the state guaranty fund.
Required process of attempting to place coverage with admitted insurers before placing with surplus lines. Must document declinations.
Licensed individual authorized to place insurance with non-admitted carriers. Must be separately licensed from standard insurance agents.
List maintained by some states of coverages that do not require a diligent search before surplus lines placement.
Federal law (part of Dodd-Frank) that established that only the insured's home state can regulate and tax surplus lines transactions.
Under NRRA, the state where the insured's principal place of business is located or, for individuals, the state of principal residence.
Tax imposed on surplus lines premiums. Rate varies by state (typically 3-5%). Collected by the surplus lines broker and remitted to the state.
A marketplace (not a company) where syndicates of underwriters accept insurance risk. Considered an eligible surplus lines insurer in all 50 states.
An insurer domiciled outside the United States. Must meet financial requirements to be eligible for surplus lines placement.
Alien surplus lines insurers must maintain a trust fund in the US (typically $5.4M minimum) as security for US policyholders.
A non-admitted insurer that meets the state's financial and regulatory requirements to write surplus lines business.
Surplus Lines Insurance Multi-State Compliance Compact. Interstate agreement streamlining surplus lines tax allocation and compliance.
Maintains the Quarterly Listing of Alien Insurers eligible to write surplus lines in the US.
Certain large commercial insureds (industrial insureds) may be exempt from diligent search and other surplus lines requirements due to their sophistication.
States typically require surplus lines insurers to maintain minimum capital and surplus (often $15-45M) and file annual financial statements.
Entity in some states that reviews and stamps surplus lines filings to verify compliance with placement requirements and facilitate tax collection.
Surplus lines policies are NOT covered by state insurance guaranty funds. If the non-admitted insurer becomes insolvent, the policyholder may suffer loss.
Surplus lines broker must: verify insurer eligibility, conduct diligent search, collect and remit premium tax, maintain records, notify insured of non-admitted status.
Surplus lines broker must provide written notice that coverage is placed with a non-admitted insurer not covered by the state guaranty fund.
Non-admitted insurers are not subject to state rate and form filing requirements. They can offer customized coverage and pricing.
Period of tight underwriting, higher premiums, and reduced capacity in the admitted market. Increases demand for surplus lines placement.
Period of competitive pricing, broad coverage, and abundant capacity in the admitted market. Reduces surplus lines activity.

flashcards.term: Types of Risks in Surplus Lines
flashcards.definition: Unique, unusual, or high-hazard risks. Includes entertainment, environmental, product liability, professional liability, and emerging risks like cyber.
An intermediary authorized by an insurer to underwrite, bind coverage, and handle claims. Often used by surplus lines insurers.
Traditional surplus lines coverage for ocean cargo, hull, and liability. One of the oldest forms of surplus lines insurance.
Written refusal from an admitted insurer to provide coverage. Documentation of declinations is required for the diligent search.
Arizona requires a separate surplus lines license, diligent search with 3 declinations, and surplus lines premium tax of 3%.
Some states require a resident licensed surplus lines broker to countersign policies placed by non-resident brokers.
Surplus lines brokers must maintain records of placements, diligent search documentation, and premium tax filings for a state-specified period (typically 5 years).
Another term for surplus lines. Coverage that exceeds what the admitted market will write, placed with non-admitted insurers.
Surplus lines often covers specialty risks: cannabis, drones, gig economy, sharing economy, cyber, and emerging technology.
Without guaranty fund coverage, policyholder protection depends on the financial strength of the surplus lines insurer. AM Best ratings are critical.
Key advantage of surplus lines: underwriters can customize coverage terms, conditions, and pricing without state filing requirements.

reference.tax_reference_markdown:
# Verified Tax Numbers for 2025 and 2026

## Source: IRS Notice 2025-67 (IR-2025-111, Nov 13, 2025)

### Retirement Plan Contribution Limits

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| 401(k)/403(b)/457(b)/TSP deferral limit | $23,500 | $24,500 |
| 401(k) catch-up (age 50-59, 64+) | $7,500 | $8,000 |
| 401(k) super catch-up (age 60-63, SECURE 2.0) | $11,250 | $11,250 |
| Total 401(k) with catch-up (50-59, 64+) | $31,000 | $32,500 |
| Total 401(k) with super catch-up (60-63) | $34,750 | $35,750 |
| IRA contribution limit | $7,000 | $7,500 |
| IRA catch-up (age 50+) | $1,000 | $1,100 |
| SIMPLE IRA deferral | $16,500 | $17,000 |
| SIMPLE IRA (certain applicable plans) | $17,600 | $18,100 |
| SIMPLE catch-up (age 50+) | $3,500 | $4,000 |
| SIMPLE catch-up (certain applicable, 50+) | $3,850 | $3,850 |
| SIMPLE super catch-up (age 60-63) | $5,250 | $5,250 |
| Defined contribution limit (415(c)) | $70,000 | $72,000 |
| SEP contribution limit | $70,000 | $72,000 |
| Annual compensation limit (401(a)(17)) | $350,000 | $360,000 |
| Roth catch-up wage threshold (SECURE 2.0) | $145,000 | $150,000 |
| Highly compensated employee threshold | $160,000 | $160,000 |

**SECURE 2.0 Note (effective 2026):** Participants with prior-year FICA wages exceeding $150,000 must make catch-up contributions as designated Roth contributions.

### IRA Phase-Out Ranges (Traditional IRA Deduction)

| Filing Status | 2025 | 2026 |
|--------------|------|------|
| Single (covered by workplace plan) | $79,000-$89,000 | $81,000-$91,000 |
| MFJ (contributing spouse covered) | $126,000-$146,000 | $129,000-$149,000 |
| MFJ (contributor NOT covered, spouse IS) | $236,000-$246,000 | $242,000-$252,000 |
| MFS (covered by workplace plan) | $0-$10,000 | $0-$10,000 |

### Roth IRA Income Phase-Out Ranges

| Filing Status | 2025 | 2026 |
|--------------|------|------|
| Single/HOH | $150,000-$165,000 | $153,000-$168,000 |
| MFJ | $236,000-$246,000 | $242,000-$252,000 |
| MFS | $0-$10,000 | $0-$10,000 |

### Saver's Credit Income Limits

| Filing Status | 2025 | 2026 |
|--------------|------|------|
| MFJ | $79,000 | $80,500 |
| HOH | $59,250 | $60,375 |
| Single/MFS | $39,500 | $40,250 |

### HSA Contribution Limits

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| HSA self-only | $4,300 | $4,400 |
| HSA family | $8,550 | $8,750 |
| HSA catch-up (age 55+) | $1,000 | $1,000 |
| HDHP minimum deductible (self-only) | $1,650 | $1,700 |
| HDHP minimum deductible (family) | $3,300 | $3,400 |
| HDHP max out-of-pocket (self-only) | $8,300 | $8,500 |
| HDHP max out-of-pocket (family) | $16,600 | $17,000 |

### Gift and Estate Tax

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| Annual gift tax exclusion | $19,000 | $19,000 |
| Gift to non-citizen spouse | $190,000 | $194,000 |
| Estate/gift tax unified credit (OBBBA) | $13,990,000 | $15,000,000 |
| Top estate/gift/GST tax rate | 40% | 40% |

Note: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA, signed July 4, 2025) made the TCJA estate tax exemption PERMANENT and raised it to $15 million per person ($30 million per married couple with portability) beginning in 2026, indexed for inflation from 2027 onward. Step-up in basis at death is preserved. No changes to marginal estate tax rates, valuation rules, or trust taxation.

### Standard Deduction (Source: IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-32, with OBBBA amendments)

| Filing Status | 2025 (post-OBBBA) | 2026 |
|--------------|------|------|
| Single/MFS | $15,750 | $16,100 |
| MFJ | $31,500 | $32,200 |
| HOH | $23,625 | $24,150 |
| Additional (65+/blind, single/HOH) | $2,000 | $2,050 |
| Additional (65+/blind, MFJ per person) | $1,600 | $1,650 |

**OBBBA Note:** The standard deduction was permanently extended and increased above pre-OBBBA levels. OBBBA retroactively raised 2025 amounts (from $15,000 single / $30,000 MFJ to $15,750 / $31,500). Personal exemptions remain permanently at $0.

### OBBBA Senior Deduction (TEMPORARY: 2025-2028 only)

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Deduction per qualifying taxpayer (age 65+) | $6,000 |
| MFJ (both spouses 65+) | $12,000 |
| Phase-out threshold (Single/HOH) | $75,000 MAGI |
| Phase-out threshold (MFJ) | $150,000 MAGI |
| Phase-out rate | 6% of MAGI exceeding threshold |
| Full phase-out (Single) | $175,000 |
| Full phase-out (MFJ) | $350,000 |
| Availability | Both itemizers and standard deduction filers |
| Ineligible status | Married Filing Separately |

**Critical:** This deduction is TEMPORARY (2025-2028) and is separate from both the standard deduction and the existing additional standard deduction for age 65+. It does not require receipt of Social Security benefits.

### SALT Deduction (OBBBA — new for 2025)

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| SALT deduction cap | $40,000 | $40,400 |
| Phase-down threshold (MAGI) | $500,000 | $500,500 |
| Phase-down rate | 30% of MAGI excess | 30% of MAGI excess |
| SALT floor (cannot reduce below) | $10,000 | $10,000 |
| Expiration | End of 2029 | — |

**Note:** OBBBA raised the SALT cap from $10,000 (TCJA) to $40,000 for 2025, indexed thereafter. High-income taxpayers face a phase-down that can reduce the cap to $10,000.

### Social Security (Source: SSA COLA Fact Sheet 2026)

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| COLA increase | 2.5% | 2.8% |
| SS wage base (OASDI) | $176,100 | $184,500 |
| Medicare wage base | No limit | No limit |
| Additional Medicare tax (>$200K single / >$250K MFJ) | 0.9% | 0.9% |
| Earnings test (under FRA) | $23,400/yr ($1,950/mo) | $24,480/yr ($2,040/mo) |
| Earnings test (year of FRA) | $62,160/yr ($5,180/mo) | $65,160/yr ($5,430/mo) |
| Max SS benefit at FRA | $4,018/mo | $4,152/mo |
| Quarter of coverage | $1,810 | $1,890 |
| SGA (non-blind) | $1,620/mo | $1,690/mo |
| SGA (blind) | $2,700/mo | $2,830/mo |

### 2026 Federal Income Tax Brackets (Source: IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-32)

| Rate | Single | MFJ | HOH |
|------|--------|-----|-----|
| 10% | $0-$12,400 | $0-$24,800 | $0-$17,700 |
| 12% | $12,401-$50,400 | $24,801-$100,800 | $17,701-$67,450 |
| 22% | $50,401-$105,700 | $100,801-$211,400 | $67,451-$105,700 |
| 24% | $105,701-$201,775 | $211,401-$403,550 | $105,701-$201,775 |
| 32% | $201,776-$256,225 | $403,551-$512,450 | $201,776-$256,200 |
| 35% | $256,226-$640,600 | $512,451-$768,700 | $256,201-$640,600 |
| 37% | $640,601+ | $768,701+ | $640,601+ |

**OBBBA Note:** The 37% top rate is now permanent. OBBBA provided an additional ~4% inflation adjustment for the bottom two brackets (10% and 12%) versus ~2.3% for higher brackets, creating slightly more favorable treatment for lower-income ranges.

### 2026 Capital Gains Tax Brackets

| Rate | Single | MFJ | HOH |
|------|--------|-----|-----|
| 0% | $0-$49,450 | $0-$98,900 | $0-$66,200 |
| 15% | $49,451-$545,500 | $98,901-$613,700 | $66,201-$579,600 |
| 20% | $545,501+ | $613,701+ | $579,601+ |

Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) of 3.8% applies on lesser of net investment income or MAGI exceeding $200,000 (single) / $250,000 (MFJ). These thresholds are NOT inflation-indexed.

### AMT Exemptions 2026

| Filing Status | Exemption | Phase-out Begins | 28% Rate Applies Above |
|--------------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Single | $90,100 | $500,000 | $244,500 |
| MFJ | $140,200 | $1,000,000 | $244,500 |

**OBBBA Note:** AMT exemptions made permanent. Phase-out thresholds were reduced from 2025 levels ($625,350 single / $1,252,700 MFJ) back to approximately 2018 levels, and the phase-out rate increased from 25% to 50%. This represents a slight tax increase for some high-income filers.

### QBI Deduction (Section 199A) — 2026

| Filing Status | Phase-out Begins | Phase-out Ends |
|--------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Single | $201,775 | $276,775 |
| MFJ | $403,500 | $553,500 |

**OBBBA Note:** QBI deduction made permanent. Phase-out range expanded from $50,000 to $75,000 (single) and from $100,000 to $150,000 (MFJ). New $400 minimum deduction added for taxpayers below phase-out threshold.

### Medicare 2026 (Source: CMS Fact Sheet, Nov 14, 2025)

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| Part B standard monthly premium | $185.00 | $202.90 |
| Part B annual deductible | $257 | $283 |
| Part A inpatient deductible | $1,676 | $1,736 |
| Part A daily coinsurance (days 61-90) | $419 | $434 |
| Part A lifetime reserve day coinsurance | $838 | $868 |
| SNF daily coinsurance (days 21-100) | $209.50 | $217.00 |

### Medicare IRMAA Brackets 2026 (Single Filers)

| Income (MAGI) | Part B Premium | Part D Surcharge |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|
| <= $109,000 | $202.90 | $0.00 |
| $109,001-$137,000 | $284.10 | $14.50 |
| $137,001-$171,000 | $405.80 | $37.50 |
| $171,001-$205,000 | $527.50 | $60.40 |
| $205,001-$499,999 | $649.20 | $83.30 |
| >= $500,000 | $689.90 | $91.00 |

For MFJ, double the income thresholds.

### EITC 2026

| Children | Max Credit | Phaseout Ends (Single) | Phaseout Ends (MFJ) |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 0 | $664 | $19,540 | $26,820 |
| 1 | $4,427 | $51,593 | $58,863 |
| 2 | $7,316 | $58,629 | $65,899 |
| 3+ | $8,231 | $62,974 | $70,224 |

### Child Tax Credit 2026
- Maximum CTC: $2,200 per qualifying child (OBBBA made permanent, inflation-adjusted)
- Refundable portion: $1,700
- Phase-out begins: $200,000 (single) / $400,000 (MFJ)

### Key Legislative Changes (OBBBA — One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, 2025)

**Permanent provisions:**
- Made TCJA individual tax provisions PERMANENT (37% top rate, expanded brackets, higher standard deduction)
- Estate tax exemption set at $15M per person (permanent, inflation-adjusted from 2027)
- QBI deduction made permanent with expanded phase-out range and $400 minimum
- CTC increased to $2,200 (permanent, inflation-adjusted)
- Personal exemption remains $0 (permanent)
- AMT higher exemptions made permanent (but phase-out thresholds reduced)
- Standard deduction boost made permanent (retroactive to 2025)
- Itemized deduction limitation (Pease) remains eliminated

**Temporary provisions (2025-2028):**
- $6,000 senior deduction for age 65+ (phases out at $75K/$150K MAGI)
- Tax-free tip income deduction (up to $25,000)
- Tax-free overtime income deduction

**Temporary provisions (2025-2029):**
- SALT deduction cap increased to $40,000 (from $10,000), with phase-down for high earners

**Other notable changes:**
- QSBS (Section 1202): Holding period shortened from 5 to 3 years, exclusion increased from $10M to $15M, gross asset limit raised from $50M to $75M (effective 2026)
- Employer-provided childcare credit increased from $150,000 to $500,000 ($600,000 for eligible small businesses)
