sections.title: How to Use This Manual
paragraphs: This manual is a standalone, comprehensive resource for mastering the complete Executive MBA curriculum while simultaneously applying every concept to financial services practice. No external platform, software, or supplementary material is required.
VISUAL   Three Modes of Use

KEY INSIGHT  Active Learning Strategy Research shows active engagement produces 3x better retention than passive reading. For each section: (1) Read the concept. (2) Cover the definition and try to state it yourself. (3) Work through the example before reading the solution. (4) Read the Financial Services Application and identify how it applies to your current role. (5) Complete the Mastery Check at the end of each chapter.
Chapter Map
ACCOUNTING Debits & Credits  |  Revenues & Expenses  |  Stocks & Bonds  |  Financial Statements  |  Ratios  |  Managerial Accounting  |  Excel

sections.title: Accounting
paragraphs: Accounting is the language of business. Every decision — from hiring an employee to acquiring a company — ultimately shows up in the financial statements. This chapter takes you from the fundamental accounting equation through financial statement analysis, ratio interpretation, and managerial decision-making tools like CVP analysis and activity-based costing.
VISUAL   The Accounting Cycle
VISUAL   Income Statement Waterfall
VISUAL   Balance Sheet Structure
Debits & Credits

SCENARIO  Opening Day at a New Business Sarah opens a consulting firm by investing $50,000 of personal savings and taking a $30,000 bank loan. She buys $20,000 of equipment and keeps $60,000 in cash. Balance sheet: Assets = $60,000 cash + $20,000 equipment = $80,000. Liabilities = $30,000 loan. Equity = $50,000 investment. Check: $80,000 = $30,000 + $50,000. Every transaction she makes from here must keep this equation balanced.

FORMULA   Accounting Equation Assets = Liabilities + Equity Assets: owned items. Liabilities: debts owed. Equity: owner investment + retained earnings. Worked Example: Assets $500K, Liabilities $300K. Equity = $200K. Borrow $100K: Assets $600K = Liabilities $400K + Equity $200K. Common Mistake: Forgetting that EVERY transaction requires two entries to keep this equation balanced.
Revenues & Expenses

SCENARIO  Accrual vs. Cash: Why It Matters A law firm completes a $50,000 case in December but the client pays in January. Under accrual accounting: $50,000 revenue in December (when earned). Under cash accounting: $50,000 revenue in January (when received). The December income statement looks completely different under each method. Most businesses use accrual because it better matches revenues with the expenses incurred to generate them.

FORMULA   Net Income Net Income = Total Revenues - Total Expenses Revenue: income from operations. Expenses: all costs of generating that revenue. Worked Example: Revenue $1M, COGS $400K, SG&A $200K, Interest $50K, Tax $87.5K. NI = $262,500. Common Mistake: Confusing cash received with revenue earned. Accrual accounting recognizes revenue when EARNED.
Stocks & Bonds

SCENARIO  FIFO vs. LIFO in a Rising Market A retailer bought 100 widgets at $10, then 100 more at $15. They sold 100 widgets. Under FIFO (first-in, first-out): COGS = $1,000 (sold the $10 units), ending inventory = $1,500. Higher profit, higher taxes. Under LIFO (last-in, first-out): COGS = $1,500 (sold the $15 units), ending inventory = $1,000. Lower profit, lower taxes. Same economic reality, different reported results. This is why understanding inventory methods matters for analysis.

FORMULA   Breakeven Point Breakeven Units = Fixed Costs / (Price - Variable Cost per Unit) Fixed costs: don't change with volume. Price - VC = Contribution Margin per unit. Worked Example: Fixed costs $100K. Price $50. VC $30. CM = $20. Breakeven = 5,000 units. Common Mistake: Assuming fixed costs stay fixed at all volumes. Beyond the relevant range, a second facility may be needed.
Financial Statements

FORMULA   Return on Equity ROE = Net Income / Total Equity Measures return generated on shareholder investment. Worked Example: NI $500K, Equity $2M = 25% ROE. Compare to industry average ~15%: this company is generating strong returns. Common Mistake: Comparing ROE across industries without context. Capital-intensive industries naturally have lower ROE.
Ratios

FORMULA   Current Ratio Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities Above 1.0 generally healthy. Below 1.0 = potential liquidity risk. Worked Example: CA $800K, CL $500K = 1.6. Company has $1.60 for every $1.00 due within a year. Common Mistake: Assuming higher is always better. Ratio of 5.0 may mean too much idle cash not being invested.
Managerial Accounting

FORMULA   Debt-to-Equity D/E = Total Liabilities / Total Equity Higher = more leveraged. Industry norms vary widely. Worked Example: Liabilities $3M, Equity $2M = 1.5. Moderate leverage for manufacturing; high for services. Common Mistake: Ignoring industry context. Banks operate at D/E of 10:1+ because their business model requires it.
Excel
Excel
Excel
Excel
Excel

CASE STUDY  CVP Analysis for a Product Launch Decision A company considers launching a new product. Price: $120. Variable cost: $75. Additional fixed costs: $450,000/year. Contribution margin = $45/unit. Breakeven = 10,000 units. Market research estimates demand of 15,000 units. Projected profit: 5,000 x $45 = $225,000. But if demand is only 8,000? Loss: 2,000 x $45 = -$90,000. The CVP analysis shows management needs confidence that demand exceeds 10,000 before greenlighting. Sensitivity: what price increase would be needed to break even at 8,000 units? ($450,000/8,000) + $75 = $131.25 — an 9.4% price increase.
Connections to Other Disciplines
KEY INSIGHT  Finance Financial statements are the inputs to valuation models. Every DCF analysis starts with the income statement and cash flow statement you learned to build here.
KEY INSIGHT  Strategy Ratio analysis is used for competitive benchmarking. Comparing your ratios to competitors reveals strategic strengths and weaknesses.
KEY INSIGHT  Marketing & Pricing CVP analysis from managerial accounting directly informs pricing decisions. Your breakeven point determines the minimum viable price.
Financial Services Applications
[DATA AS OF: March 2026. Verify current rates before client-facing use.]

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Insurance Carrier Financial Analysis Statutory Accounting Principles (SAP) used by state regulators are more conservative than GAAP — assets valued lower, liabilities recognized earlier. When evaluating a carrier like National Life Group (AM Best A+, $57.4B AUM), read the statutory annual statement for surplus position, Risk-Based Capital (RBC) ratio (above 300% = strong), loss ratios, and investment portfolio composition. Understanding carrier financials lets you confidently recommend products backed by strong balance sheets.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Commission and Override Accounting First-year commissions on IUL products typically run 80-115% of target premium. Renewal commissions of 2-5% in years 2-10. As a manager, override income on team production represents 10-25% of their first-year commissions. Revenue recognition: commission income is earned when the policy is issued and first premium paid — not when the application is submitted. This distinction affects cash flow planning significantly.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Tax Treatment of Insurance Products IRC Section 7702 defines life insurance for tax purposes. Benefits: tax-free death benefits (Section 101(a)), tax-deferred cash value growth, tax-free policy loans (if not a Modified Endowment Contract). The MEC test (7-pay test) limits how much premium can be paid in the first 7 years. 1035 exchanges allow tax-free transfers between qualifying policies. Understanding these rules is essential for designing client solutions and explaining policy benefits accurately.

MASTERY CHECK  Accounting Mastery Check Can you: (1) Define the 10 most important terms in this chapter without looking? (2) Apply each major framework to a business scenario you haven't seen before? (3) Work through the key formulas with different numbers? (4) Explain how Accounting concepts connect to at least 2 other disciplines in this manual? (5) Describe how these concepts apply in financial services practice? If yes on all five, you have mastery.
Next: Markets & Economies
MARKETS & ECONOMIES Supply & Demand  |  Economic Worldview  |  Markets & Externalities  |  Economic Fluctuations  |  Fiscal & Monetary Policy  |  International Trade

sections.title: Markets & Economies
paragraphs: Economics provides the framework for understanding how markets work, why prices change, what drives economic growth, and how government policy shapes the business environment. This chapter covers microeconomics (individual markets and decision-making) and macroeconomics (the economy as a whole, including growth, inflation, unemployment, and policy).
VISUAL   SWOT Analysis Framework
VISUAL   Supply & Demand Equilibrium
Supply & Demand

SCENARIO  How a Supply Shock Cascades Through Markets A drought destroys 30% of Brazil's coffee crop. Supply curve shifts left (less available at every price). At the old price, shortage emerges. Price rises to new equilibrium — higher price, lower quantity. Coffee shops face higher input costs. Some raise prices (passing cost to consumers). Others absorb it (lower margins). Some switch to tea offerings (substitution). This is exactly what happened in 2021 when frost damage caused a 50% price spike. Understanding supply/demand shifts lets you anticipate price movements in any market.

FORMULA   Price Elasticity of Demand Ed = (% Change in Quantity Demanded) / (% Change in Price) |Ed| > 1 = elastic. |Ed| < 1 = inelastic. |Ed| = 1 = unit elastic. Worked Example: Gym raises price 20%. Members drop 15%. Ed = -0.75 (inelastic). Revenue increases with price increase because quantity drops proportionally less. Common Mistake: Forgetting the revenue implication: elastic goods — price increase DECREASES revenue. Inelastic goods — price increase INCREASES revenue.
Economic Worldview

SCENARIO  Why Insurance Is Inelastic Price elasticity for term life insurance is approximately -0.3 (inelastic). A 10% premium increase causes only a 3% drop in policies sold. Why? Because insurance is a necessity for people with dependents, and switching costs are high (new underwriting, potential health changes). This means carriers can raise premiums with relatively small customer losses. For advisors, it means clients are more sensitive to perceived VALUE than to price — focus conversations on coverage adequacy, not premium shopping.

FORMULA   GDP GDP = C + I + G + (X - M) C: consumer spending. I: business investment. G: government spending. X: exports. M: imports. Worked Example: US GDP ~$28 trillion. Consumer spending is ~68% ($19T). Government ~17%. Investment ~18%. Net exports slightly negative (trade deficit). Common Mistake: Confusing GDP growth with wellbeing. GDP measures economic output, not distribution, sustainability, or quality of life.
Markets & Externalities
Economic Fluctuations
Fiscal & Monetary Policy
International Trade
International Trade
International Trade
International Trade
International Trade
International Trade
International Trade

CASE STUDY  Adverse Selection in Health Insurance The individual health insurance market exhibits classic adverse selection: people likely to need healthcare are more likely to buy coverage. Healthy people opt out, making the insured pool sicker, driving premiums higher, causing more healthy people to exit — a death spiral. The ACA addressed this with coverage mandates (forcing healthy people in) and premium subsidies. In life insurance, adverse selection is managed through underwriting: medical exams, health questionnaires, and risk classification ensure pricing reflects actual risk. Understanding market failures isn't just theory — it's the foundation of insurance product design.
Connections to Other Disciplines
KEY INSIGHT  Finance Interest rates drive the cost of capital. When the Fed changes rates, WACC changes for every company — affecting investment decisions you'll model in Finance.
KEY INSIGHT  Marketing & Pricing Price elasticity from economics directly informs pricing strategy. Inelastic demand = pricing power. Elastic demand = compete on value, not price.
KEY INSIGHT  Strategy PESTEL analysis (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal) uses macroeconomic understanding to assess the external strategic environment.
Financial Services Applications
[DATA AS OF: March 2026. Verify current rates before client-facing use.]

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Interest Rate Impact on Insurance Products The Federal Reserve's rate decisions cascade through financial services: Fed funds rate (3.50-3.75% as of March 2026) affects Treasury yields, which affect insurance company general account investment returns, which affect IUL cap rates and FIA crediting rates. When rates rise, new IUL policies generally offer better cap rates (good for sales). When rates fall, existing policies may underperform illustrations. Understanding this chain lets you time product recommendations and set realistic client expectations.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Arizona Market Demographics Pima County (Tucson metro, ~1M population, University of Arizona anchor, significant retiree market). Mohave County (Lake Havasu/Kingman, ~220K, retirement and second-home market). Santa Cruz County (~47K, border economy, underserved for financial services). The $84 trillion great wealth transfer over 20 years creates sustained demand. With the estate tax exemption permanently at $15M/individual (OBBBA, July 2025), planning focus shifts from tax avoidance to income tax optimization, asset protection, and legacy planning.

MASTERY CHECK  Markets & Economies Mastery Check Can you: (1) Define the 10 most important terms in this chapter without looking? (2) Apply each major framework to a business scenario you haven't seen before? (3) Work through the key formulas with different numbers? (4) Explain how Markets & Economies concepts connect to at least 2 other disciplines in this manual? (5) Describe how these concepts apply in financial services practice? If yes on all five, you have mastery.

sections.title: Finance
paragraphs: Finance answers the fundamental question: how do we make decisions about money across time? From evaluating whether a project is worth investing in, to determining how a company should fund itself, to valuing an entire business — finance provides the quantitative tools for value-based decision-making. The time value of money is the foundation: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow.
VISUAL   Capital Budgeting Decision Process
VISUAL   Capital Structure Spectrum
Time Value of Money

SCENARIO  The Power of Compounding Invest $10,000 at 8% annually. After 10 years: $21,589. After 20 years: $46,610. After 30 years: $100,627. Your original $10,000 grew 10x — but $90,627 of that growth came from returns on returns (compound interest), not your original investment. At 12%, the 30-year value jumps to $299,599. The 4% difference in return rate produces a 3x difference in outcome over 30 years. This is why even small differences in investment returns matter enormously — and why financial planning starts as early as possible.

FORMULA   Future Value FV = PV x (1 + r)^n PV: present value. r: rate per period. n: number of periods. Worked Example: $10,000 at 8% for 10 years: FV = $10,000 x 1.08^10 = $21,589. Common Mistake: Not matching rate to compounding period. Annual 8% with monthly compounding: use r=0.08/12, n=120.
Financial Management

SCENARIO  NPV vs. IRR: When They Disagree Project A costs $50K, returns $80K in year 1. IRR = 60%, NPV at 10% = $22.7K. Project B costs $500K, returns $700K in year 1. IRR = 40%, NPV at 10% = $136.4K. IRR says choose A (60% > 40%). NPV says choose B ($136.4K > $22.7K). Who is right? NPV. Project B creates 6x more value despite a lower percentage return. IRR measures efficiency; NPV measures value creation. When they conflict (different project sizes, different timing), ALWAYS follow NPV.

FORMULA   Present Value PV = FV / (1 + r)^n FV: future amount. r: discount rate. n: periods. Worked Example: $50,000 in 5 years at 10%: PV = $50,000/1.10^5 = $31,046. Worth paying up to $31,046 today. Common Mistake: Using wrong discount rate. Rate must reflect risk: government bond = risk-free; startup = much higher.
Capital Structure

FORMULA   Net Present Value NPV = Sum[CF_t / (1+r)^t] - Initial Investment CF_t: cash flow at time t. r: discount rate. Positive NPV = accept. Worked Example: Invest $100K. Returns $35K/yr for 4 years at 10%. NPV = $31.8K + $28.9K + $26.3K + $23.9K - $100K = $10.9K. Accept. Common Mistake: When NPV and IRR disagree (mutually exclusive projects), ALWAYS follow NPV.
Modigliani-Miller

FORMULA   WACC WACC = (E/V x Ke) + (D/V x Kd x (1-T)) E: equity. D: debt. V: E+D. Ke: cost of equity. Kd: cost of debt. T: tax rate. Worked Example: E=$60M, D=$40M, Ke=12%, Kd=6%, T=30%. WACC = (0.6x12%) + (0.4x6%x0.7) = 7.2% + 1.68% = 8.88%. Common Mistake: Forgetting to use AFTER-TAX cost of debt. Interest is tax-deductible, so 6% debt at 30% tax = 4.2% true cost.
Capital Budgeting

FORMULA   CAPM (Cost of Equity) Ke = Rf + Beta x (Rm - Rf) Rf: risk-free rate. Beta: stock volatility vs. market. Rm-Rf: market risk premium. Worked Example: Rf=3%, Beta=1.2, Market premium=7%. Ke = 3% + 1.2x7% = 11.4%. Common Mistake: Using historical beta for a company whose risk profile has changed significantly.
Value Creation

CASE STUDY  Regional Expansion Capital Budgeting A financial services firm evaluates two growth strategies. Option A: Hire 5 advisors at $30K each ($150K total), expecting $400K first-year production generating $80K override income, growing 15% annually. Option B: Invest $150K in digital marketing, expecting $200K leads generating $60K revenue year 1, growing 25% annually. At 12% discount rate, 5-year NPV of A = $127K, NPV of B = $148K. Despite A having higher first-year returns, B creates more total value due to higher growth rate. This demonstrates why NPV — not gut feel or first-year returns — should drive capital allocation.
Connections to Other Disciplines
KEY INSIGHT  Accounting Financial statements from Accounting are the inputs to every valuation. DCF starts with the cash flow statement; multiples analysis uses income statement data.
KEY INSIGHT  Strategy Capital budgeting is how strategy gets funded. Porter's frameworks identify WHICH opportunities to pursue; NPV determines WHETHER they create value.
KEY INSIGHT  Data & Decisions Probability distributions from Data & Decisions power scenario analysis in capital budgeting. Monte Carlo simulation uses probability to model investment uncertainty.
Financial Services Applications
[DATA AS OF: March 2026. Verify current rates before client-facing use.]

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Premium Financing NPV Analysis Client needs $5M death benefit. Annual premium $100K for 10 years. Loan rate: SOFR (3.65%) + 250bps = 6.15%. Policy crediting rate: 7.5% (1.35% spread). Year 1: loan balance $106,150, cash value ~$85,000. Year 5: loan ~$585K, cash value ~$475K. Year 10: loan ~$1.06M, cash value ~$1.15M (crossover). At death: $5M benefit minus loan payoff = net benefit. Stress test: at 5.5% crediting, crossover extends to year 15+. This is why running multiple scenarios is non-negotiable in premium financing.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Agent Recruiting as Capital Budgeting Cost per recruit: $15K-30K (licensing, training, marketing support, time). Expected outcome: 35% probability agent reaches $100K production in 18 months. At 20% override: $20K annual return on $25K investment = 80% ROI if successful. Expected value: 35% x $20K = $7K expected return on $25K = 28% expected ROI. Still attractive but very different from headline 80%. This is portfolio thinking: recruit enough agents to let the law of large numbers work in your favor.

MASTERY CHECK  Finance Mastery Check Can you: (1) Define the 10 most important terms in this chapter without looking? (2) Apply each major framework to a business scenario you haven't seen before? (3) Work through the key formulas with different numbers? (4) Explain how Finance concepts connect to at least 2 other disciplines in this manual? (5) Describe how these concepts apply in financial services practice? If yes on all five, you have mastery.

sections.title: Leading Organizations
paragraphs: Leadership determines whether strategy gets executed. This chapter covers cultural intelligence for diverse environments, team dynamics and group decision-making, organizational design, corporate governance, leadership theories, business ethics, and talent management. These are the people skills that differentiate managers who merely supervise from leaders who inspire.

sections.title: Strategy
paragraphs: Strategy answers: how does a firm achieve and sustain competitive advantage? This chapter covers business-level strategy (how to compete within an industry using Porter's generic strategies), corporate-level strategy (which industries to compete in through diversification and vertical integration), international expansion, strategic frameworks (PESTEL, VRIO, Value Chain), implementation through the Balanced Scorecard, and strategic decision-making under uncertainty.

sections.title: Data & Decisions

sections.title: Marketing & Pricing

Sole Trader Sole trader: The simplest type of business organization, where a single owner is fully in charge of his or her business. A sole trader is attractive because of its simplicity: • The owner doesn’t need to register with Companies House, so information is private. • The set up costs are very low. • The owner only needs to register with Revenues and Customs when making over £1000 in a fiscal year. • The owner pays personal income taxes on profits.

Americans with Disabilities Act: Makes it illegal to discriminate against disabled individuals. Pregnancy Discrimination Act: Makes it illegal to discriminate against pregnant women. Pregnant and disabled individuals must be provided reasonable accommodations, unless they cause undue hardship to the employer.
